- **Reward signaling upfront:** "Digital Trophy" badge visible on the challenge card before user commits — surfaces the reward mechanic immediately to motivate action.
- **Carousel pagination for goals:** Multiple suggested goals in a swipeable card with dot indicators keeps the section compact while offering personalization options.
- **Hierarchy of commitment:** Three escalating asks — Set a Goal (low), Record an Activity (medium), Join a Challenge (social/higher commitment) — ordered by friction.
- **Partial card bleed:** Challenge section shows edge of a second card, communicating horizontal scrollability without any instructional text.
- **Conversational empty state copy:** "Just choose a sport and hit Start. All your stats will be waiting when you finish." — reduces anxiety about getting started, reframes the empty state positively.

---

## Notes
- Empty state = content opportunity, not a void to apologize for
- Social proof number on card header = clever placement before the CTA
- One accent color only — discipline creates strong brand signal
- Reward badge visible pre-commitment → show the prize before the ask
- Personalized name in headline = cheap but effective engagement hook
- Partial bleed card = scroll affordance without UI chrome
- Escalating commitment ladder built into feed order
- Full-width button = unmissable primary action, use sparingly (only one per section)
- Carousel dots on goal card → multiple suggestions without overwhelming

## Screen
Crypto purchase flow — amount entry screen. Sits mid-funnel after asset selection, before payment confirmation. Three-zone layout: large amount display at top, transaction detail rows in middle, custom numeric keypad + CTA at bottom. Full dark theme.

## Layout & Spacing
Vertical thirds: ~30% for amount display, ~25% for transaction context, ~45% for keypad + action. Clear horizontal dividers separate each zone. Amount display is left-aligned rather than centered — unusual choice that feels more like a text input field. The BTC equivalent sits directly below the fiat input with generous breathing room. Keypad keys are evenly distributed with no visible borders, relying purely on spacing to define tap targets.

## Components
- **Amount display**: Oversized fiat number in bright blue with a blinking cursor pipe character; currency label (SGD) rendered in large muted gray — strong active/inactive contrast within the same text line
- **Network pill/badge**: Dark rounded pill with asset icon + label + chevron — compact selector that doesn't compete with primary input
- **Transaction detail rows**: Two rows (Buy asset / Pay with method) each with circular icon, label, sublabel, and right chevron. A subtle vertical connector line between rows implies a flow/sequence relationship
- **Custom numpad**: Pure text keys on dark background, no button outlines. Backspace uses a left-arrow icon. Decimal point included
- **CTA button**: Full-width, tall pill shape with blue-to-cyan horizontal gradient. High contrast against dark background

## Typography & Color
- **Amount number**: Very large (~64–72pt equivalent), bold, bright blue — primary focus
- **Currency label**: Same size, medium gray — visually subordinate but spatially equal
- **Converted value**: Small, muted gray — secondary info
- **Row labels**: Medium weight white for primary, gray for sublabel
- **Keypad digits**: Large, white, light weight — readable without visual noise
- Color palette: Near-black background (#0a0a0a range), electric blue for active input and CTA gradient, orange for BTC asset icon, muted grays for secondary text. Blue used exclusively for interactive/active states.

## Patterns
- **Two-currency simultaneous display**: Fiat input is primary but crypto equivalent updates live below — removes cognitive load of mental conversion without cluttering the input itself
- **Active/inactive contrast within single text string**: Blue number + gray currency code in the same oversized line communicates "you're editing the number, not the currency" without any additional UI
- **Connector line between list rows**: The thin vertical line between "Buy" and "Pay with" rows implies these are linked steps in a sequence, not independent options — subtle flow metaphor
- **Keypad without button chrome**: No borders or backgrounds on numpad keys forces focus onto the amount display rather than the keyboard itself — the input feels more like a financial terminal than a form
- **Network selector as pill above rows**: Placing the network context above the asset rows establishes hierarchy — network → asset → payment method reads top to bottom as a dependency chain
- **Gradient CTA on dark background**: Blue-to-cyan gradient pops significantly against flat dark UI; reserved only for the primary action, making it the single most colorful element on screen

## Notes
- Two-tone single-line amount input (active color + passive color) = steal this for any currency/unit input
- Connector line between related rows = underused pattern for showing step dependency
- Keypad with zero chrome = works when surrounding context is strong enough to anchor the interaction
- Live conversion below input = reduces anxiety in high-stakes transactions
- Pill network selector = good pattern for "context that can be changed but usually isn't"
- Gradient button reserved as sole accent = makes CTA unmissable without being aggressive

## Screen
Health/fitness tracking dashboard — the home screen of a calorie and activity tracking app. This is the primary daily summary view users land on after login. Layout is a vertical scroll of modular data cards, with a persistent bottom bar that combines search and navigation.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized as a single-column card feed beneath a standard header. The hero card (calories) takes up significant vertical real estate with a large donut chart. Below it, a 2-column card grid handles secondary metrics (steps + exercise side by side). A third row of cards begins and bleeds off-screen, signaling scrollability. Pagination dots between the hero card and the grid suggest the hero area is horizontally swipeable. Consistent card padding and generous white space inside each card keep data readable without feeling cramped.

---

## Components
- **Hero donut chart card** — Large circular progress ring with multi-color arc segments (orange for food consumed, blue for remaining). Center displays a bold numeric value + label. Right side has a vertical icon-label-value list for breakdown stats.
- **Pagination dots** — 4 dots below the hero card; first dot filled blue, others gray. Indicates swipeable card carousel.
- **2-column metric cards** — Equal-height cards with title, icon, primary value, and a sub-label or progress bar.
- **Linear progress bar** — Thin, pill-shaped track with a small red fill dot; used in Steps card to show goal progress.
- **"+" action buttons** — Small plus icons in card headers for quick data entry inline, without navigating away.
- **Persistent search bar** — Pill-shaped, white on blue background, with a barcode scanner icon on the right. Floats above the nav bar.
- **Bottom nav bar** — Blue background, white icons and labels, active state shown by bold label weight.

---

## Typography & Color
**Type hierarchy:** Section title ("Today") is large and heavy. Card titles are medium-weight. Metric values (806, 449, 215) are bold and large for scannability. Supporting labels and subtitles are small, light gray. Formula text ("Remaining = Goal - Food + Exercise") is the smallest, used as explanatory subtext.

**Color palette:**
- Primary blue (#2B5CE6 approx) — nav bar, active states, donut ring fill
- Orange/amber — food consumed arc segment, fire icon
- Red/coral — steps icon, progress dot
- Neutral gray — card backgrounds, inactive dots, secondary text
- White — card surfaces, search bar

Color is used functionally: blue = progress/goal tracking, orange = calories consumed, red = activity/urgency cues.

---

## Patterns
- **Persistent contextual search elevated above nav** — The food search bar lives in the nav zone but visually floats above it, making the most frequent action (logging food) immediately accessible from the home screen without a separate tap to navigate.
- **Inline + entry points on cards** — Rather than routing users to a separate logging screen, the "+" on Exercise and Weight cards suggests direct in-context data entry, reducing friction.
- **Formula as subtitle** — Showing the calculation logic ("Remaining = Goal - Food + Exercise") directly under the card title teaches users the model while they use it — onboarding baked into the dashboard.
- **Donut + breakdown hybrid** — The ring chart alone would be ambiguous; pairing it with a vertical breakdown list (Goal / Food / Exercise) on the right side gives both gestalt overview and precise values simultaneously.
- **Card carousel with dots for secondary metrics** — Using swipeable cards for the hero section allows more data types without cluttering the screen, while dots make the hidden content discoverable.
- **Partial card bleed** — The weight card is intentionally cut off at the bottom, a classic scroll affordance cue that communicates "there's more below" without any explicit instruction.

---

## Notes
- Search-in-nav is clever — primary action lives at thumb reach, always visible
- Donut + right-side legend = space-efficient data density pattern worth stealing
- Formula subtitle = progressive disclosure of system logic, doubles as education
- "+" on card headers = micro-entry points, keeps users in flow
- Bleed + pagination dots = two scroll affordances used simultaneously, belt-and-suspenders discoverability
- 2-col card grid breaks monotony of single-column feed
- Color coding by data type (blue=goal, orange=food, red=activity) creates a learnable visual language

## Screen
Ride-hailing app, vehicle tier selection screen. Sits after destination entry, showing available ride options before booking confirmation. Split layout: top half is a live map with route visualization, bottom half is a scrollable bottom sheet listing service tiers with pricing.

## Layout & Spacing
Classic map + bottom sheet split. The sheet takes roughly 55% of the screen. A promo banner acts as a visual "hinge" between map and list — a clever divider that also communicates value. List rows have generous vertical padding (~16px), creating comfortable touch targets. The CTA bar at the bottom is fixed, floating above the list. Horizontal rule separators between rows are subtle/light, keeping the list airy.

## Components
- **Search bar**: Pill-shaped, white, with back arrow and edit icon flanking the destination text. Compact and unobtrusive over the map.
- **Map route**: Blue polyline with animated car cluster near origin, green ETA badge (circle with "2 min"), and arrival time tooltip at destination pin.
- **Promo banner**: Full-width indigo/purple strip with checkmark icon and dropdown affordance — collapsible, communicates discount is active.
- **Ride tier rows**: Each row has a 3D isometric vehicle illustration, tier name + capacity + ETA metadata, and a dual-price stack (discounted price bold, original struck through in gray).
- **Selected state**: Green border outline around the Economy row — border-only selection, no fill, keeps illustrations visible.
- **Payment row**: Pill with avatar icon + bag icon + payment method label + chevron — compact multi-info selector.
- **CTA button**: Large green rounded pill, full-width minus the secondary schedule button. Schedule action is a separate circular green button with calendar icon.

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Tier name in medium-weight ~16px; price in bold ~16px; metadata (ETA, capacity) in small ~12px gray. Promo banner text in white, small caps feel.
- **Color palette**: White background, indigo/purple accent for promo strip, green for selection state + CTA + map ETA badge, gray for secondary text and strikethrough prices, blue for map route.
- **Functional color use**: Green = confirmed/selected/action. Purple = savings/promotional. Gray = original/deprecated price. Blue = navigation/route.

## Patterns
- **Dual-price strikethrough within the row**: Discount is shown inline next to each option rather than as a separate summary — forces immediate price comparison at point of decision.
- **Promo banner as map/sheet divider**: The discount strip does double duty — structural separator AND persistent value reminder. Expandable via chevron suggests more detail without cluttering.
- **Border-only selection indicator**: Using a colored outline rather than a background fill preserves the visual weight of vehicle illustrations while clearly marking selection.
- **Contextual CTA label**: Button reads "Select Economy" — dynamically reflects the currently selected tier, reducing cognitive load and confirming selection before committing.
- **Split CTA row**: Primary action (book now) + secondary action (schedule) separated into pill + circle, same color, same row — visually unified but functionally distinct without modal interruption.
- **3D isometric vehicle illustrations**: Each tier uses a distinct, stylized 3D car render rather than icons or photos — communicates vehicle class quickly, adds premium feel without photography overhead.
- **Arrival time tooltip on map**: "Arrive by 8:41" callout on destination pin bridges the map and the list — connects route visualization to the booking decision.

## Notes
- Promo-as-divider trick is reusable anywhere you have a map+sheet split
- Strikethrough original price inline per row > summary discount at checkout
- Dynamic CTA label pattern works for any selection-then-confirm flow
- Border selection state preserves illustration visibility — better than highlight fill for image-heavy lists
- Schedule as secondary circular CTA avoids a separate flow entry point
- 3D car renders > flat icons for tier differentiation — worth the asset cost
- Green ETA bubble on map creates visual anchor linking map to list data

## Screen
Language learning app — topic/lesson browser screen. This is the home or "learn" tab where users select vocabulary topics to study. Sits at the top of the learning flow, post-onboarding. Layout is a scrollable feed with a hero "resume" section at top and categorized topic cards below.

## Layout & Spacing
Two distinct zones: (1) a full-bleed deep purple hero section occupying roughly 40% of the screen with current lesson progress, and (2) a scrollable content area with horizontally scrolling card rows grouped by category. Section headers use generous top padding to create clear breathing room between categories. The hero section bleeds to edges while card rows have consistent left-aligned padding with visible card clipping on the right to signal horizontal scrollability.

## Components
- **Hero banner**: Deep purple with a ghost/watermark illustrated character at right — low-opacity same-hue treatment keeps it decorative without competing
- **Progress bar**: Segmented pill bar (filled + unfilled segments) with an eye icon and fraction count (8/14) — custom, not a standard slider
- **CTA button**: Yellow-green pill button with uppercase label + play icon arrow; high contrast against purple bg
- **Countdown timer**: Top-right pairing of digital time + stopwatch icon — compact inline treatment
- **Flag + level badge**: Circular flag icon paired with a small circular level badge (gold/green) — stacked/overlapping positioning
- **Topic cards**: Rounded rectangle cards with illustrated food/character imagery, color-coded backgrounds (teal, purple, green, orange, pink), info "i" badge top-right corner
- **Section headers**: White bold title + optional subtitle in muted white/lavender below

## Typography & Color
Two-level type hierarchy in hero: small caps label ("FOUNDATION") as category tag, large bold display for lesson name. Section headers use large bold white. Card labels are small white below cards. Color palette: deep purple dominant (#4B0082 range), yellow-green accent for primary CTA, card backgrounds use distinct saturated hues (teal, medium purple, green, orange, pink) to differentiate topic categories. Color is used functionally — each topic category gets a unique hue, making visual scanning fast.

## Patterns
- **Ghost character in hero**: Illustrative character rendered at ~20% opacity in same purple hue as background — adds personality and depth without visual noise. Smart use of monochromatic illustration
- **Segmented progress bar with eye icon**: Non-standard progress treatment — segmented rather than continuous, with a visibility/review icon suggesting some items are for review vs. new. Adds semantic meaning to progress
- **Horizontal scroll with deliberate card clipping**: Right card visibly cut off signals more content without any explicit "see more" affordance
- **Info badge on cards**: Small circular "i" overlay top-right on every card — consistent pattern for accessing metadata without cluttering the card face
- **Timer as ambient pressure mechanic**: Countdown prominently placed in header creates urgency without interrupting browsing — passive motivator
- **Category + title label hierarchy in hero**: "FOUNDATION / Starter Pack" uses a subordinate category label above the main title — good wayfinding for users in a large content library

## Notes
- Ghost character trick: same hue, low opacity = personality without distraction — steal this
- Segmented progress bar > continuous bar for lesson-based content (shows discrete units)
- Card color-coding as category wayfinding — no text labels needed at a glance
- CTA in hero = "resume" pattern, not "start" — implies continuity, reduces friction
- Timer in nav area creates soft urgency without modal interruption
- "i" info badge pattern: non-destructive way to surface extra info on dense cards
- Two-zone scroll: sticky hero concept + free-scroll content below worth exploring

## Screen
Dating app profile browsing/discovery screen. This is the main feed/explore view where users scroll through potential matches. Layout is a vertical scroll feed with a sticky filter bar at top, an educational nudge banner, a profile card with name/status, a large portrait photo, and a partially visible video card below. Bottom navigation is persistent.

---

## Layout & Spacing

Content flows top-to-bottom in clear horizontal bands: filter bar → onboarding nudge → profile metadata → photo → secondary media card. The profile photo takes up the dominant vertical real estate (~55% of visible content area), creating a media-first hierarchy. The nudge banner sits in a soft lavender card with generous internal padding and rounded corners, clearly separated from the feed content. Profile name and status sit outside/above the photo rather than overlaid on it — a deliberate choice to keep text legible without a gradient scrim.

---

## Components

- **Filter chips (top bar):** Pill-shaped with border outlines, chevron dropdowns, left-aligned filter icon. Horizontally scrollable implied. Subtle and lightweight.
- **Onboarding nudge banner:** Rounded card, lavender/muted purple background, circular icon container with heart motif, two-line copy (headline + subtext). Feels informational, not intrusive.
- **Profile header row:** Large bold name, green active status dot + "Active now" label inline, verified badge with checkmark icon, overflow menu (···), and a secondary icon (undo/rewind).
- **Photo card:** Full-bleed rectangular image with rounded corners. Heart/like button floated bottom-right corner of image — white circle with heart icon, subtle shadow.
- **Video preview card:** Dark overlay card partially visible, timestamp badge (0:11) top-right, large dismiss X button bottom-left in dark circle.
- **Bottom nav:** 5 icons — logo mark, star (favorites), heart (likes), chat, profile avatar thumbnail.

---

## Typography & Color

- **Name:** Large, heavy serif or bold sans — high visual weight, anchors the profile identity
- **Status/badges:** Small, regular weight, muted — clearly secondary
- **Nudge copy:** Medium body, two-tier hierarchy (bold headline / lighter subtext)
- **Color palette:** Near-monochrome UI — white backgrounds, dark text, muted grays. Accent color is a soft purple/lavender used in the nudge banner and verified badge. Green dot for active status. The restraint in color makes the accent moments meaningful.
- **Functional color use:** Green = live/online presence; purple = trust/verification + brand accent; dark overlay = media/video context

---

## Patterns

- **Metadata above image, not overlaid:** Name, status, and verification sit in their own row above the photo rather than as a scrim overlay. Prioritizes readability and gives the photo full visual integrity.
- **Like button embedded in photo corner:** The heart CTA lives inside the image frame at bottom-right — contextually tied to the content it acts on, not separated into a toolbar. Reduces cognitive distance between seeing and acting.
- **Onboarding nudge as feed card:** The "start sending likes" prompt is integrated into the scroll flow as a card, not a modal or tooltip. Non-blocking, skimmable, dismissible by scrolling past.
- **Verified badge inline with name row:** Trust signal is placed at the same visual level as the name — not buried in a profile detail page.
- **Video card peek:** The partially visible video card below creates scroll affordance and content depth — implies richness beyond the first photo.
- **Active status as social proof:** "Active now" with green dot functions as urgency/relevance signal without being pushy — it's metadata, not a CTA.
- **Dismiss X on video card:** Large, high-contrast close button on the video preview suggests the video is playing or expanded — the X is sized for thumb reach and positioned away from content center.

---

## Notes

- Name-above-photo = underrated pattern for legibility, worth stealing
- Like btn in image corner = action proximity principle in action
- Nudge banner as inline card > modal interruption every time
- Lavender as trust/brand accent — soft, non-aggressive
- Active status dot = tiny but high-value engagement signal
- Peek pattern on lower card = scroll invitation without explicit CTA
- Filter chips: outline style keeps bar lightweight, doesn't compete with content
- Avatar thumbnail in nav = profile access without label text

## Screen
Food delivery app home screen — the primary discovery/browse surface. This is the entry point after onboarding, sitting at the top of the main user flow. Layout flows top-to-bottom: location header → search bar → promotional banner → food category scroll → filter chips → restaurant grid. Dense information architecture packed into a single scrollable feed.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in clearly delineated horizontal bands. The promotional banner spans full width with generous height, creating visual breathing room before the denser content below. Category icons use equal spacing in a horizontal scroll. Restaurant cards use a 3-column grid with tight gutters, allowing maximum density. Filter chips sit in their own dedicated row, creating a clear separation between navigation and content. Section label ("RECOMMENDED FOR YOU") uses wide letter-spacing to signal a content zone shift.

## Components
- **Location selector**: Bold location name + chevron + truncated address subtitle — two-line treatment establishes hierarchy
- **Search bar**: Rounded pill with search icon, placeholder text showing a contextual suggestion ("bread"), mic icon for voice — full-width minus the VEG MODE toggle
- **VEG MODE toggle**: Isolated in its own labeled box adjacent to search — toggle is off/grey state, green when active presumably
- **Promotional banner**: Full-bleed card with illustrated character, bold typographic hierarchy, ribbon-style "FREE DELIVERY" badge with contrasting red/white
- **Category scroll**: Food photography thumbnails in circles + text labels, first item is a promotional tile (50% OFF) styled differently to stand out
- **Filter chips**: Pill-shaped, outlined style; "New" badge on Schedule chip adds recency signal
- **Restaurant cards**: Image-dominant cards with discount badge overlaid top-left, rating pill bottom-left overlaid on image, restaurant name + delivery time below
- **Bottom nav**: Three tabs — Delivery (active, pink icon), Dining, and a third-party quick-commerce tab with distinct yellow background pill treatment

## Typography & Color
Strong type hierarchy: location name is bold/large, address is small/muted grey. Section headers use spaced caps. Restaurant names are medium-weight, metadata (time) is small and grey. Primary palette is warm pink/coral as brand color throughout (background tint, active states, icons). Discount badges use high-contrast red or dark overlays. Rating pills use amber/yellow star. The yellow bottom tab for the quick-commerce service is a deliberate color break — signals a different product/destination. White cards against the light pink background create soft separation without hard borders.

## Patterns
- **Contextual search placeholder**: Showing "bread" as placeholder rather than generic "search for food" — implies personalization or trending, adds specificity
- **Dietary mode toggle adjacent to search**: Placing VEG MODE directly next to search rather than in settings acknowledges it as a frequent, session-level preference — reduces friction for a large user segment
- **Promotional category tile**: First item in category scroll is a discount offer tile, not a food category — hijacks the navigation pattern to serve a promotional goal without a separate banner slot
- **Discount badge on image vs. below**: Overlaying "50% OFF up to ₹100" directly on the restaurant photo keeps the card compact while surfacing the most conversion-driving info
- **Third-party tab with distinct visual treatment**: The quick-commerce tab uses a branded yellow pill background, visually differentiating it from native tabs — communicates "this takes you somewhere different"
- **Rating overlaid on image**: Rating pill sits on the photo rather than below, preserving vertical card density
- **"New" badge on filter chip**: Signals product updates inline within the filter row rather than via notification — low-interruption feature discovery

## Notes
- VEG MODE toggle placement = genius friction reduction for dietary preferences, steal this for any app with persistent filter modes
- Contextual search placeholder as soft personalization signal — even if static, feels smart
- 3-col restaurant grid is unusually dense for food apps — works because images carry the weight
- Promotional first-slot in category nav = monetizable real estate without adding a new component
- Bottom tab with brand color override = clear pattern for embedding partner/third-party destinations
- Ribbon badge style on hero banner adds physicality/tactility — more engaging than flat text
- Spaced-caps section headers as zone dividers — clean, no extra lines needed
- Rating on image overlay keeps card height tight — consider for any card-heavy listing screen

## Screen
Fashion retail app home/splash screen. This is the entry point or homepage of a high-end clothing e-commerce app. Layout is a single-column editorial composition: oversized wordmark at top, large editorial photo dominating the center, right-aligned collection caption below, and a minimal bottom navigation bar.

## Layout & Spacing
The wordmark bleeds into the photo — the logotype begins above the image and the photo crops behind/under it, creating a layered z-depth effect. The editorial image is left-offset (doesn't span full width on the right), leaving white space on the left edge. Collection text is right-aligned, stacked in three lines, sitting below the image with generous breathing room before the nav bar. No padding uniformity — asymmetry is intentional.

## Components
- **Wordmark/Logo**: Massive display serif, nearly full-width, sits partially above and overlapping the hero image. Functions as both branding and visual design element.
- **Hero Editorial Image**: Portrait-oriented, roughly 60% of screen height. Not full-bleed — has a defined left margin, giving it a "placed" editorial feel rather than a background fill.
- **Collection Caption**: Three-line right-aligned text block. Spaced label-style, all caps, small tracking. Acts as a magazine-style caption.
- **Bottom Nav Bar**: Five items — Home (icon), Search (icon), MENU (text label, center), Bag (icon), Profile (icon). Minimal, hairline weight icons. "MENU" as a text item rather than a hamburger icon is distinctive.

## Typography & Color
- **Wordmark**: Heavy editorial serif, high contrast thick/thin strokes — luxury fashion vernacular
- **Caption text**: All-caps, tight tracking, small size — reads like editorial print subheading
- **Color palette**: Near-monochrome — white background, pure black type, muted olive/taupe in the photography. No accent colors. The palette defers entirely to the imagery.
- **Functional color use**: None — no color used for interaction states, CTAs, or hierarchy signals. Restraint is the strategy.

## Patterns
- **Typographic layering over imagery**: The wordmark overlaps the photo without a scrim or overlay — the white background above bleeds into the letterforms which then sit on top of the image. Creates depth without drop shadows.
- **Asymmetric image placement**: The hero image has a visible left margin, making it feel "placed" like a magazine spread rather than a full-bleed app background. Signals editorial intent.
- **Text as navigation item**: "MENU" written out as a word in the nav bar instead of a hamburger icon — elevates the interaction to feel typographic and intentional, consistent with the brand voice.
- **No CTA on the home screen**: There's no "Shop Now" button or promotional overlay. The screen trusts the image and collection label to drive exploration. Radical restraint.
- **Right-aligned caption block**: Unusual alignment choice for mobile (typically left-aligned). Creates visual tension and a magazine-layout feel.
- **Zero color hierarchy**: Normally color signals what's interactive. Here, everything is black on white — forces the user to explore rather than be directed.

## Notes
- Wordmark-as-design-element, not just identity — let it collide with the image
- Try "placed" images with margin instead of full-bleed for editorial feel
- All-caps spaced caption text below hero = instant editorial upgrade
- MENU as text in nav bar — consider when brand voice can replace icon convention
- No hero CTA = bold trust in imagery; works when brand equity is high
- Right-align text blocks occasionally to break grid monotony
- Monochrome UI + color photography = the photo does all the emotional work
- Asymmetry + white space > symmetry + fullness for luxury feel

## Screen
Micro-mobility rental app — map view with an active vehicle type filter panel. This sits at the top of the main discovery flow, likely triggered by a filter/type selector. The layout layers a floating card over a live map, with persistent action buttons anchored at the bottom.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct z-layers: map base, floating filter card (top), and bottom action bar. The filter card uses generous internal padding with two centered icon options side by side. Map pins scatter naturally across the map layer. Bottom CTA buttons float above the map without a background bar — they sit directly on the map surface, creating a minimal footprint. The filter card has rounded corners and a soft shadow, clearly elevated above the map.

---

## Components
- **Filter card**: White rounded card, ~half-screen width, centered. Contains title, two circular icon selectors, and close/profile icons in corners
- **Vehicle type selectors**: Circular icon containers — inactive state is light gray fill with muted icon; active state is white fill with bold green stroke ring and full-color icon
- **Map pins**: Small circular icons with green dashed/solid ring borders showing scooter glyphs — dashed ring may indicate low battery or clustering
- **Bottom CTAs**: Two pill-shaped buttons — "My group" in solid light green with group icon; "Scan" in white with border and scan-frame icon
- **Location button**: Small white circular FAB with navigation arrow icon, bottom-right of map

---

## Typography & Color
- Title "Vehicle types" in medium-weight, dark sans-serif — clear hierarchy anchor
- Vehicle labels below icons in regular weight, small size
- **Color palette**: White, light gray, and green (#00C853 range) as the sole accent — used exclusively for active states, CTAs, and map markers
- Green functions as the single semantic signal: selected, available, actionable

---

## Patterns
- **Dashed ring on map pins**: Subtle differentiation — likely signals a different state (low battery, reserved, clustered) without adding color complexity. Worth studying as a state-encoding technique using stroke style rather than color
- **Icon-first selection**: Vehicle type filter uses large circular image selectors rather than text tabs or chips — reduces cognitive load, faster scanning
- **Desaturated inactive state**: Inactive "Bikes" option uses a washed-out, ghosted icon — communicates unavailability or de-selection without hiding the option entirely
- **Floating dual-CTA without a bar**: "My group" and "Scan" float directly on the map with no background tray — preserves map visibility while keeping actions accessible
- **Asymmetric CTA weighting**: Primary action (My group) gets solid green fill; secondary (Scan) gets outlined white — clear hierarchy without size difference
- **Filter card as overlay, not bottom sheet**: The panel appears top-anchored rather than sliding from the bottom, keeping the map context visible below the selection

---

## Notes
- Dashed vs solid ring on map pins = clever stateful encoding, steal this
- Ghost/muted inactive icon in selector = better than graying out text
- No bottom sheet for filter — top card keeps map visible, feels lighter
- Floating pills on map > action bar tray for minimal UI
- Single accent color doing ALL the work: active ring, CTA fill, map markers
- Icon selectors at ~80px feel very tappable and premium vs chip filters
- Try dashed borders for "pending" or "low resource" states on map markers

## Screen
A personal finance / crypto wallet dashboard — the home screen of a multi-account financial app. Sits at the top of the navigation hierarchy. Layout flows top-to-bottom: personalized header → aggregate balance → sparkline chart with time filters → 2×2 account card grid → dismissible promotional banner → bottom nav with FAB.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content is left-aligned in the header zone, creating a grounded, document-like feel rather than centered symmetry. The balance figure gets generous breathing room above the chart. The 2×2 grid uses equal gutters and card sizing, creating a clean quadrant structure. The promo banner sits outside the grid as a distinct, lower-priority layer. Vertical rhythm is loose in the hero zone (balance + chart) and tighter in the card grid.

---

## Components
- **Balance display** — oversized dollar amount with the cents visually de-emphasized via lighter gray weight, same size but lower contrast
- **Performance badge** — small green pill with upward arrow icon + percentage; sits inline with the label, not the number
- **Sparkline chart** — minimal, no axes, no fill, just a hairline stroke; nearly invisible until the eye adjusts; communicates trend without demanding attention
- **Time range selector** — plain text tabs, no pill/underline treatment; selected state is bold weight only, no color change
- **Account cards** — white rounded-rect cards with colored app-style icons (squircle format), label in muted gray, value below in the same dollar/cents contrast split as the hero
- **Upsell card** — flat, dismissible (×), with pagination dots suggesting a carousel; icon, headline, subtext layout
- **FAB** — large black circle with white plus, floats above the nav bar; high contrast, clearly primary action

---

## Typography & Color
Strong two-level hierarchy: large black bold for primary values, small gray for labels. The dollar/cents split (black dollars, gray cents) is a recurring micro-pattern that adds visual interest while preserving readability. Background is pure white inside the frame; light gray app chrome surrounds it. Color is used sparingly and functionally: green only for positive performance, icon colors are app-specific (blue, orange, purple, black), keeping the overall palette neutral. No decorative color.

---

## Patterns

**Dollar/cents contrast split** — Using the same typesize but dropping the cents to ~40% opacity creates a natural focal hierarchy within a number without changing scale. Reused consistently from hero to cards, making it a design language element.

**Icon-as-account-identity** — Each account uses a distinct squircle app icon rather than a generic category icon. This borrows the visual language of a home screen, making accounts feel like distinct "apps" the user owns.

**Invisible chart** — The sparkline is so subtle it reads almost as a texture. This is a deliberate choice to provide data density without visual weight — the chart is there for those who look, not demanding attention from those who don't.

**Upsell as dismissible card with pagination** — Promotional content is treated as a peer-level card, not a banner or modal. The × and dot indicators signal it's temporary and skippable, reducing friction and perceived intrusiveness.

**Bold-only selected state** — Time range filter uses font-weight change alone (no color, no underline, no pill) for the active state. Extremely quiet but legible — keeps the filter row from competing with the chart data.

**Personalized title as primary header** — The user's name in the page title ("Sam's Wallet") replaces a generic screen title, immediately making the experience feel owned rather than generic.

---

## Notes
- Dollar/cents split opacity trick → steal for any financial figure display
- Squircle icons as account avatars → makes accounts feel tangible/ownable
- Ghost sparkline = data without visual noise; try this for secondary metrics
- Bold-only tab selection = worth testing when color would compete with content
- Promo card with × + dots = the least annoying upsell pattern possible
- FAB as the only clear CTA → everything else is browsing, one button for doing
- Personalized header = tiny copy change, huge ownership feeling

## Screen
Mental wellness / health tracking app home screen. Serves as the daily dashboard and entry point — sits at the top of the main navigation flow. Layout is a single-column vertical scroll with a top utility bar, a greeting hero section, an insights card, a plan progress section, and an embedded mood check-in card.

## Layout & Spacing
Generous top padding before the greeting creates breathing room. The greeting + social proof text are left-aligned and given significant vertical space, establishing hierarchy before content begins. The insights card floats in a rounded container with subtle shadow. The plan section introduces a vertical timeline-style left rail (circle + line) suggesting sequential steps. The mood check-in card is a self-contained elevated panel with its own internal padding — card-within-a-screen pattern. Mood options use a 2-column grid with a centered orphan row for the fifth option, which feels intentional and balanced.

## Components
- **Top utility bar**: Three icon buttons (avatar with notification dot, gift/rewards with badge count, search) — pill-shaped or circular, light gray fill
- **Insights card**: Rounded rectangle, white fill, avatar/mascot icon in a soft purple circle, green status dot with category label, chevron right
- **Plan progress indicator**: Unfilled circle + vertical line acting as a timeline node — minimal, structural
- **Mood check-in card**: Elevated white card with a dreamy illustrated header (soft lavender/peach clouds), category label in purple with sun icon, question prompt, and 2-col emoji+text mood buttons
- **Mood buttons**: Pill-shaped, light gray background, emoji face icon left-aligned with colored fill, label text — each emoji has a distinct color coding (green, lavender, muted purple, peach/salmon, pink-red)
- **Dark mode toggle**: Small circular button with half-moon icon overlaid on the card illustration header

## Typography & Color
**Type hierarchy**: Large bold sans-serif greeting (~28–30pt, near-black) → medium gray social proof subtext → bold section header ("Start day 1…") → card label in purple (small, medium weight) → question in bold (~18pt) → mood labels in regular weight. Clean two-weight system throughout.

**Color palette**: Near-white background (#F4F4F6 approx), white cards, deep near-black text, soft purple/lavender as the primary brand accent (used on mascot, category labels, card accents), green for active/positive status, emoji colors as semantic mood indicators (green = great, muted = neutral, warm red = bad). Color carries emotional meaning, not just decoration.

## Patterns
- **Social proof as ambient motivation**: "947,858 people are thriving today" — real-time community count used not as a feature but as passive encouragement on the home screen. Non-intrusive but psychologically warm.
- **Mascot as data proxy**: The insights card uses a character avatar (face icon) rather than a chart or number — makes personal data feel approachable rather than clinical.
- **Mood spectrum with color-coded emoji**: Each mood option has a distinct hue that maps intuitively to emotional valence. The progression from green → lavender → muted → warm salmon → red mirrors a felt emotional gradient without needing explanation.
- **Illustrated card header as emotional priming**: The dreamy cloud illustration at the top of the mood card sets a calm, non-threatening tone before asking a vulnerable question. Visual context shapes the user's emotional state before interaction.
- **Timeline node as progress framing**: The empty circle + vertical line on the left edge reframes a content card as a step in a journey — adds narrative momentum without a progress bar.
- **Dark mode toggle embedded in content**: Placing the light/dark toggle directly on the card illustration (not in settings) makes it feel like a contextual, in-the-moment preference — reduces friction.
- **Orphaned 5th mood option centered**: Rather than forcing a 3rd row or uneven grid, the single "Bad" option is centered — signals it's the outlier, subtly de-emphasizing it without hiding it.

## Notes
- Social proof copy as emotional warmth — not "X users" but "X people thriving"
- Emoji mood pickers > sliders for quick emotional self-report — lower cognitive load
- Illustrated card headers = tone-setting, not decoration
- Timeline left-rail = lightweight way to imply sequence/progress without a progress bar
- Mascot in data card = humanizes analytics
- Color-as-emotion in selectable options is underused pattern worth stealing
- Card-within-screen with its own illustration header creates a "mini app" feel
- Gift icon with numeric badge = rewards/streaks system hinted without being foregrounded

## Screen
A journaling/reflection app's home/today screen. This is the primary entry point — the daily prompt screen users land on to begin a reflection session. Full-bleed ambient background with a conversational prompt and three input method options. Layout is two-zone: upper content area (prompt + greeting) and lower action cluster (input modes).

## Layout & Spacing
Full-bleed gradient background unifies the entire screen. Content is bottom-weighted — the greeting and prompt text sit in the lower-third of the main canvas, leaving generous empty space above that functions as visual breathing room. The three input buttons are centered horizontally near the bottom of the content area, well above the nav bar. A floating "+" button bridges the content area and nav bar, sitting at the edge of both zones. Text is left-aligned against the ambient background, creating an editorial feel.

## Components
- **Ambient background**: Soft blurred gradient — muted slate blue fading into warm blush/tan. Feels atmospheric, not decorative.
- **Greeting text**: Small, lowercase, low-weight — almost a whisper line above the main prompt.
- **Prompt headline**: Large, bold, lowercase serif-adjacent sans — the dominant text element.
- **Sparkle/magic button**: Small circular icon button below the prompt text, suggesting AI prompt generation or randomization.
- **Input mode buttons**: Three frosted/translucent circular buttons (type, yap, speak) with icons + labels. Center button (yap) is larger, elevated as primary action.
- **Floating "+" button**: White circle, bottom-right, elevated with shadow — persistent creation shortcut.
- **Top identity badge**: Eye icon + "your conscious" label — subtle branding/persona element, not a traditional header.
- **Bottom nav**: Dark bar, 4 items, icon + label, minimal.

## Typography & Color
All text is white on the ambient background. Two-level hierarchy: small regular weight greeting line → large bold prompt question. Entirely lowercase throughout — a deliberate tonal choice that softens the interface and feels conversational rather than instructional. Color palette: cool blue-grey in upper half, warm sandy blush in lower half — the gradient itself communicates a transition from mind to emotion, or morning to warmth. Nav bar is near-black, creating a hard base contrast.

## Patterns
- **Lowercase-only UI voice**: Every label, prompt, and nav item is lowercase. Creates a calm, non-authoritative tone — the app speaks like a friend, not a form.
- **Input mode selection as primary UI**: Rather than defaulting to a keyboard, the screen presents three distinct input modalities (type, yap, speak) as equal-ish choices. Center/larger button signals a preferred path without forcing it.
- **Ambient gradient as emotional UI**: The background isn't decorative — the warm-to-cool gradient shift does emotional work, setting a contemplative mood before any interaction happens.
- **Personalized prompt with name injection**: "hope your day is going great, Sam!" functions as a micro-moment of intimacy that primes the user emotionally before the actual CTA.
- **Magic/AI trigger inline with content**: The sparkle button sits directly below the prompt text, suggesting "generate a different prompt" — contextually placed rather than buried in settings.
- **Floating + as escape hatch**: The white FAB at the nav boundary serves users who want to skip the prompt entirely and just create — respects different user intents.
- **Vertical content gravity**: Keeping the prompt in the lower third forces the eye downward toward actions naturally, without needing visual arrows or affordance cues.

## Notes
- Lowercase-only as brand voice — try this for wellness/emotional apps
- 3-input-mode picker > defaulting to keyboard; respects voice-first users
- Gradient bg doing emotional heavy lifting — no illustration needed
- Name in greeting = cheap personalization, high warmth ROI
- Sparkle icon below prompt = "not feeling this? try another" — genius low-friction escape
- FAB bridging content + nav bar = clever spatial trick
- "yap" as label for audio journaling — playful copy in a sea of serious wellness apps
- Empty upper half = intentional negative space, resist the urge to fill it

## Screen
AI-powered search/query interface within a news or information app. This is a modal search overlay — likely triggered from a main feed or home screen. The layout is minimal: close button top-left, large open text field, trending/suggested news headlines below, and a toolbar above the keyboard with mode controls.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content sits in a clean white modal card. The search input occupies the top third with generous vertical padding, creating clear visual breathing room before the suggestion list. Headlines are left-aligned with consistent row height (~56px), separated by implied whitespace rather than dividers. The toolbar is pinned just above the keyboard — tight to the input context, not floating arbitrarily.

---

## Components
- **Close button (X):** Small circular ghost button, top-left. Minimal, unobtrusive.
- **Search input:** Large, open text field with blinking cursor. No border, no background fill — just a bottom rule or pure whitespace separation. Placeholder text is large and conversational.
- **Trending headlines list:** 4 rows of real news headlines in muted gray. Each row has a diagonal arrow icon (↗ style) at the right — indicating "jump to topic" affordance.
- **Toolbar strip:** Globe icon, + icon, "Search" pill/dropdown with chevron, mic icon, waveform/audio icon. Compact, icon-forward.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Placeholder text:** Large (~28–32px), light gray, serif or clean sans — feels editorial and open-ended
- **Headlines:** ~15–16px, medium gray (#999–#aaa), regular weight — deliberately de-emphasized
- **Toolbar labels:** Small, gray, secondary hierarchy
- **Color palette:** Almost entirely white + light gray. No accent color visible. Monochromatic restraint. The cursor is the only "active" color signal.

---

## Patterns

**Conversational placeholder as UX framing:** "Ask anything..." sets an AI/natural language expectation before the user types — reframes search as dialogue, not keyword entry.

**Trending content as pre-input scaffolding:** Rather than leaving the search state empty, real headlines populate the space immediately. These aren't "recent searches" — they're editorially curated, which adds value even if the user never types. Reduces blank-state anxiety.

**Diagonal arrow icon as suggestion affordance:** The ↗ icon (upper-left diagonal arrow) is a subtle but smart choice — it signals "this is a pre-formed query you can launch" without a full button. Lower visual weight than a chevron or ">" but still clearly interactive.

**Mode selector in toolbar:** The "Search ∨" pill suggests the input has multiple modes (search vs. ask/AI vs. something else) — the user can switch context without leaving the input state. Power without clutter.

**Keyboard-anchored toolbar:** The action strip lives between content and keyboard, not in the nav bar. This keeps tools within thumb reach and contextually tied to the input moment.

---

## Notes
- "Ask anything" > "Search" — language shift signals AI capability instantly
- Trending headlines = zero-state content strategy, not just placeholder filler
- ↗ icon for suggestions — lower weight than button, still tappable
- Mode pill in toolbar = multi-modal input switcher, worth stealing
- Full white modal + gray text = lets content breathe, no UI noise competing
- No search button — implies return key or voice; reduces visual clutter
- Mic + waveform icons side by side — static vs. active voice states?

## Screen
Home/discovery screen of a BNPL (buy now, pay later) financial app. Sits at the top of the main navigation flow — the default landing state after login. Layout stacks vertically: dark hero header → promotional banner carousel → merchant grid → account section → tab bar.

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct zones with clear visual separation: (1) dark header section with search + CTA, (2) light content area with carousel and merchant grid, (3) accounts section. The dark-to-light transition creates a strong visual break without a divider line. Merchant grid uses a tight 4-column layout with consistent square cards. Section headers ("Most visited," "Your accounts") use generous top padding to breathe between content blocks.

## Components
- **Search bar**: Full-width pill shape, white on dark bg, left-aligned icon — functions as primary navigation into store discovery
- **Icon buttons (header)**: Bell + avatar in circular containers, same size, grouped right — clean paired utility cluster
- **Hero CTA block**: Bold headline + reassurance microcopy + solid blue pill button — classic anxiety-reducing financial CTA pattern
- **Promotional banner**: Full-bleed image card with overlaid dark pill button, pagination dots centered below — standard carousel treatment
- **Merchant grid tiles**: Rounded square cards, white bg, logo-only content, truncated label beneath — app-icon metaphor applied to commerce
- **Tab bar**: 5-item, icon + label, active state in blue with filled icon variant

## Typography & Color
Two-tone palette: near-black (#1a1a1a-ish) header vs. white content area. Blue (#5469d4-ish) used exclusively for primary actions and active states — high signal color. Header typography: bold sans-serif headline ~22px, secondary copy in muted gray ~14px. Section labels ("Most visited") are heavy/bold ~18px. Merchant names are small, light-weight, centered — clearly secondary. Type hierarchy is tight: 3 levels max.

## Patterns
- **Dark hero as trust anchor**: Financial apps use dark headers to signal seriousness/security before transitioning to lighter browsing content — psychological zoning
- **Anxiety-reduction copy adjacent to CTA**: "Checking won't affect your credit score" placed directly under headline, above button — objection handling baked into layout order
- **App-icon metaphor for merchants**: Treating stores like apps (square rounded tiles, logo-only, grid layout) leverages deeply familiar mental model — reduces cognitive load for store discovery
- **Truncated merchant names with ellipsis**: Deliberate truncation signals "more to explore" without breaking grid rhythm
- **Carousel pagination dots inside image**: Dots overlaid on banner rather than below it — saves vertical space, keeps content density high
- **Section title as scroll anchor**: "Your Affirm accounts" visible but cut off — intentional scroll invitation, content peeking pattern

## Notes
- Dark header → light body = strong zone separation without explicit dividers
- Reassurance copy always lives *between* headline and CTA, never after
- Logo-only merchant tiles = maximum density, zero wasted space
- 4-col grid works because logos are recognizable without text
- Pill CTA on dark bg: blue reads louder than it would on white
- Peek pattern at bottom section = implicit scroll affordance, no "see more" needed
- Avatar as circular icon button = profile access without label

## Screen
Home/dashboard screen of a productivity/notes app. This is the primary landing screen after login — a personal workspace hub showing recent content and document navigation. Layout flows top-to-bottom: account header → recents carousel → workspace section list → utility CTA → FAB → tab bar.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is divided into clearly separated horizontal bands with generous vertical breathing room between sections. The "Jump back in" card uses a wider card format (nearly half-screen width) with a tall image area above the label — portrait card ratio. The list section sits inside a rounded rectangle container, creating a card-within-screen effect. The "Browse templates" banner sits outside that container as a distinct visual tier. Consistent left-edge alignment across all list items creates strong vertical rhythm.

## Components
- **Account header row**: Avatar initial + workspace name with dropdown chevron + email subdomain. Three-dot overflow right-aligned.
- **Recents card**: Large rounded card with gray placeholder thumbnail area, document icon centered, title below. Horizontally scrollable implied.
- **Section header**: Label ("Private") + ellipsis + plus icon — inline section controls.
- **List rows**: Chevron expand > doc-type icon > label > plus icon. Consistent 4-element structure per row, full-width tap targets.
- **Browse templates banner**: Rounded pill/card with icon left, label center-left, decorative stacked document illustration right. Subtle gray fill.
- **FAB**: Circular white button with raised shadow, positioned bottom-right above tab bar. Custom icon (AI/assistant indicator).

## Typography & Color
Near-monochromatic palette — off-white background (#F2F2F2 range), white cards, mid-gray icons and secondary text, dark charcoal for primary labels. Zero accent color used — all hierarchy achieved through weight and gray-scale contrast alone. Type hierarchy: workspace name (medium weight, ~16px) → section labels (small, gray, uppercase-adjacent) → list item labels (medium, ~16px, dark) → email/subdomain (small, gray). Minimal and intentionally restrained.

## Patterns
- **"Jump back in" recency pattern**: Surfaces last-accessed content as a visual card rather than a list item — different format signals different intent (resume vs. navigate).
- **Dual-mode document access**: Same document ("Getting Started on Mobile") appears in both the recents card AND the list below — recency layer and hierarchy layer coexist without feeling redundant because they serve different mental models.
- **Per-row inline creation**: Each list item has its own `+` icon, enabling contextual child creation without drilling into the item first. Reduces navigation depth.
- **Section-level controls mirrored**: The section header also has `...` and `+` — macro and micro creation affordances at both levels simultaneously.
- **Content-type iconography in lists**: Each row uses a different icon (document, checkmark, list, person) to communicate page type at a glance — the sidebar becomes a visual legend.
- **FAB as AI/assistant entry**: The FAB uses a non-standard icon suggesting an intelligent assistant rather than a simple compose action — elevates AI as a primary workflow shortcut.
- **Decorative illustration in utility CTA**: The templates banner uses a stacked-papers illustration as right-side decoration — makes a functional CTA feel warmer without adding color.

## Notes
- Recents as cards, navigation as lists — two formats, two mental models, same content layer
- Inline `+` per row = creation without context switching
- All hierarchy via gray scale only — no color needed when structure is tight
- FAB above tab bar, not overlapping — intentional z-layer separation
- Account switcher in header (chevron) — workspace identity always visible
- Section container card = visual grouping without headers-only approach
- Doc-type icons as list metadata — scannable content taxonomy
- "Jump back in" label is conversational/human — tone choice worth noting

## Screen
Plant care app — individual plant detail/care confirmation screen. Appears after a user logs a watering action. Dark modal overlay sits above the main plant collection. Primary purpose: feedback confirmation + quick-action hub for a single plant. Layout stacks vertically: header identity → hero image card → mascot character → feedback message → action bar.

---

## Layout & Spacing

Content flows in a tight centered column with generous breathing room between the image card and the message pill. The image card takes roughly 45% of screen height — dominant and intentional. The mascot character is physically overlapping the bottom edge of the image card, bridging two sections. The action bar floats at the bottom with visible card edges on either side hinting at horizontal scrollability. Vertical rhythm feels musical — big, pause, small, pause, actions.

---

## Components

- **Image card**: Large rounded-rectangle (heavy corner radius ~28px), white background, glowing green border/shadow suggesting health status. Gallery icon overlay in bottom-left corner.
- **Mascot blob**: Round, smiling green character sitting at the card's bottom edge — 3D-style with soft gradient shading. Casts a subtle drop shadow.
- **Feedback pill**: Dark gray rounded pill/stadium shape, contains a teal heart icon above bold confirmation text + secondary personalized subtext. Feels like a "toast" that's been promoted to a permanent state.
- **Action buttons**: Pill-shaped, three visible in a horizontal scroll row. Center "Water" button is enlarged, blue with a water drop icon — clearly the primary action. Flanking buttons (Snooze, Track) are smaller, muted purple-gray. Partially visible buttons on edges signal scroll affordance.
- **Nav controls**: X button (close) top-left, three-dot menu top-right — both in circular dark gray containers.

---

## Typography & Color

**Type hierarchy:**
- Plant name: Large, bold white — dominant
- Species name: Small, spaced caps, warm olive/khaki — secondary identifier
- Feedback headline: Bold white, mid-size — celebratory
- Feedback subtext: Regular weight, lighter gray — personal/warm

**Color palette:**
- Background: Near-black (#1a1a1a range)
- Accent green: Used on card border glow, mascot, heart icon — signals life/health
- Blue: Reserved exclusively for the primary Water CTA — high contrast, action-oriented
- Muted purple-gray: Secondary action buttons — recedes appropriately
- White/off-white: All primary text

Color is used with clear functional intent: green = plant health status, blue = primary action, purple = secondary actions.

---

## Patterns

- **Mascot as emotional bridge**: The blob character sits at the seam between the photo and the message, physically connecting the plant's identity to its "voice." It anthropomorphizes the plant without replacing the real photo.
- **Promoted toast pattern**: The confirmation message is styled like a toast notification but given permanent, prominent placement — turns ephemeral feedback into a moment worth reading.
- **Personalization in micro-copy**: "You're the best, Sam." — using the user's name in the plant's voice creates a parasocial loop that drives engagement.
- **Glow border as health indicator**: The green luminous border on the image card communicates plant status (healthy) without any explicit label or icon — purely ambient data.
- **Asymmetric action bar scaling**: The primary action button is physically larger than secondary ones — size hierarchy replaces color alone as the differentiator.
- **Horizontal scroll action tray**: Partially visible buttons communicate more options exist without cluttering the screen — progressive disclosure through spatial overflow.
- **Modal with carousel hint**: Gray card edges peeking from both sides of the image suggest swipeable plants — navigation is embedded in the layout itself.

---

## Notes

- Mascot overlapping card = emotional "seam" trick — steal this
- Glow border as silent status indicator — no label needed
- "Promoted toast" — make feedback feel like a moment, not a dismissal
- Name in plant's voice = tiny delight, big retention signal
- Size = hierarchy in action bar, not just color
- Partial card edges = swipe affordance without arrows or dots
- Dark bg makes green + blue pop hard — consider for care/health apps

## Screen
Personal finance dashboard — the main overview screen of a budgeting/expense tracking app. Sits at the top of the navigation hierarchy as the home/landing view. Layout splits vertically into two zones: a teal/dark header section containing the hero metric and line chart, and a white card-list section below showing account category breakdowns.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Two-zone vertical split: ~60% dark header, ~40% white content area below. The transition between zones is a soft curve/wave edge rather than a hard line, creating visual continuity. Hero number sits top-left aligned (not centered), giving it editorial weight. Chart occupies the full width of the header with no horizontal padding, letting it bleed edge-to-edge. Account rows below use generous vertical padding with subtle dividers between cards, creating breathing room without heavy borders.

---

## Components
- **Segmented toggle (Net worth / Spending):** Pill-shaped, the active state is a white filled pill on the teal background — minimal, clean contrast switch
- **Hero metric:** Oversized display number in white, left-aligned
- **Delta indicator:** Small circular green icon with down arrow + inline text — color-coded directional change label
- **Line chart:** Full-bleed, white stroke on teal, with a filled area gradient beneath; active data point shown as a white-outlined green circle node
- **Axis labels:** Floating dark pill labels for Y-axis ($1K, $500); light gray text for X-axis dates
- **Tooltip:** Dark rounded rectangle "Tap to expand" — doubles as affordance hint
- **Expand row:** Text link + pagination dots + three-dot overflow menu — compact control strip between sections
- **Account category rows:** White cards with teal-tinted label text on left, value right-aligned; negative values prefixed with "−$"
- **Notification badge:** Red circle with count on bell icon in tab bar

---

## Typography & Color
**Type hierarchy:** Hero number is very large (~48–56pt), bold, white. Section labels (Cash, Credit cards, Loans) are medium weight, teal-colored. Supporting text (delta comparison, axis labels) is small, muted. Tab bar labels are small/regular weight.

**Color palette:**
- Primary: Muted teal/dark green (header background, label text in cards)
- White: Chart line, hero number, card backgrounds
- Green accent: Delta icon, active chart node
- Red: Notification badge only — reserved purely for alerts
- Dark charcoal: Axis pill labels, tooltip background

Color is used functionally — teal = brand/neutral data, green = positive/active state, red = urgent attention only.

---

## Patterns
- **Contextual tooltip as interaction hint:** "Tap to expand" appears directly on the chart as a speech-bubble tooltip anchored to the active data point — teaches interaction without a separate onboarding step
- **Pagination dots in a utility strip:** Dots between "Expand" text and overflow menu suggest the chart is horizontally swipeable — compact discovery mechanism
- **Blurred/redacted values:** Account balances are blurred out (privacy mode), but the structure and labels remain — privacy-first pattern that still communicates information architecture
- **Wave/curved section divider:** The transition from dark header to white content uses a gentle curve rather than a flat edge, softening the hard zone break and adding depth
- **Left-aligned hero metric:** Breaking from centered hero numbers gives the screen an editorial/dashboard feel rather than a typical app feel
- **Negative value formatting:** "−$[value]" with explicit minus sign on credit cards — clear debt signaling without color-coding alone
- **Delta comparison inline with metric:** Placed immediately below the hero number with an icon, creating a tight semantic unit between current value and its trend

---

## Notes
- Wave divider between bg zones = underused pattern, very effective
- Tooltip as feature discovery > coach marks or tooltips in modals
- Privacy blur keeps layout integrity intact — don't hide, just obscure
- Left-align hero numbers for dashboard feel vs. centered for app feel
- Teal label text in white cards = subtle brand color reinforcement without heavy UI chrome
- Red badge = only red on screen → maximum alert salience
- Pagination dots in control strip = compact swipe hint, worth stealing
- Delta icon + text as single inline unit = tight, scannable

## Screen
Urban mobility / transit app home screen. Combines real-time map view with destination search. Sits at the entry point of a trip-planning flow. Layout is split vertically: top ~55% is full-bleed map, bottom ~45% is a white card with greeting, search bar, and recent/suggested destinations.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Hard split between map and content panel — no overlap or bottom sheet ambiguity. The white panel has generous internal padding (~20–24px sides). The greeting headline sits flush left with comfortable breathing room before the search bar. Destination suggestions are list items with consistent left-icon + two-line text, separated by implied whitespace rather than dividers. Compact but not cramped.

---

## Components
- **Map view** — Full-width, no chrome overlay. A clustered map pin (pill shape) groups bike icons + count badge in indigo/purple. Subtle car icon on map surface.
- **Hamburger/filter button** — Floating white card with subtle shadow, top-left of map. Has a pink notification dot on the menu icon — signals active filters or alerts.
- **Search bar** — Rounded rectangle, white with light border/shadow. Pink search icon left-anchored inside field. Placeholder text in light gray.
- **Destination rows** — Icon (pink location pin) + primary location name (medium weight) + secondary address line (gray, smaller). No dividers — spacing alone separates rows.
- **Bottom nav** — 5 icons: search, bike, transit, key/pass, globe with badge. Active state on search (filled). Globe has a purple notification badge.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Greeting** — Large, bold, dark navy/black. Warm and personal.
- **Location names** — Medium weight, dark. Secondary address in muted gray — clear two-level hierarchy.
- **Placeholder text** — Light gray, low contrast intentionally passive.
- **Color palette**: Deep navy text, hot pink/magenta as primary accent (search icon, location pins, notification dot), indigo/purple for map cluster pill and nav badge, white card backgrounds, standard map palette underneath.
- Pink used exclusively for interactive/actionable elements; purple for status/count indicators.

---

## Patterns
- **Notification dot on filter/menu icon** — Unusual placement signals something needs attention without interrupting the map. Easy to miss, rewards attentive users.
- **Clustered map pin as pill** — Instead of a number-only badge, the cluster shows mode icons (two bike icons) + overflow count. Communicates *what* is clustered, not just *how many*.
- **Personalized greeting as section header** — "Hello there, Jon" replaces a generic screen title. Warms the utilitarian search flow without adding a separate onboarding moment.
- **Recent destinations surfaced pre-tap** — Showing saved/recent locations below the unfocused search bar reduces friction; user may never need to type at all.
- **Map as ambient context, not primary UI** — Map fills the top half but has no interactive affordances shown. It's orientation/context, not the task surface. Task lives in the card below.
- **Split-screen without sheet metaphor** — The white panel is a hard-edged card, not a draggable bottom sheet. Signals this is a stable home state, not a temporary overlay.

---

## Notes
- Pink accent on search icon = draws eye immediately to the action
- Cluster pill with mode icons > generic number badge — steal this
- Greeting + search = one-two punch: personal then functional
- Pre-populated recents below unfocused field = smart zero-state
- Notification dot on hamburger is subtle but powerful — use sparingly
- Hard map/card split feels cleaner than bottom sheet for home screens
- Purple badge on globe nav = secondary alert hierarchy vs. pink primary
- No dividers in suggestion list — spacing alone works at this density

## Screen
A content/publishing platform's home feed screen. This is the primary discovery surface — the first screen users land on after onboarding. It shows a personalized article feed with tab-based filtering. Layout is a vertical scroll list with a sticky header and tab bar.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in card-like rows separated by thin dividers (not boxed cards — open layout with dividers). Each article row has generous internal padding. The thumbnail sits right-aligned, floating beside the text block, creating a two-column micro-layout within each row. Metadata row (date, claps, comments) sits below the text with clear vertical breathing room. The page title "Home" is large and left-aligned with significant top spacing, giving it editorial weight.

---

## Components
- **Page title**: Large, bold, left-aligned — functions as a section header, not a nav bar
- **Tab bar (filter)**: Horizontal scrollable tabs with underline indicator on active state; includes a "+" icon at the far left for adding custom tabs
- **"New" badge**: Small green pill label on the "Featured" tab — inline with tab label, not superscript
- **Article row**: Avatar + author name header, bold title, subtitle/deck text, right-aligned thumbnail, metadata footer
- **Metadata row**: Star/boost icon (gold), date, clap count with hand icon, comment count with bubble icon — all small, muted gray
- **Action buttons**: Minus/block icon (circle outline) and ellipsis (more options) — subtle, low-weight
- **FAB**: Green circular floating action button with edit/compose icon, bottom-right
- **Bottom nav**: Icon-only, minimal; profile icon has a small gold sparkle indicator (notification or new content signal)

---

## Typography & Color
Strong two-level hierarchy: bold serif-style headline at ~18–20pt, regular body/deck text at ~14pt in medium gray. Author/publication attribution is small and uses mixed weight (publication name bold, author name regular). Metadata is smallest, lightest — clearly tertiary. 

Color palette is near-monochrome: white background, black headlines, gray body and metadata. Two accent colors used sparingly and functionally — **gold/yellow** for the boost star icon and FAB sparkle badge, **green** for the "New" pill badge and FAB button. These two accent colors carry all the interactive/status signaling.

---

## Patterns
- **Open card layout with dividers instead of boxed cards** — feels editorial, like a newspaper, rather than app-like. Reduces visual noise significantly.
- **"+" tab for customization** — surfaced directly in the tab row rather than buried in settings. Lowers friction for personalization.
- **Inline "New" pill badge on tab label** — signals new content type without disrupting the tab bar's visual rhythm. More elegant than a notification dot.
- **Publication + author attribution pattern** — "In [Publication] by [Author]" — gives two layers of context in one compact line, useful for platforms with nested content hierarchies.
- **Negative action in metadata row** — the minus/block button lives in the same row as engagement stats, not hidden in a long-press or swipe. Surfaces content control without making it prominent.
- **Sparkle indicator on profile avatar in nav** — subtle animated-feeling badge that signals something new without a hard red dot. Softer, less alarming signal.
- **FAB for compose** — unusual on a feed screen; implies reading and writing are equal first-class actions.

---

## Notes
- Open divider layout > card layout for text-heavy content feeds
- Gold star for "boosted/featured" = warm, non-intrusive quality signal
- "In Publication by Author" — steal this attribution pattern for multi-author platforms
- Inline pill badge on tabs is cleaner than notification dots
- Minus button in-row = content control without hiding it
- Sparkle on avatar = soft "something's new" signal, avoid red dots where possible
- FAB on feed = commitment to creator-first positioning
- Two accent colors max, each with one job

## Screen
Single post detail/thread view in a social microblogging app. Sits one level deep from a feed — user tapped into a specific post to see its replies and engagement. Layout is vertical stack: nav bar → original post → contextual banner → reply thread → composer input → tab bar.

## Layout & Spacing
Original post gets generous vertical breathing room — timestamp and view count sit on their own line below the post body, giving it editorial weight. Clear visual hierarchy separates the "parent" post from replies via a subtle horizontal divider. The contextual share banner is inset with light background fill, creating a contained card within the scroll flow. Reply items are more compact/dense than the parent post, reinforcing the hierarchy. Bottom composer sits flush above the tab bar, anchored persistently.

## Components
- **Nav bar**: Back arrow left, centered bold label, overflow (···) right — minimal, no color
- **Author row**: Circular avatar + display name + handle inline; platform logo mark floated far right as a subtle branding/source tag
- **Post body**: Large-ish plain text, no container/card — floats directly on white
- **Metadata row**: Timestamp · date · view count in muted small text, dot-separated
- **Action bar (parent)**: 5 icons evenly spaced — comment (with count), repost, like, bookmark, share. Outlined/stroke style icons
- **Contextual banner**: Light blue-tinted card, upload icon + descriptive text + dismiss X. Soft rounded corners, no hard border
- **Reply row**: Compact — avatar, name, handle, timestamp, mute icon, overflow all on one line; reply text below; own action bar with analytics icon (bar chart) added
- **Composer input**: Full-width pill/rounded rect with placeholder text, very low visual weight
- **Tab bar**: 5 icons, active state shown with small blue dot on home icon; notification bell has blue badge with count

## Typography & Color
- Display name: medium-weight ~16px, black
- Handle/metadata: regular, ~13px, medium gray
- Post body: regular ~17–18px, black — largest text on screen, draws eye immediately
- Timestamp line: ~13px, gray — clearly tertiary
- Banner text: ~14px, dark gray, regular weight
- **Color palette**: Near-monochrome — black, white, multiple grays. Single accent: blue used exclusively for interactive signals (dot on active tab, notification badge, banner background tint). No decorative color.

## Patterns
- **View count as social proof metric** displayed inline with timestamp — treats views as a lightweight engagement signal without inflating importance
- **Contextual education banner mid-feed**: Rather than a modal or tooltip, the share prompt is embedded inline as a dismissible card between the post and its replies — non-blocking, contextually relevant placement
- **Analytics icon in reply action bar** (bar chart) but absent from parent post action bar — subtle role differentiation: reply authors get analytics access, reinforcing the "your content" ownership model
- **Platform logo as source attribution** floated top-right of parent post — functions as both branding and content origin marker, unusual placement that doesn't interfere with content
- **Persistent composer anchored above tab bar** — keeps reply action always accessible without a FAB, reduces tap distance
- **Mute icon directly in reply header row** — surfaces a moderation action at the content level rather than hiding it behind overflow, prioritizing control for the post author viewing replies
- **Active tab indicator as micro-dot** rather than filled icon or underline — extremely minimal active state

## Notes
- Inline dismissible banners > modals for contextual education
- View count beside timestamp = low-friction metric display, no dedicated section needed
- Role-based action bars (different icons for own post vs. reply) — smart contextual tooling
- Blue used ONLY for system signals (active, unread) — zero decorative use = high signal clarity
- Mute surfaced at reply level = author-first moderation UX
- Persistent composer above tab bar = no FAB needed in thread views
- Platform logo top-right as subtle provenance mark — worth stealing for multi-source feed apps

## Screen
AI conversational assistant home screen — the entry/discovery point where users choose how to engage. Two-column card grid dominates the layout, with a persistent chat input bar anchored at the bottom. This is a "mode selection" or "conversation starter" screen sitting at the top of the app hierarchy.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Generous warm cream background creates breathing room. Two-column grid of equal-width cards with consistent gutters (~12px). Cards are tall rectangles — image occupying roughly 55% top, label text in the bottom 45%. Bottom chat bar floats above content, slightly inset from screen edges with rounded pill shape. Page title sits high-left with profile icon top-right. Cards intentionally bleed off-screen bottom, signaling scrollability.

---

## Components
