- **Welcome header** — Large serif/display weight, left-aligned, dark forest green
- **Profile button** — Circular icon button, muted cream fill, top-right corner
- **Conversation mode cards** — Rounded rectangle (~16px radius), cream background, top-half illustration, bottom-half label text. No borders, subtle shadow implied by card lift
- **Illustrations** — Editorial-style gouache/watercolor paintings, not photography or flat icons — warm, humanistic, analog feeling
- **Chat input bar** — Pill-shaped, full-width minus margins, contains logo mark left, placeholder text center, phone/voice icon button right in teal circle

---

## Typography & Color
**Type hierarchy:**
- Title: Large display serif, ~32px, deep forest green
- Card labels: Medium weight serif, ~18–20px, same dark green, left-aligned, multi-line allowed

**Color palette:**
- Background: Warm off-white/cream (#F5F0E8 range)
- Text/UI: Deep forest green (~#2D4A3E)
- Accent: Muted teal on the voice CTA button
- Illustrations bring in terracotta, sage, warm browns — all desaturated/vintage-toned

Color used functionally: single accent (teal) reserved exclusively for the primary action button, making it the only true CTA on screen.

---

## Patterns
**Illustration as UX wayfinding** — Each card uses a distinct painted scene to emotionally communicate the mode before the user reads the label. The image does the heavy lifting; text confirms. This is rare and effective.

**Intentional editorial art style** — Gouache/watercolor illustrations signal warmth, humanity, and non-digital intimacy — directly countering the "cold AI" perception. Deliberate brand-through-art-direction pattern.

**Mode-first onboarding** — Rather than a blank chat input, the screen presents curated conversation intents. Reduces blank-canvas anxiety. Lowers barrier to first interaction.

**Scrollable grid implies abundance** — Cards cut off at bottom edge, communicating "more options exist" without requiring a "see more" button.

**Voice CTA elevated** — Phone icon in the chat bar elevates voice as equal to text, unusual for AI chat apps. Teal circle draws eye immediately.

**No iconography except profile** — All navigation/mode selection handled through illustrated cards, not icon+label tabs. Removes abstraction layer.

---

## Notes
- Painted illustrations > icons for emotional mode selection — steal this
- Cream bg + forest green = warm authority, not sterile tech
- Single accent color discipline — teal only on primary action
- Card = image (emotion) + label (confirmation) pattern
- Bleed cards off-screen = implicit scroll affordance, no "scroll for more" needed
- Voice parity with text in input bar — worth considering for any assistant UI
- Serif type choice signals thoughtfulness, not speed — intentional pacing cue
- No color-coded categories — unity over differentiation here

## Screen
Social discovery app — map-based people-finding screen. Full-screen map view showing nearby users with a persistent action bar at the bottom. Sits in the core "explore" flow after onboarding. The map is the entire UI canvas, not a component within a screen.

## Layout & Spacing
Full-bleed map occupies 100% of screen real estate. City/location label overlaid top-left in large bold type. Action bar floats at bottom as a pill-shaped element sitting on top of the map. No traditional header or nav chrome — everything lives on the map layer. The bottom action zone uses generous padding and a clear visual separation between the primary CTA and secondary action.

## Components
- **User avatar pins**: Circular cropped photos used as map pins — no traditional teardrop pin shape, just the face itself as the locator
- **Distance orbit rings**: Dotted circular rings radiating from the central user avatar with "22M" labels at multiple points around the circumference — distance shown as a radial annotation, not a badge
- **3D emoji/gift objects**: Large 3D-rendered objects scattered across the lower map (hearts, fire, poop, eyes, crying face, beer, hello badge) with small text labels beneath each — function as interactive map items/gifts
- **Primary CTA pill**: Wide golden-yellow gradient pill button with "Message" text — high contrast, warm color against cool map
- **Secondary action button**: White circular button with phone icon, right-aligned next to the primary pill
- **Distance compass widget**: Small circular widget bottom-left showing "7.2K mi" with compass tick marks — analog instrument aesthetic
- **Zoom indicator**: "4X" typographic overlay on map in heavy condensed type

## Typography & Color
- Location label: Extra-bold/black weight, large (~32pt), all-caps feel — dominates the top of screen
- "4X" zoom: Heavy condensed display type, used as a map overlay annotation
- Gift labels: Small caption text beneath 3D objects, casual/lowercase
- Distance labels: Small, tight, placed along orbit ring
- **Color palette**: Warm golden yellow for primary CTA (high energy), white for secondary button, map uses standard muted beige/tan tones, 3D objects introduce saturated color pops (red, orange, purple, brown). Overall feel is warm-toned.

## Patterns
- **Face-as-pin**: Using the actual user photo as the map marker removes abstraction — you're not tracking a dot, you're seeing a person. Dramatically more humanizing than icon pins.
- **Radial distance display**: Distance shown as orbit rings around the user rather than a static badge — communicates "you are the center, others orbit you" conceptually. The repeated "22M" around the ring reinforces precision from any reading angle.
- **3D objects as map content**: Gifts/reactions rendered as 3D objects placed geographically on the map — blurs the line between AR and 2D map, makes the map feel alive and playful rather than utilitarian
- **Floating split action bar**: Primary + secondary actions paired in a floating bar rather than full-width — keeps map visible, feels less intrusive
- **Zero chrome philosophy**: No cards, no panels, no drawers visible — all UI is either overlaid on the map or floated above it. Maximizes immersion.
- **Warm CTA against cool map**: The golden pill button creates instant visual hierarchy against the desaturated map background — no need for drop shadows or borders

## Notes
- Face pins > icon pins for social apps — try this pattern
- Orbit rings for distance = spatial metaphor that feels native to maps
- 3D objects on 2D maps = huge engagement hook, makes map feel like a game world
- "Zero chrome" map UI — overlay everything, panel nothing
- Warm yellow CTA on cool map = strong contrast without darkness
- Compass widget as distance display — analog instrument aesthetic adds personality
- City name as giant display type overlay = instant context, no header needed
- Split pill + circle button pairing is clean — avoids two equal-weight buttons

## Screen
Home/dashboard screen of a meditation and wellness app. Entry point after login — serves as the daily hub showing streak progress, promotional content, and content discovery. Layout flows vertically: full-bleed hero image at top → streak tracker overlay → weekly habit grid → promotional banner → content section → bottom nav.

## Layout & Spacing
The hero landscape photo takes roughly 40% of the screen height, creating an immersive mood-setting zone. Content below transitions from the image into a deep navy panel, creating a seamless visual continuation rather than a hard break. The streak ring sits precisely at the image/panel boundary, bridging both zones. Weekly day indicators are evenly spaced in a single row with generous tap targets. The promo banner has comfortable internal padding with a square icon container on the left, text block in the middle, and chevron right-aligned. Consistent horizontal margins (~16px) throughout the scrollable content area.

## Components
- **Full-bleed hero image** — edge-to-edge landscape photo with no overlay or scrim; sky bleeds into the navy background naturally
- **Circular streak ring** — donut-style progress ring in cyan/blue gradient with large numeral center and label beneath; sits on a dark circular base with subtle shadow
- **Weekly habit dots** — 7 small circles labeled M–F+weekend; completed days show white checkmarks on slightly brighter circles; today's circle has a visible ring/outline treatment; past incomplete days are muted/ghosted
- **Confirmation microcopy** — centered, two-line motivational text below the habit row
- **Promotional banner card** — rounded rectangle with dark navy fill, purple square icon container (gift icon), bold title, secondary descriptor text, right chevron
- **Bottom nav bar** — pill-shaped frosted/translucent container with 4 icons; active state (Home) has filled white icon + label; inactive states are dimmer

## Typography & Color
**Type hierarchy:** Logo in elegant script at top. Streak number is the largest text element (~36px bold). Section header "Popular on Calm" is medium-weight sans-serif. Banner title is bold white. Supporting text is small, regular weight, slightly muted white/light blue. Day labels are tiny caps.

**Color palette:**
- Deep navy blue (#1a2a4a range) — primary background
- Cyan/sky blue gradient — streak ring accent, active states
- Muted slate blue — inactive day dots, ghost states
- Purple/lavender — promotional icon background, adds warmth contrast
- White — primary text, active icons
- Full-color photography — hero zone only

Color is used functionally: cyan signals progress/achievement, purple signals commercial/promotional, muted tones signal inactive/past states.

## Patterns
- **Threshold placement of key widget** — the streak ring straddles the hero image and the content panel, making it feel anchored in the scene rather than just UI chrome; creates depth and visual interest
- **Natural color continuation** — the deep blue of the mountain lake photo transitions almost imperceptibly into the navy app background; no hard edge, no overlay needed
- **Graduated day-dot states** — three distinct visual states (past incomplete = dark muted, completed = checkmark bright, today = outlined ring) communicate temporal position without any labels
- **Goal-reached positive reinforcement copy** — the microcopy appears only after the goal threshold is met, making it feel earned rather than generic
- **Frosted pill nav bar** — the bottom nav uses a floating pill with translucency rather than a full-width bar, reducing visual weight at the bottom and maintaining the immersive feel
- **Promotional banner as content card** — the offer is styled identically to content cards (same rounding, same dark fill) so it feels native rather than like an ad interruption
- **Scenery customization affordance** — the landscape icon in the top-left implies the hero image is changeable, turning a static background into a personalization feature

## Notes
- Hero-to-UI color match = zero-cost immersion trick, worth stealing
- Streak ring at image boundary = "bridge element" pattern — use to connect hero zones to content
- Three-state habit dots: ghost / check / outlined-today — clean temporal encoding
- Pill nav floats = less visual weight, more premium feel
- Promo card styled as content = reduces ad blindness
- Positive microcopy gated behind goal completion = micro-delight moment
- Purple accent for commercial CTAs = separates monetization from core UX without being jarring

## Screen
Travel discovery app home screen — the entry point of the app experience. Serves as an exploratory feed combining search, location-based suggestions, and curated content sections. Layout flows vertically: header → search → location prompt → editorial card carousel → trending section (partially visible). Primary interactive elements: global search bar, location permission CTA, horizontally scrollable destination cards, heart/save buttons, bottom navigation.

## Layout & Spacing
Clean vertical rhythm with generous section padding between distinct content zones. The search bar gets significant breathing room above and below — treated as a primary action, not just a utility. The location prompt sits in its own contained row with clear left-to-right reading: icon → copy → chevron. Section headers ("Plan your next adventure") are left-aligned, bold, and sit flush before their carousels. Card carousel bleeds off the right edge, signaling horizontal scroll without any explicit indicator. Two distinct card sizes visible — larger editorial cards in the first carousel vs. smaller square cards in trending.

## Components
- **Search bar**: Full-width pill shape, light stroke border, left-aligned search icon, muted placeholder text. No fill color — white/transparent background.
- **Location prompt row**: Circular green icon container with a location-plus glyph, two-line text block (bold headline + muted subtext), trailing arrow chevron. Inline, non-modal permission request.
- **Destination cards (large)**: Full-bleed photography, rounded corners, city/country name in white bold type anchored bottom-left. Category tags (pill chips) top-left. Heart icon top-right in a white circle button. Gradient overlay implied at bottom for text legibility.
- **Category tag chips**: Small white rounded rectangles with dark text, sitting directly on the image.
- **Heart/save button**: White circular button, outline heart icon — consistent across both card sizes.
- **Notification bell**: Top-right header icon, minimal outline style.

## Typography & Color
Strong two-level hierarchy: bold section headers (~22px) vs. card destination names (large, ~28px bold white) vs. supporting subtext (muted gray, ~13px). The location prompt uses a medium-weight headline with a lighter secondary line beneath it — clear subordination. Color palette is restrained: white background, near-black text, medium gray for secondary text, and a single brand green used only for the location icon background. Green functions as an accent/action signal. White overlaid on photography for card text — relies on image contrast rather than a heavy scrim.

## Patterns
- **Inline permission request as a feature hook**: Rather than a system dialog, the location access prompt is embedded as a contextual card row with benefit-first copy ("Looking for something nearby?"). Reduces friction and frames the permission as a user benefit.
- **Category tags on imagery**: Floating pill labels on the card images act as content metadata without requiring a separate text area below the card — keeps cards compact and scannable.
- **Partial card bleed for scroll affordance**: The second card in the carousel is intentionally cropped, communicating horizontal scrollability without any scroll indicator UI. Elegant implicit signaling.
- **Save action embedded in browse**: Heart buttons are present during discovery, not just on detail pages — reduces the steps to capture intent at the moment of inspiration.
- **Two carousel densities**: Large immersive cards for editorial/curated content vs. smaller square cards for trending — visual hierarchy between "aspirational" and "popular" content types without needing labels to explain the difference.
- **Benefit-first microcopy**: "Plan your next adventure" and "Trending with travelers" are emotionally framed, not functional labels like "Destinations" or "Popular."

## Notes
- Inline permission prompt > modal interrupt — always frame as user benefit first
- Bleed crop = scroll hint, no arrows needed
- Two card sizes = implicit content hierarchy (editorial vs. social proof)
- Tags on image = metadata without layout cost
- Green accent used sparingly = high signal value when it appears
- Save-on-browse pattern — capture intent at discovery, not just detail
- White pill chips on photography — needs careful image selection to maintain legibility
- Section headers: conversational/aspirational tone over functional labels

## Screen
A multi-currency digital wallet home screen — fintech/money transfer app. This is the primary dashboard, the first screen after login. Layout flows top-to-bottom: header utility bar → horizontal wallet selector → action shortcuts → recently paid contacts → transaction history → bottom nav.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is divided into two clear visual zones separated by a subtle horizontal rule: the "active/functional" upper zone (wallets + actions) and the "historical/informational" lower zone (transactions). The wallet selector is a horizontally scrollable pill row — compact and non-intrusive. The action shortcuts use a horizontal card carousel with generous padding inside each tile. Section headers ("Recently paid," "Transactions") use a consistent label + "View all" pill pattern. Transaction rows use comfortable vertical rhythm with clear left-icon / center-text / right-amount three-column structure.

## Components
- **Wallet selector pills**: Rounded rectangle chips with flag emoji + currency code + balance. Selected state appears as black filled circle (app logo). "Add wallet" uses a + icon chip at the end of the row.
- **Action cards**: Square-ish rounded cards in bold distinct colors (green, yellow, purple). Each contains a 3D illustrative icon and a short label at the bottom-left. Partially visible third card signals horizontal scroll.
- **Recently paid avatars**: Circular avatar thumbnails in a horizontal row — blurred/redacted here but clearly a contact scroll strip.
- **Transaction rows**: Left-aligned icon (branded or avatar), merchant name + timestamp, right-aligned amount. Currency conversion transactions show two-line amounts (debit in black, converted credit in green).
- **"View all" pill**: Small rounded rectangle with light gray fill, minimal label — consistent across both sections.
- **Bottom nav**: 5-item tab bar with outline icons + labels. "Pay" uses a diagonal arrows icon suggesting transfer directionality.

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Section headers are bold ~18px; transaction merchant names are medium weight ~15px; timestamps and secondary info are light/regular ~13px in gray.
- **Amounts**: Right-aligned, medium-bold. Negative amounts in near-black, positive amounts in green — classic debit/credit color coding.
- **Palette**: White background, near-black text. Accent colors confined to the action cards (vivid green, yellow-green, purple). Green used functionally for positive transactions. Gray used for secondary text and UI chrome.
- **Color is purposeful**: No decorative color in the transaction list — color only appears where it carries meaning (positive = green, action cards = category-coded).

## Patterns
- **Partial card reveal as scroll affordance**: The third action card is deliberately cropped, signaling horizontal scrollability without any explicit indicator — clean and implicit.
- **Dual-amount transaction row**: Currency conversion shows both the deducted amount and the received amount in a different currency stacked — solves a genuinely complex data display problem elegantly in one row.
- **Wallet selector as persistent context**: The currency pills stay visible above the fold, keeping the user oriented about which wallet is active without requiring a separate screen.
- **3D illustration in action cards**: Using isometric/3D objects in the shortcut cards gives them visual weight and memorability compared to flat icon buttons — makes actions feel more tangible.
- **Blurred "Recently paid" section**: Privacy-first pattern — contacts are obscured by default, reducing shoulder-surfing exposure without hiding the feature's existence.
- **"Get help" as top-right text link**: Avoids burying support behind a hamburger menu — surfaces it at the header level, suggesting the app expects users may need help frequently (common in fintech onboarding contexts).

## Notes
- Dual-currency transaction row = killer pattern for any FX/conversion product
- Partial card crop = free scroll affordance, no arrows needed
- Keep action cards illustrative not iconographic — more tactile, more memorable
- Wallet chips as persistent balance display = no need to navigate to see balance
- Privacy blur on contacts = good default, consider toggle
- "View all" pill is a great alternative to text links for section expansion — feels tappable
- Color reserved for meaning only in data rows — restraint = clarity
- 3-column transaction layout (icon / description+time / amount) is the gold standard for finance lists

## Screen
Onboarding checklist screen from a fitness/class-booking app. This is a post-signup activation screen designed to guide new users through key setup steps. Sits between initial account creation and first meaningful engagement. Single-column accordion list layout with a progress indicator at top and back navigation.

## Layout & Spacing
Content flows top-down: nav bar → progress track → divider → accordion list items. The expanded first item creates clear visual hierarchy — it gets noticeably more vertical real estate than collapsed items. Collapsed rows are uniform in height, creating a clean rhythm. Left-aligned circle checkboxes with consistent left margin across all rows. The CTA button is left-aligned (not full-width), which feels intentional and less aggressive.

## Components
- **Progress bar (segmented):** 5 discrete pill-shaped segments, first filled in red, rest in light gray. Communicates 1-of-5 completion visually without numbers.
- **Accordion rows:** Circle checkbox (unfilled = incomplete) + label text + chevron right-aligned. Expanded state reveals description text + action button below label.
- **Circle checkboxes:** Outlined, empty circles — no fill, no check. Simple incomplete state. No checked state visible.
- **CTA button:** Rounded pill shape, solid red fill, white uppercase text, left-aligned, compact width. Not full-bleed.
- **Chevrons:** Up chevron on expanded item, down on collapsed — standard but clean.
- **Divider line:** Thin separator between progress bar and list.

## Typography & Color
- **Title:** Medium-weight, centered, ~17px — standard nav title treatment
- **Row labels:** Regular weight, ~16px, dark gray/near-black
- **Description text:** Smaller, ~14px, medium gray — clear secondary hierarchy
- **Button label:** Bold, uppercase, small caps feel, white on red
- **Color palette:** White background, near-black text, medium gray secondary text, light gray inactive segments, red (#E8394D-ish) as the single accent — used on progress fill and primary CTA only. Red is the sole action color, used sparingly and consistently.

## Patterns
- **Segmented progress over percentage:** Using discrete pill segments instead of a continuous bar maps 1:1 to checklist items — users can intuit "5 steps total" without reading anything.
- **Auto-expand first incomplete item:** The first task opens by default, removing friction — user lands with immediate context and a clear next action.
- **Contained CTA width:** The action button doesn't stretch full-width, which reduces visual weight and feels less coercive — appropriate for an optional setup step.
- **Checklist as feature discovery:** Each item names a specific feature ("class filter," "bookmark") — the checklist doubles as a product tour without feeling like a tutorial.
- **Motivational microcopy in expanded state:** "so we can celebrate your wins" — emotional framing embedded in functional instruction, not a separate tooltip.
- **Collapse-to-focus:** Keeping unstarted items collapsed reduces cognitive load — user sees the task list exists but isn't overwhelmed by all instructions at once.

## Notes
- Segmented progress pills = 1 pill per task, elegant pairing
- Left-aligned pill button = softer CTA, worth testing vs full-width
- Auto-open first item = remove one tap from the critical path
- Checklist = stealth feature tour pattern
- Emotional copy inside functional description — not a separate element
- All accent color budget spent on progress + CTA only, nothing else
- Empty circle checkbox = intentionally unfinished feeling, creates tension to complete

## Screen
An educational/onboarding explainer screen within a payment app, explaining in-store payment options. Sits between account setup and active payment flows — likely accessed via a help or "learn more" entry point. Layout is a single scrollable column with a hero promotional card at top, two grouped accordion sections below, and a feedback banner anchored at the bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is divided into three clear zones: (1) a contextual action card, (2) instructional accordions grouped under labeled sections, (3) a sticky feedback prompt. Generous white space between accordion rows creates visual breathing room. Section labels ("How to pay," "Additional info") are left-aligned with no container, acting as lightweight dividers. The bottom feedback banner bleeds edge-to-edge, contrasting with the padded content above.

## Components
- **Hero card**: Rounded card with large bold headline left-aligned, a pill-shaped CTA button in black, and a collage of lifestyle images bleeding off the right edge — partially clipped for a dynamic, editorial feel. Dismissible via an ✕ icon in the top-right corner.
- **Accordion rows**: White rounded-rectangle cards, each with a leading icon (outlined, monochrome), label text, and a chevron-down on the right. Uniform height, no visible dividers between items — separation achieved purely through card gaps.
- **Section headers**: Small, medium-weight labels on the background color — no lines or heavy treatment.
- **Feedback banner**: Light blue tinted strip with a megaphone icon and inline text — low-hierarchy but persistent.

## Typography & Color
Two-level type hierarchy: bold large headline in the hero card, regular body weight for accordion labels, small regular for section headers. No extreme size contrast — calm and readable. Color palette is near-monochrome: off-white/light gray background, white cards, black text and CTA button. The light blue feedback strip is the only chromatic accent, drawing the eye without competing. Icons are simple outlines in dark gray.

## Patterns
- **Clipped image collage on card**: Images intentionally overflow the card boundary on the right — creates depth and editorial energy without extra screen real estate.
- **Dismissible promotional card within an informational screen**: Unusual to embed a store-selection CTA inside a "how it works" explainer — bridges education and action in one surface.
- **Icon-led accordions as navigation metaphor**: Each payment method gets a distinct icon (card, QR, phone) making the list scannable before reading — reduces cognitive load for users who already know what they need.
- **Dual section grouping**: Separating "How to pay" from "Additional info" signals priority without hiding content — users can ignore the second group on first pass.
- **Persistent feedback prompt at bottom**: Anchored survey prompt that doesn't interrupt the content scroll — passive data collection embedded in the flow.
- **Negative space as separator**: Accordions use gap-between-cards rather than dividers or borders — cleaner and more modern than traditional list separators.

## Notes
- Collage bleeding off card edge = cheap way to add visual richness, steal this
- Black pill button on white card = high contrast CTA without color dependency
- Accordion icons do double duty: visual scan aid + reinforce payment method concept
- Blue feedback strip = non-modal survey, consider for post-action moments
- Section label as sole divider (no line, no box) — works when BG color ≠ card color
- Dismissible card inside help screen = hybrid edu/action pattern worth exploring
- All rounded corners, no hard edges anywhere — contributes to soft, trustworthy feel

## Screen
An onboarding/education screen from a fintech or BNPL-style payment app explaining how the payment service works. Sits between account setup and active use — a "how it works" explainer that bridges discovery and action. Layout is a scrollable single column with a hero promo card at top, a section label, then expandable accordion-style payment method cards below.

## Layout & Spacing
Top hero card spans nearly full width with generous internal padding — left-heavy text layout with a collaged image cluster on the right. Section label "How to pay" sits flush left with comfortable breathing room above and below. Payment method cards are contained in a rounded white card with visible internal dividers. Content within expanded accordion uses a vertical step list with a supporting photo inset to the right. The overall background is a very light gray/off-white, making white cards lift slightly.

## Components
- **Hero promo card**: White rounded card, dismissible (×), bold left-aligned headline, pill CTA button (black, rounded-full), collaged lifestyle images overlapping the right edge — creates depth and visual interest
- **Pill/badge tags**: Small outlined pills ("3 min setup," "Valid 24h") in light gray with small text — metadata without visual weight
- **Accordion rows**: Icon + label + chevron (up/down) pattern; expanded state reveals step content inline rather than navigating away
- **Vertical step list**: Bullet dots connected by a vertical line — classic stepper pattern used inline within accordion
- **Secondary row link**: "View all cards" with right chevron — tertiary navigation embedded within the card
- **Inset photo**: Rounded rectangular photo thumbnail floated right within the expanded step content, grounding abstract steps in real-world context

## Typography & Color
- **Headline (hero)**: Large, bold, black — high contrast, commanding
- **Section label**: Medium weight, left-aligned, slightly smaller — clear hierarchy marker
- **Accordion titles**: Medium weight with icon, consistent sizing
- **Step text**: Two-level — bold step name + lighter gray subtext description beneath
- **Badge text**: Small, gray, low emphasis
- Color palette: Near-white background (#F2F2F7 range), pure white cards, black for primary text and CTA, medium gray for secondary text, badges in light gray. Minimal color — functional and clean.

## Patterns
- **Dismissible promo card within an educational screen** — unusual to have a store-selection CTA embedded in a "how it works" flow; creates a shortcut for ready users without abandoning the explainer for explorers
- **Accordion with inline step visualization** — rather than linking to separate detail pages, the steps expand in-place with a visual (photo) that shows the physical action, reducing cognitive load
- **Metadata badges before steps** — "3 min setup" and "Valid 24h" appear before the steps, not after. This front-loads trust/reassurance before the user reads the effort required
- **Stepper-within-accordion** — nesting a linear step flow inside a collapsible component is a compact way to show process without a full onboarding screen
- **Collaged image as hero decoration** — images overlap the card boundary slightly, breaking the rigid grid and adding editorial quality to a utility screen
- **Icon differentiation per payment method** — each accordion row has a distinct icon (card, QR) enabling fast scanning without reading labels

## Notes
- Hero card = dual purpose: education + conversion shortcut. Smart for users at different intent levels
- "Valid 24h" badge = urgency/utility signal, not marketing fluff — worth noting for fintech contexts
- Badges before steps = anxiety reduction before effort reveal
- Inline photo in step list = show don't tell, keeps user in flow
- Collage bleeding off card edge = easy way to add visual richness without full bleed imagery
- Accordion in explainer screens = great for progressive disclosure when multiple methods exist
- Off-white bg + white cards = subtle depth without shadows being heavy

## Screen
Interest/preference selection screen in what appears to be a social or dating app onboarding flow. Users pick lifestyle interests from categorized tag clouds. Sits mid-onboarding, after basic profile setup. Layout is a scrollable vertical list of collapsible category sections with a persistent selection counter at top and floating CTA button bottom-right.

## Layout & Spacing
Top area acts as a "selection tray" showing chosen items. Categories stack vertically with generous section padding. Tags within each section use natural flow/wrapping layout — no forced grid. "See more" links sit centered below each truncated category. Breathing room between sections feels intentional, roughly 24–32px. The floating button anchors to bottom-right rather than spanning full width.

## Components
- **Selection counter label** ("My selection 1/10") — small, muted, left-aligned above tray
- **Selected tag in tray** — filled lavender/purple pill, bold text, larger than grid tags
- **Category headers** — icon + large bold text + chevron, collapsible accordion pattern
- **Unselected tags** — outlined pill/chip, light border, rounded rectangle, neutral background
- **Selected tag (in-grid)** — same lavender fill as tray tag, bold text, visually matches tray item
- **"See more" link** — centered, muted gray, minimal weight
- **Floating CTA button** — dark rounded square, right-pointing chevron, bottom-right fixed

## Typography & Color
Clear two-level hierarchy: category headers are large/heavy (~22px bold), tags are medium/regular (~14–15px). Counter label is small and subdued. 

Color palette is minimal: warm off-white background (#F5F0EB-ish), near-black text, soft lavender (#C9A8E8-ish) for selected state, medium gray for secondary text. Color is used functionally — lavender exclusively signals "selected," creating instant visual feedback across both tray and grid simultaneously.

## Patterns
- **Mirrored selection state** — selected tag appears both in the tray AND highlighted in-place within its category grid. Dual-location feedback reinforces the action without removing the item from its context
- **Quota-based selection** ("1/10") — gamifies completion, implies a maximum, creates mild urgency without pressure
- **Progressive disclosure via "See more"** — keeps initial cognitive load low; categories feel manageable even if they contain 20+ options
- **Accordion categories with icons** — chevron suggests collapsibility; icon adds scannability so users can skip irrelevant sections fast
- **Floating directional CTA** — small, unobtrusive chevron button rather than a full-width "Next" bar keeps focus on content; doesn't interrupt the browsing flow
- **Tray as living summary** — the top area acts as a lightweight cart/basket, giving users a persistent view of their choices without navigating away
- **Playful tag copy** ("Pasta or nothing," "Wine and more wine") — lowers the formality of onboarding, makes selection feel like self-expression rather than form-filling

## Notes
- Dual-state tag (tray + in-grid highlight) = clever — steal this for any multi-select scenario
- Lavender as sole accent = very clean, one color does all the work
- Floating chevron CTA > full-width button for browse-heavy screens
- "X/10" counter = soft gamification, consider for any curation flow
- Accordion + "see more" = two levels of progressive disclosure stacked
- Warm off-white bg feels softer than pure white for preference/personality screens
- Tag copy tone sets the whole app's personality — worth investing in microcopy here

## Screen
Financial hub/wallet screen in a social or payments app. This is a secondary tab destination (money/finance section) rather than the primary home. Layout flows top-to-bottom: page title + avatar, primary balance card, action buttons, then a 2×2 feature grid. Sits mid-funnel — accessible after onboarding, used for managing funds and discovering financial products.

## Layout & Spacing
White cards float on a light gray (#F2F2F2) background, creating clear visual separation without hard borders. The balance card spans full width; below it, two equal-width pill buttons sit side by side. The 2×2 grid uses consistent card sizing with generous internal padding. The grid cards are slightly shorter than the hero balance card, creating a natural visual hierarchy through size alone. Gutters between grid cards are tight (~8px), while the section gap between the balance card and grid is larger (~16px).

## Components
- **Balance card**: White rounded rectangle, label + secondary link ("Account & Routing >") on one row, large dollar amount below, two pill buttons at bottom
- **Pill buttons**: Light gray fill, no border, bold label, equal width — subdued but clearly tappable
- **Feature cards**: White rounded corners, title + chevron top-left/right, illustrated asset bottom-right bleeding slightly, balance/subtext for active products
- **Avatar**: Small circular photo top-right, acts as profile entry point
- **Bottom nav**: 5 icons, minimal stroke style, no labels

## Typography & Color
- Page title: Large, heavy black (~24px)
- Balance figure: Extra-large bold (~36px), dominant visual element
- Card titles: Medium bold (~15px)
- Secondary text: Small gray, used for subtext like "Save for a goal"
- Chevron links: Gray, small — low visual weight
- Color palette: Near-white background, white cards, black text, gray secondaries. Accent color lives entirely in illustrations — green (savings), blue (bitcoin), purple (stocks), yellow/gold (tax). Color is decorative and categorical, not functional UI color.

## Patterns
- **Illustration as product identity**: Each financial product gets a unique illustrated asset rather than an icon. The illustrations are 3D/isometric-leaning and thematic — stacked coins for crypto, layered waves for stocks, document stack for taxes. This makes abstract financial products feel tangible and distinct without using logos.
- **Bleeding illustration layout**: Artwork extends to card edges and slightly overflows, creating depth and making cards feel less boxy — a deliberate break from strict padding rules.
- **Zero-state as neutral, not empty**: $0.00 balance is displayed with full confidence — same large type, no empty state illustration or "get started" messaging. The UI trusts the user to act via the buttons.
- **Feature discovery via grid**: Financial products (bitcoin, stocks, tax) are surfaced as peer-level cards alongside the active savings product. Discovery is baked into the home state rather than buried in a menu.
- **Chevron placement signals navigation type**: "Account & Routing >" sits inline with the label as a text link; card titles have chevrons to their right — two different navigation affordances clearly differentiated by position and context.
- **Subdued primary actions**: Add/Cash Out buttons use gray fill rather than a brand color CTA, keeping the visual hierarchy calm. The balance number itself is the hero, not the buttons.

## Notes
- Illustrations do the color work — keep UI chrome monochrome, let art carry personality
- 2×2 grid is a strong pattern for "financial product shelf" — feels like a store, not a dashboard
- Bleed illustrations past card bounds = instant depth upgrade
- Zero states don't need to apologize — show the real UI, trust user intent
- Inline text links vs. card chevrons = two nav patterns coexisting cleanly
- Gray pill buttons > colored CTAs when balance display is the real focus
- Avatar top-right as sole profile entry — no "Profile" tab needed

## Screen
Event discovery and calendar app — onboarding/authentication entry point. This is the first screen a new user sees, combining marketing illustration with a sign-up bottom sheet. The layout splits into two zones: an illustrated hero taking the top ~55% and a modal-style card anchored to the bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
The bottom sheet uses generous internal padding (~24px sides) with clear vertical rhythm between headline, subtext, and CTAs. Buttons are stacked with consistent ~12px gaps. The two SSO buttons sit in a 50/50 grid row beneath the full-width options, creating a natural visual hierarchy of auth methods. The sheet has a large top-left logo mark and a dismissal X in the top-right — balanced anchoring at the corners.

## Components
- **Bottom sheet card**: White, rounded top corners (~20px radius), floats over the illustrated background with subtle shadow. Not full-screen — intentionally leaves hero visible above.
- **Primary CTA button**: Full-width, black fill, white text, fully pill-shaped (~50px height). Maximum contrast, maximum weight.
- **Secondary CTA button**: Full-width, light gray fill, dark text, same pill shape. Clearly secondary without being invisible.
- **SSO buttons (2-up)**: Square-ish with same gray fill and rounded corners, icon-only. Compact and equal-weight.
- **App icon / star mark**: 4-pointed star used as brand mark in top-left of sheet — doubles as identity anchor.
- **Illustrated hero**: 3D floating elements (avatar bubbles, calendar, location pin, ticket) arranged on concentric circle guides suggesting a radar/orbit motif.

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: ~24px, bold/black weight, near-black — immediate hierarchy
- **Body**: ~14px, medium gray, 2-line description — minimal, functional
- **Button text**: ~16px, medium weight, white on black / dark on light
- Color palette: Soft gradient background (warm peach/lavender/gray), pure white sheet, black primary action, light gray secondary actions. The 4-pointed star in the hero uses a warm orange-to-purple gradient — the only brand color accent.

## Patterns
- **Peek-behind sheet**: The bottom sheet deliberately doesn't cover the full screen, letting the illustrated world "peek" above — creates curiosity and context without a separate splash screen
- **Auth method hierarchy via shape+color**: Phone (black/primary) → Email (gray/secondary) → Apple/Google (icon-only gray) — three tiers of auth communicated purely through visual weight, no labels needed for the icons
- **Orbit/radar diagram as value prop**: The concentric circles with floating avatars and event objects visually communicate "events + people near you" without a single word of explanation
- **3D memoji-style avatars**: Using system-style avatar faces in the illustration creates immediate personal relatability and signals social features
- **Dismissible onboarding**: The X button signals low pressure — user can explore before committing, reducing friction anxiety
- **Brand mark repetition**: Same 4-pointed star appears both in the hero (large, gradient) and on the sheet (small, black) — subtle but effective brand anchoring across both zones

## Notes
- Split-zone layout (illustration top / action bottom) avoids full-screen modal coldness
- Black pill button = strong but not aggressive; works well against white card
- 2-up icon-only SSO row saves vertical space while keeping options present
- Orbit diagram = zero-copy feature communication — steal this
- Dismissible gate = trust signal, worth testing vs. forced flow
- Star/sparkle mark as brand icon trending — versatile at small sizes
- Gradient hero bg ties to brand accent color without overwhelming UI

## Screen
A subscription/plan selection screen within a fintech app. Sits in the upsell or onboarding-to-premium flow, presenting two tiered membership options side by side for comparison. Vertical scroll layout with two large plan cards stacked below a header section.

## Layout & Spacing
Header zone uses generous top padding with left-aligned title + subtitle. A pill-shaped teaser element ("Here's a 'lil overview") floats between header and cards, acting as a soft CTA bridge. Cards consume most of the viewport — nearly full-width with rounded corners and visible padding. The two cards are differentiated by background color, creating implicit hierarchy without explicit "recommended" badges. Illustration occupies the right half of each card, text the left — consistent two-column internal layout within each card.

## Components
- **Page title**: All-caps, heavy weight, left-aligned — dominates the header zone
- **Subtitle**: Regular weight, muted tone, 2-line description
- **Teaser pill**: White rounded rectangle with conversational label — low-commitment entry point
- **Plan cards**: Large rounded-rect containers with distinct background fills; each has a logo badge, plan name, description copy, illustrated mascot/object, and a CTA button
- **CTA buttons**: Solid fill, rounded pill shape, white text with arrow icon — contained within each card
- **Watermark text**: Large ghosted plan name repeated in background of each card as texture
- **Bottom nav**: Icon + label tabs, active state uses filled icon variant

## Typography & Color
- Title: Bold/black weight, dark navy, all-caps — maximum visual weight
- Plan names: Extra-bold, large size, dark navy
- Body copy: Regular weight, medium size, slightly muted — readable against colored backgrounds
- Color palette: Deep navy background, bright cobalt blue for top card, medium purple for bottom card, white for buttons and UI elements, yellow/gold for sparkle accents
- Color is used functionally to tier the plans — blue feels premium/active, purple feels secondary

## Patterns
- **"Choose Your Fighter" framing**: Gamified language reframes a mundane plan comparison as an exciting choice — reduces decision anxiety through personality
- **Watermark-as-texture**: The ghosted large plan name behind content adds depth and reinforces brand identity without adding UI clutter — purely decorative but purposeful
- **Illustration as emotional differentiator**: Each plan gets a distinct illustrated object (parachuting credit card vs. donut) that communicates the plan's benefit metaphorically — the parachute implies safety/control, the donut implies indulgence/relief
- **No feature comparison table**: Deliberately avoids the typical spec grid; instead uses short emotional benefit copy — reduces cognitive load, pushes to "Learn More" for detail
- **Teaser pill as soft entry**: The overview pill is a lower-commitment action than jumping straight into a plan — acknowledges users who aren't ready to decide
- **Card hierarchy without explicit labeling**: Blue card appears first and has a slightly more energetic illustration — implies it's the "better" option without saying "recommended"
- **Conversational microcopy**: "lil overview," "crushing pain of overdraft fees" — voice-forward copy that builds brand trust and reduces the sterile feel of financial product screens

## Notes
- Gamified plan naming ("Choose Your Fighter") → steal for any multi-option selection screen
- Watermark plan name as card bg texture — great depth trick, zero extra components
- Emotional illustration per tier > feature bullet lists
- Teaser/overview pill = smart middle-ground CTA for undecided users
- Two-column card layout: copy left, illustration right — consistent, scannable
- Color alone differentiates tiers — no "BEST VALUE" badge needed
- Conversational copy in fintech = trust signal, not unprofessional

## Screen
Subscription plan selection screen in a financial/trading app. Sits in the upgrade or paywall flow — likely triggered from a feature gate or settings. Full-screen layout with a hero image at top, scrollable plan cards in the middle, and a sticky CTA at the bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct vertical zones: (1) full-bleed hero image with headline overlaid, (2) scrollable plan card stack, (3) fixed bottom action area. Cards have generous internal padding (~20px) and sit close together with minimal gap, reinforcing the idea of comparison. The bottom CTA floats above the background with clear breathing room from the cards.

## Components
- **Hero image header** — B&W photography with logo and large white headline overlaid directly on image. No separate header bar; the image IS the header.
- **Plan cards** — Dark rounded-rectangle cards (~12–16px radius). Selected card has a vivid cyan-to-purple gradient border stroke. Unselected card has a subtle dark border. Radio-style selection indicator in top-left (checkmark circle vs. empty circle).
- **Plan label pill** — Small white-fill pill with bold all-caps black text ("ESSENTIAL", "PLUS"). Acts as a tier badge.
- **Price display** — Right-aligned within card, large weight for dollar amount, smaller "/Mo" suffix in lighter weight.
- **"See benefits" accordion trigger** — Inline text link with chevron, suggesting expandable content within the card.
- **Primary CTA button** — Full-width, tall (~56px), cyan-to-purple gradient fill with white bold text. High contrast, unmissable.
- **Secondary action** — Plain white text "Skip trial" below CTA, no button chrome.

## Typography & Color
- Headline: Large, heavy white sans-serif on image — dominant hierarchy
- Plan name pill: Small, bold, all-caps, black on white — badge-like
- Price: Medium-large, white, semi-bold; "/Mo" visually subordinate
- Description: Small, muted gray — supporting copy
- Overall palette: Near-black backgrounds (#1a1a1a range), white text, gray secondary text, cyan-to-purple gradient as the brand accent. Gradient is used exclusively for selection state and primary CTA — creating strong visual consistency between "chosen" and "act."

## Patterns
- **Gradient border as selection indicator** — Instead of a simple highlight or color fill, the selected card gets a full gradient stroke border. Elegant, premium-feeling, avoids heavy visual weight while still being unmistakable.
- **B&W hero as emotional framing** — Aspirational imagery (rock climber) sets a striving, achievement tone before the user reads a single word of copy. Color is intentionally stripped to not compete with the gradient UI below.
- **Benefit inheritance copy** — "All the benefits of ESSENTIAL and more" reduces cognitive load; user doesn't need to re-evaluate lower tier features when considering upgrade.
- **CTA copy reduces friction** — "Try free for 30 days" reframes the action as risk-free. The skip option is present but visually de-emphasized (no button, plain text) — present for trust, not for encouragement.
- **Partial card bleed** — The second card is partially cut off, signaling scrollability and implying more tiers exist — creates curiosity without cluttering.
- **Sticky gradient CTA mirrors selection gradient** — The button gradient matches the selected card border exactly, subconsciously linking "your selection" to "take action."

## Notes
- Gradient border = premium selection state worth stealing
- B&W photo + color UI = clean separation of emotion vs. function zones
- Badge pill inside card > card header label — feels more like a product tag
- Skip trial as ghost text only — trust signal without visual competition
- Price right-aligned, badge left-aligned = natural F-pattern scan across the card
- "See benefits" accordion keeps cards compact without hiding value
- Sticky CTA + ghost secondary = classic high-convert paywall pattern, but gradient execution elevates it

## Screen
Content creation hub / home screen for a social media design tool. This is the main dashboard — the entry point after onboarding. Layout flows vertically: top nav → content type selector → template carousel → tools section. Primary interactive elements are the FAB (+), content type pills, template cards, and bottom nav.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Top nav uses a three-zone layout (hamburger / logo / PRO badge). Content type selector sits in a horizontal scroll row directly below. The template carousel dominates the mid-section with a large stacked card presentation. Generous white space between sections creates clear breathing room. Section label "EXPLORE TEMPLATES" uses wide letter-spacing to feel editorial rather than utilitarian. The tools section begins peeking at the bottom, implying scroll continuation.

---

## Components
- **FAB (+ button):** Black rounded square, high contrast, breaks from the pill-shaped content type buttons intentionally
- **Content type pills:** Light gray, rounded, icon + label pairs, horizontal scroll — selected state not visible but spacing implies equal weight
- **Stacked card carousel:** Three overlapping cards at slight rotation angles, creating depth and a "fanned deck" effect — the front card is full-bleed photo with overlaid editorial typography
- **CTA chip on card:** Dark pill button ("YEAR IN REVIEW") floats at the bottom of the front card — feels like a template label/action
- **PRO badge:** Outlined rectangle with a blue notification dot — upgrade prompt that's subtle but persistent
- **Bottom nav:** 5 items, icon + label, standard underline active state

---

## Typography & Color
- Section labels: small caps, tracked out, neutral gray — editorial feel
- Card typography: blackletter/gothic display font in yellow — high contrast, decorative, mimics the template style itself
- UI chrome: clean sans-serif throughout, minimal weight variation
- **Palette:** Near-white background, black FAB, muted gray pills, the card introduces the only saturated color (dusty blue, yellow type) — UI stays neutral so template content pops

---

## Patterns
- **Stacked fanned cards as carousel:** Instead of a flat horizontal scroll, cards are layered with rotation offsets — communicates "collection" and invites interaction through implied physicality
- **Template previews that demonstrate the product:** The card shown IS a finished design, so the template section doubles as social proof of output quality
- **FAB shape contrast:** Using a rounded square instead of a circle differentiates it from iOS conventions — feels branded, not default
- **PRO upgrade as notification dot:** Avoids a banner or modal; a single blue dot on the PRO badge is enough to draw attention without interrupting flow
- **Content type as creation intent selector:** Placing Reel/Story/Post/etc. as the first decision point shapes the entire creation flow downstream — format-first mental model
- **Card CTA as contextual label:** The pill button on the template card ("YEAR IN REVIEW") functions as both a category tag and a tap target — dual-purpose component

---

## Notes
- Stacked rotated cards = great for "browse mode" before committing to scroll
- FAB as rounded square — worth stealing for non-iOS-native feel
- Let template previews do the selling — no need for descriptive copy
- Blue dot on upgrade CTA = lowest friction upsell placement possible
- Format-first creation flow (pick Reel/Story before anything else) — strong mental model anchor
- Editorial spaced-caps section headers elevate perceived quality instantly
- Keep chrome neutral/white so user content always wins visually

## Screen
Personalized home/dashboard screen for a content or wellness app. Sits at the top of the main navigation flow — the first screen after login. Vertical scroll layout with a large featured content card dominating the upper portion, followed by a secondary suggestion card peeking at the bottom. Two-zone structure: greeting header + content feed.

## Layout & Spacing
Generous top padding before the greeting creates breathing room. The greeting headline is large and left-aligned, taking up significant vertical real estate — intentional weight given to personalization. The featured card spans nearly full width with rounded corners (~20–24px radius). Content below the card image uses internal padding cleanly separating image from metadata. The second card is partially visible, signaling scrollability without explicit affordance. Vertical rhythm feels editorial rather than utilitarian.

## Components
- **Greeting headline**: Large serif-adjacent bold text, left-aligned, two lines
- **Featured content card**: White card with full-bleed image top half, text metadata + CTA bottom half. Heavy corner radius. Subtle shadow or elevation implied
- **Content type label**: Small icon + muted label text above the title — lightweight metadata row
- **CTA button**: Black pill button with icon + label. High contrast, compact. Sits left-aligned rather than full-width
- **Secondary suggestion card**: Horizontal layout — square image (rounded corners) left, text right. Partially cropped = scroll hint
- **Dismiss button**: Floating circular X button overlaid on the secondary card — ghost/light style

## Typography & Color
- Headline: Very large, bold, dark — dominates the screen, feels warm and personal
- Card title: Medium-large bold, dark
- Metadata label: Small, muted gray, paired with icon
- Background: Warm off-white/cream — not pure white, adds softness
- Card backgrounds: Pure white, contrasting slightly against cream bg
- CTA: Black fill with white text — only high-contrast element, draws eye immediately
- Overall palette: Warm neutrals + colorful abstract art image as the only chromatic element

## Patterns
- **Personalized greeting as hero element** — name-first copy ("Sam, it's great to see you") used as emotional anchor, not just utility. Large type scale makes it feel like a billboard moment
- **Image bleeds into card, fades to white** — the artwork transitions via a soft fog/gradient into the white card body below, creating seamless image-to-content merge without a hard edge
- **Left-aligned pill CTA** — resists the urge to go full-width; feels more editorial and less form-like
- **Icon reinforcement on CTA** — headphone icon repeated on both the metadata label and the button, creating visual continuity and reinforcing content type
- **Partial card crop as implicit scroll invite** — no "see more" needed; the cut-off card bottom communicates feed depth naturally
- **Dismissible suggestion card** — X button on secondary card treats it as a nudge/recommendation, not mandatory content. Respects user agency
- **Artwork as mood-setter** — abstract painterly image sets emotional tone without literal content; aesthetic-first content preview

## Notes
- Cream bg > white bg for warmth — especially in wellness/lifestyle contexts
- Gradient fade from image into card body = elegant alternative to image/text hard cut
- Greeting at display size = high personalization signal, worth the space cost
- Left-align pill button feels premium vs full-width
- Repeat icon in label + button = subtle but cohesive
- Partial bottom card = scroll affordance without dots/arrows
- Floating X on cards = lightweight dismissal pattern, non-destructive feel
- Abstract art as hero image = mood over information

## Screen
Onboarding questionnaire screen from a mental wellness / emotional health app. Step 2 of 9 in an initial setup flow asking users to select their primary motivation for using the app. Full-screen layout with large heading, radio list, and sticky CTA.

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct zones: header (back nav + progress + headline), scrollable option list, and fixed bottom CTA. Generous top padding before the headline gives it room to breathe. List items use horizontal dividers rather than card containers — keeps the layout light and open. Each row has consistent vertical padding (~20px feel). The 3-column row structure (radio → label → illustration) uses space efficiently without feeling cramped.

## Components
- **Progress indicator**: Minimal single-line dash (not a full bar) centered in the nav row — understated, non-anxious pacing signal
- **Step counter (2/9)**: Small numeric label, right-aligned — gives concrete context without emphasis
- **Headline text block**: Large, bold, conversational serif-adjacent weight — dominates the top third
- **Radio buttons**: Outlined circles, unselected state only shown — clean, no fill noise
- **List rows**: Text-left, illustration-right, separated by thin hairline dividers on dark background
- **Illustrations**: Small 3D/character-style icons unique to each option — colorful, emotionally expressive, distinct per item
- **CTA button**: Full-width pill, muted dark gray fill — intentionally de-emphasized until a selection is made

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: Large (~28–32pt), bold weight, white — conversational and direct tone
- **Option labels**: Medium (~15pt), regular weight, white/light gray — readable but subordinate
- **Step counter**: Small, muted gray
- **Palette**: Pure black background, white text, hairline gray dividers, muted gray CTA. Color lives entirely in the illustrations — creates strong focal contrast on each option
- Color is used functionally: grayscale UI = neutral/calm, illustration color = emotional warmth and differentiation

## Patterns
- **Illustration as emotional encoding**: Each answer option has a distinct character/object illustration that metaphorically represents the emotional theme — not decorative, but aids comprehension and memorability
- **De-emphasized CTA until interaction**: The Continue button is gray/inactive-looking by default — subtly communicates "make a selection first" without disabling or adding helper text
- **Conversational headline framing**: "Before jumping in, let's explore why you're here" — reframes a survey question as a collaborative moment, reducing clinical feel
- **Hairline dividers on black**: Avoids card/container boxing which would feel heavy; dividers create separation with minimal visual weight
- **No selected state shown**: Screenshot captures the zero-state — interesting choice to show the screen before any interaction, keeping it clean for reference
- **Escape hatch option**: "Another reason not listed here" as final option — respects user autonomy and reduces drop-off from non-fitting answers

## Notes
- Illustrations do the emotional lifting — UI stays neutral/minimal
- Black bg + colorful 3D chars = high contrast delight without loud UI
- Progress as a single dash, not a bar — worth trying for low-anxiety flows
- CTA muted gray = soft gate, not a hard disabled state
- Conversational copy in headlines transforms surveys into conversations
- Hairline dividers > cards on dark backgrounds for list items
- Always include an "other" escape hatch in motivation/goal selection screens

## Screen
Account setup/onboarding screen for a financial app. This is a pre-KYC (Know Your Customer) overview screen — sits between initial account creation and identity verification. Acts as a roadmap before the user commits to the full onboarding flow. Layout is single-column, top-to-bottom: icon cluster → headline → subtext → vertical stepper → legal checkbox → CTA.

## Layout & Spacing
Content uses generous left-aligned padding throughout. The stepper section has clear vertical breathing room between each step (~24–32px). The legal block is visually separated from the stepper with significant whitespace, creating a distinct zone. CTA is pinned near the bottom with enough margin to feel grounded without being a sticky bar. The abstract icon cluster at top-left is compact and decorative, not centered — feels editorial rather than illustrative.

## Components
- **Abstract icon mark**: Stacked geometric shapes (triangle, circle, square) in yellow/blue/gray — acts as a brand illustration, not a traditional app icon
- **Vertical stepper**: Three steps connected by a thin vertical line. Step 1 uses a filled blue circle with checkmark (complete). Steps 2 and 3 use outlined circles with numbers. Each step has title, description, and either a "Complete" status label or a clock icon with time estimate
- **Time estimate badge**: Small clock icon + "Approx. X min" in green — inline with step metadata
- **Checkbox**: Square checkbox (checked, blue fill) with dense legal copy inline
- **Primary CTA button**: Full-width, pill-shaped, solid blue with white label — high contrast, dominant

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: Large, bold, black — 2 lines, left-aligned, sets strong visual anchor
- **Body/subtext**: Regular weight, medium gray — clearly secondary
- **Step titles**: Semi-bold black for active/upcoming, slightly lighter for completed
- **"Complete" label**: Bold blue — matches primary color, signals success state
- **Time estimates**: Small, green — functional color coding distinct from primary blue
- **Links**: Blue underline throughout (regulatory text, legal links)
- **Palette**: White background, primary blue (#2D5BE3 approx.), green for time/status, near-black for type, mid-gray for secondary text

## Patterns
- **Time-to-complete transparency**: Each pending step shows an estimated duration (2 min, 5 min). Reduces anxiety about commitment before starting — rare and thoughtful in onboarding flows
- **Progress as reassurance**: Showing step 1 already complete (with checkmark) gives the user a sense of momentum before they've done anything on this screen — psychological foot-in-the-door
- **Regulatory link as trust signal**: "Regulations" is hyperlinked in the subtitle — externalizing the reason for data collection to a third party (the law) rather than the company. Shifts framing from "we want your data" to "we're required to"
- **Legal copy integrated, not buried**: The certification checkbox is inline in the flow rather than hidden in a modal or footer — signals transparency
- **Geometric illustration as tone-setter**: Abstract shapes instead of a literal illustration keeps the screen feeling sophisticated/financial rather than playful — deliberate brand register choice
- **Stepper as commitment device**: Showing the full journey upfront lets users mentally pre-commit to the scope — reduces drop-off mid-flow

## Notes
- Time estimates on steps = underused pattern, steal this
- "Complete" in brand blue doubles as progress indicator + positive reinforcement
- Regulatory framing as trust-building, not just compliance boilerplate
- Pill CTA at bottom but NOT sticky — intentional scroll-to-commit friction
- Abstract icon cluster top-left breaks the "centered hero illustration" cliché
- Green for time/duration creates a third functional color without feeling chaotic
- Checkbox + dense legal inline = honest design, no dark pattern burial

## Screen
Onboarding survey screen from a financial or lifestyle app. Single-question attribution survey asking how the user discovered the product. Appears late in onboarding flow — post-signup, pre-dashboard. Full-screen layout with centered header, stacked list of options, and a fixed bottom CTA.

## Layout & Spacing
Light gray background creates a canvas that makes white option cards float. Header block (title + subtitle) sits in the upper third with generous breathing room before the list begins. Option cards are evenly spaced with consistent gaps — approximately equal vertical rhythm throughout. CTA button is centered at bottom, not edge-to-edge, giving it a pill-like standalone presence rather than a footer bar feel.

## Components
- **Header text block** — Large bold question title, smaller gray subtitle below. Centered alignment.
- **Option cards** — White rounded-rectangle cards, full-width with horizontal padding. Label left-aligned, checkbox right-aligned. Subtle drop shadow or elevation implied by white-on-gray contrast.
- **Checkboxes** — Square with rounded corners. Unselected: simple outline. Selected: filled blue with white checkmark icon.
- **Selected card state** — Entire card gets a blue border stroke to reinforce selection at the container level, not just the checkbox.
- **Continue button** — Pill-shaped (fully rounded), warm salmon/coral fill, white label. Centered, not full-width.

## Typography & Color
- **Title**: Large, bold, dark near-black — clear primary hierarchy
- **Subtitle**: Small, medium-weight, muted gray — secondary/instructional
- **Option labels**: Medium weight, dark — same visual level as each other, no hierarchy within list
- **Color palette**: Off-white background (#f2f2f2 range), white cards, blue accent for selection (#2563EB range), coral/salmon CTA (~#F08070 range)
- Color is used functionally: blue = selected state, coral = forward action — two distinct accent colors with clear semantic separation

## Patterns
- **Full-row selection target** — The entire card is the tap target, not just the checkbox. The blue border on the whole card confirms this — selection feedback spans the full component, reducing precision tapping anxiety.
- **Dual selection indicator** — Both the checkbox AND the card border change on selection. Redundant feedback that makes state unmistakably clear without relying on a single small element.
- **Warm CTA vs. cool selection color** — Deliberate use of two different accent hues: blue for data/selection, coral for action/progression. Prevents visual competition and creates clear affordance hierarchy.
- **Subtitle transparency about purpose** — "(multiple selection possible)" is called out explicitly in the subtitle, reducing cognitive friction and setting expectations before the user interacts.
- **Non-full-width CTA** — Pill button centered with space on sides feels less aggressive than edge-to-edge buttons; softer, more inviting interaction pattern appropriate for optional/survey context.

## Notes
- Card border highlight on select = underused pattern, very clean
- Two accent colors (blue/coral) = smart semantic split, steal this
- Subtitle that explains the UI behavior inline ("multiple selection") — consider for any non-obvious interaction
- Pill CTA centered, not stretched — works well for low-stakes confirmations
- Gray bg + white cards = effortless depth without shadows
- Checkbox redundancy (icon + border) worth copying for accessibility + clarity
- Survey screens don't need to feel clinical — rounded everything softens it considerably

## Screen
Audio content upload screen for a podcast creation app. This is a modal/sheet that appears mid-flow when a creator chooses to publish an episode via file upload. It sits between content type selection and the actual file picker. Single-purpose screen with one primary action. Layout is vertically centered with a floating contextual card anchored to the bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Top-left dismiss (X) floats freely. Large hero illustration occupies the upper third. Text block centered below with generous breathing room. CTA button centered with substantial vertical margin above and below. Bottom contextual card is visually separated by a large whitespace gap — feels like a secondary zone entirely. The two-zone split (primary action vs. contextual help) is the key structural decision.

## Components
- **Hero illustration**: Layered concentric circles in soft lavender/periwinkle, audio waveform bars in white, overlaid with a solid deep purple circular upload button with upward arrow. Depth created through opacity layers.
- **Title**: Large bold sans-serif, single word "Upload"
- **Body copy**: Two-line centered description with supported formats inline
- **Legal line**: Smaller gray text with two underlined inline text links (Platform Rules, Terms of Use)
- **Primary CTA**: Pill/stadium button, medium purple fill, bold white label, generous padding, full-width feel but contained width
- **Contextual card**: Rounded rectangle, light gray/off-white fill, subtle border, bold heading + body copy + external link icon top-right corner

## Typography & Color
Clear three-level hierarchy: bold display title → regular body → small legal/secondary. All centered except the bottom card (left-aligned). Color palette is monochromatic purple — deep indigo for icon button, mid-purple for CTA, soft lavender for illustration background. Gray for secondary text. Inline links use underline rather than color differentiation. White background keeps everything clean. Purple is used exclusively for interactive/branded elements, creating strong functional signal.

## Patterns
- **Layered icon as illustration**: The upload arrow button is embedded inside the hero art — the functional metaphor (upload) doubles as decorative illustration. Efficient and reinforces the action before the user reads anything.
- **Two-zone modal layout**: Hard separation between "do the thing" (top) and "learn more / alternate path" (bottom card) using whitespace alone — no divider line needed. The card feels like it belongs to a different layer.
- **Contextual escape hatch**: The bottom card addresses the most likely reason someone might abandon this screen ("I don't have a file yet") and redirects rather than losing the user. It's not a secondary CTA competing with the primary — it's a support card.
- **External link icon on card**: Signals the card leads out of the current flow without disrupting the primary path. Low visual weight but high utility signal.
- **Format info inline with description**: Supported file types listed in the subtitle rather than as a separate label or tooltip — reduces cognitive overhead, answers the implicit question preemptively.
- **Legal links as plain inline text**: Terms buried in a natural sentence rather than a separate legal row — reduces friction perception while maintaining compliance.

## Notes
- Hero icon = functional metaphor + illustration in one — steal this
- Two-zone modal: action zone / support zone split by whitespace only
- "Escape hatch card" pattern for users not ready to complete action
- Concentric circle + opacity layering = cheap depth for audio/media iconography
- Inline file format disclosure in subtitle — no extra UI needed
- External link icon on card = lightweight signal, doesn't compete with CTA
- Legal as sentence > legal as footer row
- All purple = brand + interactive only, zero decorative use

## Screen
Social content creation screen for a community/blog-style app. Users compose posts with photos, a title, and body text before publishing. Sits mid-flow after initiating a new post. Layout is a single scrollable column with a sticky bottom action bar.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Top-to-bottom flow mirrors the post's reading order: media → title → body → metadata → publish. Each section is visually separated by generous white space rather than dividers. Photo row and text fields use card-style containers with rounded corners. Bottom action bar floats above content with a privacy notice sandwiched between content and CTAs — a smart placement for disclosure without interruption.

---

## Components
- **Photo thumbnail** — Rounded square with a delete icon (trash) overlaid top-right in a dark pill/badge. Active state is clearly occupied.
- **Add photo slot** — Dashed border container with icon + label + remaining count ("8 left"). Communicates capacity without a separate help tooltip.
- **Title field** — Single-line card input with an AI sparkle icon flush right inside the field. Inline, non-intrusive AI trigger.
- **Body text field** — Multi-line card with character counter (402/1000) bottom-right and a floating "Improve for me" AI button bottom-right inside the card. Counter and AI action coexist cleanly.
- **"Improve for me" button** — Dark green pill with sparkle icon, floats inside the text area. Feels contextual rather than toolbar-based.
- **Topic row** — Hash icon + label + chevron. Tappable list row pattern, marked optional inline.
- **Suggested topics** — Partially visible chip row, signals scrollability.
- **Dual CTA bar** — "Save draft" (outlined/active) + "Post now" (filled but grayed/disabled). Clear state differentiation.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Hierarchy**: Bold nav title → field text (regular weight, readable size) → secondary labels (smaller, muted gray).
- **Color palette**: Near-white background, dark near-black text, dark green (#1a5c3a-ish) as the brand/action accent used exclusively on AI features and active CTAs.
- **Functional color use**: Green = AI-assisted actions + primary action. Gray = disabled state ("Post now"), helper text. The restraint keeps green meaningful.
- **Character counter**: Small, right-aligned gray — present but not demanding attention.

---

## Patterns
- **Capacity-aware upload slot**: "8 left" inside the add-photo placeholder eliminates a separate limit warning and sets expectations proactively.
- **AI assistance embedded at point of need**: Sparkle triggers live inside the fields they affect — not in a toolbar or separate panel. Title gets a single icon; body gets a full labeled button because it's the heavier editing task. The escalation in affordance matches the complexity of the action.
- **Disabled publish with active draft save**: Prevents premature publishing (possibly title/body required) while still letting users save progress. The visual contrast between the two buttons communicates state without an error message.
- **Privacy disclosure between content and actions**: "Your post will appear publicly" sits just above the CTAs — read at the moment of decision, not buried in settings.
- **Suggested topics peek**: Partial visibility of the chip row below the topic row creates a scroll invitation without a "see more" button.
- **Trash icon on media as overlay badge**: Keeps the photo full-bleed while making deletion accessible — avoids shrinking the thumbnail to accommodate controls.

---

## Notes
- AI triggers inside fields > AI in toolbar — proximity = discoverability
- "X left" in upload slot = proactive constraint communication
- Dual bottom CTAs: one always active (draft), one conditional (publish) — never block progress
- Privacy notice placement: decision-moment disclosure pattern
- Dashed border = "empty slot" convention, universally readable
- Sparkle icon as AI shorthand — use consistently to train users
- Partial chip row = implicit scroll affordance, no extra UI needed
- Character count + AI improve = complementary, not competing

## Screen
Event creation form in a social or community app. Mid-flow creation screen where users configure a new event. Layout is a single-column vertical scroll with a large media hero at top, followed by stacked input rows, then an options section below.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is divided into three clear zones: (1) hero image block, (2) core event fields, (3) secondary options. Each zone is visually separated by subtle spacing rather than hard dividers. Form fields are grouped into frosted/translucent card containers. The image takes up roughly 40% of visible screen height, giving it strong visual priority. Fields are generously tall for touch targets. "Options" section uses a left-aligned label above its card group — classic iOS settings-style grouping.

## Components
- **Hero image uploader** — large square card with rounded corners, white/light background, displays 3D generated art. Edit button sits as a small circular icon badge at bottom-right corner of the image.
- **Event Name field** — full-width frosted input, large placeholder text, no border, blends into background.
- **Date/Time row** — single card containing both Start and End rows. Connected visually with a dotted vertical line between the two dot indicators (filled circle for Start, hollow circle for End), mimicking a timeline/itinerary metaphor.
- **Location field** — icon-left layout, single row card.
- **Description field** — icon-left layout, single row card.
- **Options rows** — stepper/picker rows with label on left, current value + chevron-up-down icon on right (indicating inline picker or dropdown).

## Typography & Color
- Overall palette: muted blue-grey, desaturated and cool. Background has a soft gradient or blur effect.
- Text is white/light grey throughout — hierarchy achieved through size and opacity rather than color contrast.
- "Create Event" title is medium weight, centered.
- Field labels are slightly smaller and lower opacity than values.
- "Options" section label is small, uppercase-adjacent, subdued — classic secondary label treatment.
- The 3D iridescent artwork creates a vivid chromatic accent against the muted UI.

## Patterns
- **Timeline connector in date fields** — using a filled dot → dashed line → hollow dot to visually link Start and End times is a clever spatial metaphor borrowed from travel/itinerary UIs. Makes the time range feel like a duration, not two separate fields.
- **Generative/AI art as default cover** — instead of a blank placeholder, the image slot is pre-filled with a high-quality 3D abstract image, reducing the empty-state anxiety of a blank form and suggesting the creative tone of the platform.
