- **Edit badge on image** — small circular button overlaid at corner rather than a separate "change photo" text link. Keeps the image area clean while remaining discoverable.
- **Frosted glass form fields** — fields have no traditional border; they read as slightly lighter translucent surfaces against the background. Creates visual cohesion without hard UI chrome.
- **Value-right pattern for options** — Visibility and Capacity show current value right-aligned with a sort/stepper chevron, communicating current state at a glance before interaction.
- **Profile avatar + dropdown in nav** — suggests account-switching or context-switching is accessible from the creation flow itself, useful for multi-account or community-based apps.

## Notes
- Dotted line between start/end = steal this for any duration input
- Pre-filled hero art = kills blank state problem elegantly
- Frosted fields with no borders = form that doesn't feel like a form
- Muted grey-blue + iridescent 3D art = strong visual identity contrast trick
- Options section = settings-style grouping works well for secondary config
- Edit icon badge on media = cleaner than text link below image
- All placeholder text same low-opacity white = consistent ghost state language

## Screen
Onboarding step in a social/friend-network app — specifically the profile photo upload step. Sits mid-flow in account creation. Single-purpose screen with one clear action. Layout is top-to-bottom linear: logo → progress → headline → upload zone → privacy note → navigation.

## Layout & Spacing
Generous vertical breathing room between each section. The upload zone takes up the majority of screen real estate — roughly 55% of the content area — making it the undeniable focal point. Headline and subhead are center-aligned and tightly grouped together, then separated from the upload zone with comfortable margin. Privacy note sits just below the card, visually attached to it rather than floating. Navigation arrow is bottom-right anchored, not a full-width button.

## Components
- **Progress bar**: Full-width, thin line just below the logo. Left portion is orange/red, right is light gray. No step numbers, no labels — purely visual.
- **Upload zone**: Large rounded rectangle card in a slightly darker warm gray than the background. Centered image+plus icon in medium gray. No border/stroke — differentiated purely through fill contrast. Rounded corners ~16px.
- **Privacy note**: Small caption with a lock icon inline. Muted gray, low visual weight. Attached spatially to the upload card.
- **Navigation arrow button**: Circular, light gray fill, arrow icon. Floating bottom-right. Minimal, ghost-like presence.
- **Wordmark**: Script/handwritten style logotype centered at top.

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: Large, bold serif-adjacent sans (~28–30px), black. High contrast, commands attention.
- **Subhead**: Regular weight, medium gray, ~15px. Centered, two lines. Warm and conversational tone.
- **Privacy caption**: ~12px, light gray, minimal hierarchy weight.
- **Palette**: Warm off-white background (#F2EDE8 range), orange-red accent for progress only, dark charcoal for headline, mid-grays for supporting text and UI elements. Single accent color used with extreme restraint.

## Patterns
- **Progress bar as sole wayfinding** — no "Step 3 of 5" label, trusting the visual fill ratio to communicate progress. Cleaner, less anxiety-inducing.
- **Oversized upload target** — making the drop zone card large reduces tap hesitation and signals this step matters; it's not a small avatar circle.
- **Privacy reassurance anchored to the action** — the lock + caption is placed directly under the upload zone, not in a footer or tooltip. Addresses the concern exactly where the user is thinking about it.
- **Floating directional CTA instead of full-width button** — the arrow-only circle allows skipping/advancing without visually competing with the primary action. Implies "you can move on, but the upload is what we want."
- **Warm, human microcopy** — "make your friends smile" reframes a potentially friction-heavy step (uploading a photo) as a social, positive act rather than a requirement.
- **Tone-matched empty state** — the upload icon uses a soft gray that matches the card fill closely, keeping the empty state calm rather than stark or demanding.

## Notes
- Single accent color (orange) used only for progress = strong signal value
- Oversized upload card = commitment framing, makes action feel important
- Privacy note placement = contextual trust-building, not buried in settings
- Floating circle nav = "soft skip" pattern, reduces pressure without hiding the option
- Warm background tint softens what could feel like a clinical form step
- Handwritten logo in onboarding = personality injection at top of every step
- No button label needed when direction is obvious — arrow alone sufficient

## Screen
Shopping/fintech super-app home screen. Serves as a dashboard hub combining e-commerce discovery, order tracking, and financial tools. This is the post-login landing screen — the user's primary daily touchpoint. Layout flows vertically: branded header → search → quick-nav icons → promotional banner → financial/order card section → secondary nav icons → ad unit → bottom tab bar.

## Layout & Spacing
Two distinct background zones create visual hierarchy: a soft pink-to-lavender gradient occupies the top ~40%, transitioning to a light warm gray for the content-heavy lower section. The white card panel floats over this transition, creating depth. Horizontal icon rows use equal-width columns with generous vertical padding. The main content card uses ~16px side margins with internal sections separated by subtle dividers. Spacing tightens slightly in the secondary icon row, fitting 6 items vs. 5 in the top row.

## Components
- **Search bar**: Full-width pill shape, white fill, soft shadow, left-aligned icon + placeholder text
- **Quick-nav icons**: Circular pill/squircle containers with illustrated emoji-style icons, label below, equal spacing
- **Dismissible banner**: Rounded rectangle with illustrated icon left, bold headline + body copy, × dismiss button top-right
- **Split info card**: Two-column card with rounded corners, each cell containing icon + label/value — used for spending power and balance at a glance
- **Order filter tabs**: 4-column horizontal row with small illustrated icons above text labels, no explicit tab styling — relies purely on layout rhythm
- **CTA row item**: "Track all your orders" uses a + icon in a circle, left-aligned, full-width tap target
- **Secondary icon grid**: 6-up row, smaller squircle icons, tighter labels, scrollable implied
- **Ad banner**: Full-bleed card with brand image right, text left, "Ad" pill badge top-right corner

## Typography & Color
Clear 3-level hierarchy: bold display weight for headlines ("Compare prices. Save money."), medium weight for card values ("$0"), regular small for labels beneath icons. Color palette: pink/rose gradient header, lavender mid-tone, warm off-white content area, white cards. Functional color use — gold/amber for rewards icon, green for loyalty, purple for saved/watch, maintaining icon-level color coding without overwhelming the neutral shell. Black used for primary text only; gray for secondary labels.

## Patterns
- **Zone-based background theming**: Using gradient-to-neutral background shift to signal transition from "discovery/navigation" zone to "utility/account" zone — teaches users where they are without explicit headers
- **Dual-purpose card split**: The two-cell card (spending power + balance) packages two financial metrics in one glance without requiring separate sections — efficient trust-building real estate
- **Icon row as shortcut navigation**: Two separate icon rows serve different intent depths — top row is primary app modes, bottom row is product/feature access — layering without cluttering
- **Order status as passive filter tabs**: Order states (All, On its way, Delivered, Returns) presented as always-visible tabs rather than hidden filters, surfacing workflow state at a glance
- **Illustrated micro-icons**: Custom emoji-adjacent icons per feature give personality while maintaining functional clarity — warmer than standard iconography
- **Transparent ad labeling**: "Ad" pill badge is visually distinct but not aggressive — maintains trust while monetizing feed space

## Notes
- Gradient zone break = great trick for separating brand/nav from utility content without a hard line
- Two-col stat card = compact financial snapshot pattern, steal for dashboards
- Order status tabs always visible = reduces friction for repeat users, no hunting for filters
- 5-col vs 6-col icon rows = intentional density shift signals feature hierarchy
- Dismissible promo banner with illustration feels editorial, not interruptive
- Bottom nav only 3 items = aggressive simplification for a complex app, forces prioritization

## Screen
Travel/accommodation booking app — date selection step in a multi-step search flow. Sits between destination input and guest/filter selection. Layout is a vertical stack: persistent top nav with category tabs, a summary card showing prior step's input, then a large content card housing the date configuration UI, and a fixed bottom action bar.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in distinct horizontal bands separated by thin dividers. The main card uses generous internal padding (~20–24px). Three logical sub-sections stack vertically: trip type toggle → duration selector → month picker. Each section has a label + control pairing. The bottom action bar floats outside the card, keeping navigation chrome separate from content. The "Where" summary card above acts as a breadcrumb/context anchor with minimal height.

## Components
- **Segmented control (trip type):** Pill-shaped, full-width, three options — "Flexible" is selected with a white elevated pill on gray track. Smooth, rounded container.
- **Chip group (duration):** Individual rounded-rectangle chips, outlined style. Selected state ("Week") uses a bold border (~2px black) with no fill change — border weight alone signals selection.
- **Month tiles:** Square cards with rounded corners, icon + month name + year. Subtle border, light background. Horizontally scrollable (August partially visible = scroll affordance).
- **Summary row:** Minimal two-column layout — muted label left, value right. Clean card with soft shadow.
- **Reset / Next bar:** "Reset" as underlined text link (left), "Next" as full black pill CTA (right). High contrast pairing.

## Typography & Color
Near-monochromatic palette — white, light gray (#F5F5F5 range), and black. No accent colors. Type hierarchy: bold large heading ("When's your trip?") → medium section labels ("Stay for a week," "Go anytime") → body/chip text. Selected states communicated entirely through weight and border, not color. Year rendered in smaller, lighter gray below month name — clear sub-label treatment.

## Patterns
- **Progressive disclosure via segmented mode switching** — three fundamentally different interaction paradigms (exact dates / month browsing / flexible) behind one toggle, avoiding UI clutter
- **Selection state through border weight only** — the duration chips use stroke thickness as the sole selected indicator, no fill, no color change — elegant restraint
- **Horizontal scroll peek** — the partially visible "August" tile teaches scrollability without any explicit indicator or arrow
- **Contextual persistence** — the "Where" card above the main panel keeps prior search context visible, reducing cognitive load mid-flow
- **Dual-register action bar** — pairing a destructive-light action (Reset as text link) with a primary CTA (filled pill) creates clear visual hierarchy without adding a third button style
- **Sub-labels on tiles** — year shown beneath month name anticipates cross-year booking confusion proactively

## Notes
- Border-weight-only selection = great for monochrome design systems
- Peek scroll on tile grids > scroll indicators
- "Flexible" search UX: hide complexity behind mode toggle, not separate screens
- Summary card as persistent breadcrumb — steal this for any multi-step flow
- Reset as underlined link keeps it present but visually subordinate — better than ghost button here
- Dividers between sub-sections work harder than headers alone for scanability
- Black pill CTA on white bg = maximum contrast, no color dependency

## Screen
Post-workout effort rating screen in a fitness tracking app. Appears as a modal or sheet after completing an outdoor walk session. Sits in the post-activity logging flow. Layout is a single-column card with a centered content zone, a slider input, and a dual-action footer.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is vertically stacked with generous breathing room between zones: title block → emoji feedback → label + description → slider → utility action → education link → primary CTA → dismiss. The card sits inset from screen edges with rounded corners, creating a contained modal feel. The slider section has notably tighter vertical padding, making it feel like a distinct "data input" zone versus the expressive upper section.

## Components
- **Emoji indicator** — Large native emoji (~60px) used as the primary emotional feedback glyph. Changes dynamically with slider position. No border or container, floats freely.
- **Gradient slider** — Custom track running cool blue → green → yellow → orange → red → purple. Represents the full exertion spectrum visually. Thumb is a white circle with blue fill. Tick marks along track suggest discrete steps.
- **HR data annotation** — Small caps label ("BASED ON AVAILABLE HR DATA") with a vertical tick line above the slider, indicating a suggested position. Subtle but smart.
- **"Clear Entry" link** — Terracotta/orange text-only button. Low visual weight, destructive-adjacent action kept understated.
- **"How To Self-Assess" row** — Left-aligned text with a chevron. Inline educational link, styled like a table row.
- **Save button** — Full-width, high-radius pill, terracotta fill, white bold label. Dominant CTA.
- **Dismiss** — Text-only, same terracotta color, below Save. Creates a clear hierarchy: save > dismiss.

## Typography & Color
- **Title**: Large bold sans-serif, black — commands attention
- **Subtitle/question**: Regular weight, medium gray — secondary prompt
- **Effort label** ("Extremely Light"): Bold, slightly smaller than title — dynamic feedback text
- **Description**: Small, light gray, centered — supporting context
- **HR label**: All-caps, very small, gray — metadata/annotation tier
- Color palette is near-monochrome (white card, black/gray type) with terracotta/burnt orange as the single accent color used across CTA, links, and interactive elements. The slider gradient is the only multi-color element, functioning as a data visualization rather than decoration.

## Patterns
- **Emoji as dynamic state indicator** — Using a native emoji instead of a custom icon or numeric scale adds warmth and immediate emotional legibility. It likely animates as the slider moves, making the interaction feel alive.
- **Gradient-as-scale** — The color gradient on the slider encodes meaning (cool = easy, hot = hard, purple = max) so users can read effort level spatially and chromatically without reading labels.
- **Data-suggested default** — The HR annotation with a tick mark above the slider pre-positions the thumb based on objective data while leaving subjective override to the user. Respects both data and personal perception.
- **Three-tier description per state** — Emoji + bold label + plain-language description creates redundant encoding: visual, named, and experiential. Reduces ambiguity for a subjective metric.
- **Education link embedded in action flow** — "How To Self-Assess" placed between the input and the CTA catches users who are uncertain before they commit, without interrupting confident users.
- **Save/Dismiss pairing without modal chrome** — No cancel button in a traditional sense; "Dismiss" is soft and text-only, making abandonment feel low-stakes while "Save" is clearly the intended path.

## Notes
- Emoji-as-slider-feedback = steal this for any subjective rating input
- HR-suggested position tick = great pattern for "here's what the data says, but you decide"
- Gradient track doubles as legend — no separate key needed
- Terracotta accent on both destructive (Clear) and primary (Save) — works because weight/size differentiates intent
- "How to self-assess" inline edu link = reduce friction without a tooltip or modal
- All-caps metadata annotation style for data-sourced context
- Dismiss below Save = soft opt-out, not an escape hatch

## Screen
Onboarding goal-setting screen in a reading/learning app. Step 2 of a multi-step onboarding flow where the user selects a daily time commitment. Sits between initial preference capture and account creation. Layout is top-to-bottom linear: progress indicator → headline → scroll picker → CTA.

## Layout & Spacing
Generous vertical whitespace between the headline and the picker creates a calm, uncluttered feel — the picker floats in the lower-middle third of the screen. The CTA is anchored to the bottom with comfortable padding. The contextual tooltip sits left of the picker, horizontally paired to create a two-column micro-layout within an otherwise single-column screen.

## Components
- **Step progress bar**: Horizontal line with filled/unfilled segments and a numbered dark circle node. Current step labeled below in small text ("Aspiration").
- **Drum/scroll picker**: Vertical number scroll with strong center-selection emphasis. Numbers above and below fade in opacity and decrease in size, creating depth. Selected value ("10") is bold, large, and blue. Unit label ("min") sits inline to the right of the selected number only.
- **Contextual tooltip/callout**: Soft blue rounded hexagonal badge to the left of the picker with a right-pointing tail. Contains translated meaning of the selected value ("25 book summaries a month"). Updates dynamically with scroll.
- **CTA button**: Full-width, high-radius pill, solid blue, white label. Pinned to bottom.

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: Large, bold, dark — clear primary hierarchy
- **Subhead**: Small, medium-weight gray — reassuring micro-copy ("You can change it later")
- **Picker selected**: Large bold blue — active state communicates selection clearly
- **Picker unselected**: Progressively lighter blue, smaller — visual depth cue
- **Palette**: Warm off-white background (#F5F0EC-ish), primary blue for interactive/selected elements, muted blue for secondary picker values and tooltip fill. Monochromatic blue system throughout interactive layer.

## Patterns
- **Value translation tooltip**: The most notable pattern — converts abstract numeric input ("10 min") into a meaningful real-world outcome ("25 book summaries a month"). Reduces cognitive load and motivates commitment by making the goal tangible.
- **Drum picker over slider**: Deliberate choice — discrete values feel more intentional and committal than a continuous slider. Scrolling through numbers creates micro-engagement.
- **Fading peripheral values**: Depth illusion on a flat picker — top/bottom numbers fade out, reinforcing the "slot machine" metaphor without 3D perspective tricks.
- **Inline unit label**: "min" appears only next to the selected value, not repeated on every row — reduces noise while maintaining context.
- **Step label below progress node**: Naming the current step ("Aspiration") reframes a functional progress bar as a narrative journey marker.
- **Reassurance copy**: "You can change it later" directly below the headline — preemptively handles decision anxiety at a commitment moment.

## Notes
- Tooltip-as-value-translator = killer pattern for any goal/preference setting screen
- Unit label only on selected row → clean
- Warm background + blue accent = low anxiety, focused palette
- Progress bar with named steps > numbered steps alone
- Lots of dead vertical space — intentional breathing room, not wasted space
- Consider: does tooltip animate/update live as user scrolls? Should.
- Drum picker works well for small integer ranges; breaks down for large ranges

## Screen
A community content feed screen within a language-learning or conversation-practice app. This is a browse/discovery screen where users explore community-created conversation topics or rooms. Sits in the middle of a typical flow — post-onboarding, pre-session. Layout is a vertical scrolling card list with a segmented filter row at top and a persistent floating CTA at bottom.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in three clear horizontal bands: header/nav, filter chips, scrollable card list. Cards have generous internal padding (~16px) with consistent vertical gutters between them (~10–12px). The floating CTA button sits above the tab bar, overlapping the list slightly — creating a clear z-layer separation. Card content is left-aligned with the action (heart + count) pinned to the far right, creating a clean left-to-right scan path.

---

## Components
- **Segmented filter pills**: Three options (Hot, New, Top today). "Hot" is selected — white fill with dark text and icon, elevated with subtle shadow. Unselected pills are gray/muted. "Top today" includes a dropdown chevron, suggesting sub-filtering.
- **Content cards**: White rounded rectangles with emoji/illustration thumbnail on left, title + truncated description in center, heart icon + count on right. Cards with attributed content show a username handle above the title in smaller gray text.
- **Floating CTA button**: Rounded pill, bold blue fill, white text with a wand/magic icon. Persistent across scroll.
- **Header**: Large bold "Community" with a secondary muted "Topics" label — likely a tab toggle. History and favorites icons sit top-right.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Title text**: Bold, ~18–20pt, black — high contrast, dominant
- **Usernames**: Small, gray, ~11pt — clearly tertiary
- **Descriptions**: Regular weight, ~13pt, medium gray — supporting role
- **Heart counts**: Small, gray, right-aligned — unobtrusive but scannable
- **Color palette**: Near-white background (#F2F2F7 system gray), white cards, blue CTA (#3B5BDB-ish), muted gray for inactive states. Color is used almost exclusively functionally — blue = primary action, gray = inactive/secondary, white = content surface.

---

## Patterns
- **Dual-label header as implicit tab**: "Community" (bold/black) vs "Topics" (muted/gray) in the header suggests a tab toggle disguised as a title — avoids a full tab bar row while still offering navigation.
- **Attribution hierarchy in cards**: Some cards show a creator handle above the title, others don't — implying official/curated content has no attribution while user-generated content does. Subtle trust signal baked into layout.
- **Like count as social proof without engagement pressure**: Heart + number is read-only in list view — surfaces popularity without making the action feel mandatory at browse stage.
- **Floating CTA overlapping scroll content**: The "Create your own" button floats above the list but below the tab bar, creating a persistent nudge without consuming layout space. The slight overlap with scrolling content reinforces its floating nature.
- **Emoji/illustration thumbnails as content type signals**: Each topic uses a relevant emoji-style image (books, coffee cup, pencil, plate) — fast visual categorization without requiring text labels or category tags.
- **Truncated descriptions with ellipsis**: Consistent two-line truncation creates uniform card height while teasing enough content to drive taps.

---

## Notes
- Header-as-tab-toggle worth stealing — saves vertical space vs full tab row
- Attribution only on UGC cards = implicit quality tiering
- Floating pill CTA above tab bar = persistent but non-blocking creation nudge
- Emoji thumbnails = zero-cost visual taxonomy
- Heart count visible in list = social proof at browse, not just detail view
- "Top today" with chevron = progressive disclosure on filter complexity
- All cards same height via 2-line description clamp — uniformity without rigidity

## Screen
Fitness/workout app — content browse/library screen. Sits one level deep from a home or dashboard, accessed via back-navigable drill-down. Layout is a vertical scrolling list with a horizontal filter row at top. Primary interactions are filter chips and tappable workout cards.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct horizontal zones: nav bar, filter strip, card list. Cards use a left-anchored thumbnail with right-side text block — classic media list pattern. Generous card height (roughly 100–110px) gives thumbnails enough real estate to read as preview imagery. Consistent vertical gap between cards (~10–12px). Cards have visible rounded corners and subtle shadow/elevation, floating slightly off the background. Filter strip has no visible container — chips sit directly on the page background.

---

## Components

- **Filter chips (horizontal scroll):** Pill-shaped. Active state ("All") uses a soft lavender/periwinkle fill with bold text. Inactive states are plain text, no container — low visual weight until selected.
- **Workout cards:** White rounded-rect cards with drop shadow. Left side: square thumbnail image with rounded left corners matching card radius. Right side: stacked metadata.
- **Content type label:** Small running figure icon + "Workout" label in light gray — acts as a category/type badge inline with text.
- **Title text:** Bold, medium-large, black.
- **Duration text:** Small, gray, tertiary weight.
- **Back nav:** Simple chevron left, no label. Page title centered in all-caps spaced tracking.

---

## Typography & Color

- **Page title:** All-caps, wide letter-spacing, medium weight — formal/editorial feel
- **Card title:** ~17–18px, semibold/bold, near-black
- **Type label:** ~12px, gray, icon + text pairing
- **Duration:** ~13px, medium gray
- **Color palette:** Near-white background (#F5F5F5 range), white cards, black text, mid-gray secondary text, single accent — soft periwinkle/lavender for active filter state. Accent color is used *only* on the selected filter, making it highly intentional and scannable.

---

## Patterns

- **Ghost inactive filters:** Inactive filter options have zero visual container — just text. This reduces chrome noise and lets the selected state feel more pronounced without needing strong contrast on inactives. The selected chip "appears" rather than others "disappearing."
- **Thumbnail as mood signal, not information:** Images are cropped to show instructor/person rather than any text or graphic overlay. No duration badge on image, no play button overlay — keeps cards clean and trusts the text metadata.
- **Redundant content type label:** Every card says "Workout" with an icon — this seems redundant on a Workouts screen, but likely exists because this component is reused across contexts (classes, programs, challenges). Designing components for reuse rather than context-specific optimization.
- **No explicit CTA on cards:** No "Start," "Play," or arrow — the entire card is the tap target, reducing visual noise while keeping affordance clear through card elevation.
- **Duration as primary filter proxy:** Duration is the only metadata shown beyond title/type. No difficulty, instructor name, or equipment tags — deliberate constraint that keeps scanning fast.
- **Floating cards on tinted background:** White cards on off-white/light gray background creates subtle depth without heavy shadows. Low-effort hierarchy.

---

## Notes

- Active filter = filled pill, inactive = naked text — steal this for low-noise filter bars
- Running figure icon as content-type label = reusable component pattern, not screen-specific design
- No play button overlay on thumbnails = trust the card affordance
- All-caps spaced nav title feels editorial/premium — works for fitness/lifestyle
- Single accent color used *once* on screen = maximum impact for minimum palette
- Card shadow is very subtle — almost more "white card on gray bg" than true elevation
- Duration-only metadata = strong editorial restraint, forces content naming to do heavy lifting

## Screen
E-commerce checkout flow, Step 1 of 3 — delivery address entry. Sits at the beginning of a linear purchase funnel. Single-column scrollable form layout with a persistent step indicator at top. Primary interactions are text input fields, a dropdown selector, and a checkbox.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content flows top-down: security header → step progress → page title → form fields. Generous vertical spacing between each field group (~16–20px gaps) keeps the form breathable despite being data-dense. The title "Add your delivery address" uses extra top margin to create a clear section break from the nav. Fields are full-width, maximizing tap target size on mobile.

---

## Components
- **Step progress bar**: Three labeled text steps with an underline indicator. Active step is black/bold with a solid underline; upcoming steps are gray with a lighter underline. Simple but communicates position clearly without icons.
- **Text input fields**: Outlined with a light border, rounded corners, comfortable height. Validated fields show a green checkmark on the right — inline, not a separate message.
- **Checkbox (opt-out)**: Small square checkbox with descriptive copy inline. Notably framed as opt-out rather than opt-in.
- **Dropdown (Country/region)**: Full-width, matches input field styling, chevron icon right-aligned.
- **Security indicator**: Lock icon + "Secure checkout" text in the header — small, non-intrusive trust signal.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Hierarchy**: Bold large heading (~22px) for page title; medium-weight labels (~13px) above fields; regular body for input values; small gray text for helper copy.
- **Color palette**: Near-black for active text and labels, mid-gray for inactive steps and placeholder text, green (#4CAF50-ish) for validation checkmarks, white backgrounds throughout.
- **Color function**: Green is used exclusively for validation — no other decorative use, so it carries clear signal weight. Gray creates a visual hierarchy between completed/pending steps.

---

## Patterns
- **Inline real-time validation**: Checkmarks appear inside the field on the right as each field is successfully completed — reduces cognitive load, gives immediate positive feedback without disrupting flow.
- **Opt-out consent framing**: The marketing checkbox is pre-unchecked but framed as "tick to opt-out" — an unusual dark pattern worth noting; the copy implies ticking means you *don't* want updates, inverting typical checkbox logic.
- **Predictive address search**: The last visible field uses "Start typing to search your address" — autocomplete pattern that reduces manual entry friction significantly.
- **Step labels with progress underlines**: Instead of circles or numbered bubbles, underline bars beneath text labels are minimal and typographically clean — works well in a luxury/editorial context.
- **Security trust signal placement**: Lock icon placed in the top nav bar rather than near the CTA button — establishes trust before the user even begins filling the form.

---

## Notes
- Inline ✓ validation > toast/error messages for form UX
- Opt-out checkbox copy = dark pattern, worth flagging in audits
- Step indicator using text + underline bar = elegant alternative to bubble steppers
- Address autocomplete as last field = smart progressive disclosure (country first, then search)
- Lock icon in header = trust-building before commitment, not just at CTA
- Full-width inputs on mobile = always, no excuses
- Gray step labels for future steps = low visual noise, keeps focus on current task

## Screen
Account creation / registration screen for a financial/crypto app. This is step 1 of a multi-step onboarding flow. Layout is a single-column form with a header section, progress indicator, and three stacked input fields. Keyboard is active, focused on the first field.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content flows top-to-bottom: nav row → headline block → form fields. Generous vertical spacing between the incentive copy and the first field (~24px gap). Field labels sit tight above their inputs (~4–6px), creating clear label-input pairing. Fields are full-width with rounded corners and consistent height. The incentive block uses a two-line hierarchy with a "See terms" link below — all left-aligned, matching the form alignment.

---

## Components
- **Progress indicator (top center):** Three pill/dash segments; first is filled blue, others are light gray. Thin and minimal — doesn't dominate the header.
- **Close/X button (top left):** Plain icon, no container — low visual weight, keeps escape hatch available without competing with content.
- **Input fields:** Outlined style with rounded rectangles. Active field has a vivid blue border; inactive fields use a subtle gray border. Labels are bold and positioned outside/above the field.
- **Active field label:** Turns blue to match the active border — reinforces focus state at the label level, not just the border.
- **"See terms" link:** Blue, inline, small — subordinate but accessible.

---

## Typography & Color
- **Headline:** Large, bold, black — clear primary hierarchy
- **Incentive subhead ("Get up to $200"):** Bold, slightly smaller — secondary emphasis
- **Body copy:** Regular weight, gray — tertiary
- **Link text:** Blue, same size as body — functional color use
- **Active label:** Blue bold — mirrors field focus state
- **Inactive labels:** Black bold — consistent weight across states, only color changes
- **Palette:** White background, near-black text, mid-gray for inactive strokes and placeholder text, single brand blue for all interactive/active states

---

## Patterns
- **Incentive copy embedded in form header:** Unusual placement — the reward hook ("Get up to $200") lives inside the form step itself, not just on a splash screen. Reduces drop-off at the friction point of data entry.
- **Label color mirrors field focus state:** When a field is active, its label turns blue — the focus indicator spans both label and border, creating a larger visual "active zone" that's easier to track.
- **Progress indicator as dots/dashes, not numbered steps:** Avoids communicating total step count explicitly, which can discourage users from starting if the number feels high.
- **Keyboard "next" key visible:** The keyboard is shown with "next" as the return key — signals the designer considered keyboard flow explicitly, guiding users through fields without tapping.
- **No submit button visible:** CTA is deferred — the keyboard's "next" key handles progression, keeping the screen uncluttered while keyboard is open.

---

## Notes
- Blue label + blue border on focus = bigger active zone, try this
- Incentive hook in form header, not just onboarding splash — retention tactic mid-funnel
- Dash-style progress bar hides total step count intentionally
- No CTA button while keyboard open — "next" key does the job
- Label weight stays bold in both states; only hue changes (black → blue)
- "See terms" as inline link under incentive = compliance without modal interruption

## Screen
Social media app login screen. Entry point to the authentication flow. Classic single-column layout with logo at top, form fields in the middle third, and account creation anchored to the bottom. Minimal — no imagery, no marketing copy.

## Layout & Spacing
Vertical rhythm divided into three clear zones: logo zone (top ~40%), form zone (middle), registration zone (bottom). Generous empty space between logo and form creates breathing room and draws focus downward. The "Create new account" button is visually separated from the login cluster by a large gap — intentional hierarchy that deprioritizes sign-up without hiding it. Form fields are flush-stacked with tight spacing between them, grouping them as a unit.

## Components
- **Input fields**: Large pill-adjacent rounded rectangles, white fill, no visible border stroke — rely on subtle shadow/contrast against background. Placeholder text in light gray.
- **Primary CTA (Log in)**: Full-width, deeply rounded pill shape, solid blue fill, white bold label. High contrast, dominant.
- **Secondary text link (Forgot password?)**: No button chrome, plain dark text, centered. Minimal visual weight.
- **Secondary CTA (Create new account)**: Full-width pill, white fill, blue border stroke, blue label text — outlined ghost variant. Clearly subordinate to primary.
- **Logo**: Rounded square icon, gradient fill (warm orange-to-purple), centered with significant top padding.
- **Parent brand attribution**: Small icon + text at very bottom, low contrast gray. Purely informational.

## Typography & Color
- Type hierarchy: Logo (graphic) → Field labels (medium gray, regular weight) → Button label (white, semibold) → Forgot password (dark, regular) → Create account (blue, medium weight) → Attribution (small, gray)
- Background: Soft lavender-to-white gradient, nearly imperceptible — adds warmth without distraction
- Primary action color: Strong royal blue — used only on the primary button fill and secondary button's outline/text, creating visual consistency
- No black text; darkest elements are near-black, keeping the screen feeling light

## Patterns
- **Intentional CTA weight gradient**: Three levels of button prominence (filled → text link → outlined) map perfectly to three levels of user intent (login → recovery → new user). Teaches hierarchy through form.
- **Flexible identifier field**: Single field accepting username, email, or phone number reduces friction — no need to decide which field to use. Label text communicates all three options inline.
- **Spatial separation as priority signal**: Large whitespace gap before "Create new account" acts as a soft divider without using a line or rule. The secondary action feels like a different section.
- **Background gradient as brand echo**: The muted lavender gradient subtly mirrors the app's colorful identity without competing with content.
- **Ghost button for secondary action**: Outlined button gives the sign-up path enough presence to be found but never competes with the primary flow.
- **Parent attribution as trust signal**: Placing the parent company mark at the bottom functions as a quiet credibility cue for hesitant users.

## Notes
- Ghost button + filled button pairing = clean way to offer two paths without visual noise
- Flexible identifier input = reduce cognitive load, don't force users to categorize themselves
- Huge logo-to-form whitespace = confidence move, resists urge to fill space
- Background gradient: almost nothing, but does a lot for warmth
- Forgot password as plain text link = right call, any button treatment would over-elevate it
- Three-zone vertical layout (brand / action / acquisition) reusable pattern for any auth screen

## Screen
Phone number entry screen for a mobile app's onboarding/registration flow. This is the first step of authentication — user enters phone number to receive an OTP. Layout splits into two clear zones: the form content area (top ~55%) and the native numeric keyboard (bottom ~45%). Primary interactive elements are the country code selector, phone number input field, clear button, "already have account" link, and sign up CTA button.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is left-aligned with generous top padding for the headline. The phone input row uses a two-part split: a compact country selector pill on the left and a wider text input taking remaining width. Substantial whitespace between the input row and the login link, then a large gap before the CTA — this breathing room prevents the form from feeling cramped. The CTA button sits just above the keyboard, anchored to the transition zone between content and keyboard.

## Components
- **Back arrow**: minimal, top-left, no container
- **Country code selector**: rounded pill with flag emoji + dial code, lighter background, visually distinct from input field
- **Phone input field**: rounded rectangle, light gray fill, active state with populated number, clear (×) button inside trailing edge
- **Clear button**: small circular gray icon inside field, appears only when field has content
- **Login link**: inline text link in blue, no underline, sentence-style phrasing ("Already have an account? Log in")
- **Sign up CTA**: full-width, heavily rounded (pill shape), solid blue fill, white label, strong visual weight
- **Native keyboard**: system numeric keypad, white keys on light gray background

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: large, bold/black weight, high contrast black — dominates hierarchy immediately
- **Subtitle**: small, regular weight, medium gray — supporting role
- **Input text**: medium weight, dark — readable against gray field
- **Link text**: blue, matches CTA button color — creates visual consistency in interactive elements
- **CTA label**: white on blue, medium weight

Color palette: near-white background, blue (#3B5BDB-ish) as the single accent used for both the primary action and the text link, gray for input fields and secondary elements. Clean two-tone functional palette.

## Patterns
- **Split country/number input**: separating dial code into its own tappable component rather than a dropdown or inline prefix — makes country switching feel like a first-class action
- **CTA placement above keyboard**: the Sign Up button stays visible and reachable even with keyboard open — it doesn't get buried; the layout anticipates keyboard state
- **Inline clear button**: appears contextually inside the field rather than outside, keeping the row clean until needed
- **Login link as escape hatch**: placed close to the input, not in the header or footer — positioned where a returning user would naturally pause after seeing the form
- **Whitespace as pacing**: the large empty space between input and CTA creates a visual "breath" that makes the single-field form feel calm rather than sparse
- **Keyboard zone as visual separator**: the gray keyboard background creates a natural content/action division without needing a divider line

## Notes
- CTA above keyboard = must design for keyboard-up state explicitly, not as afterthought
- Country selector as separate pill = feels more intentional than inline prefix text
- One accent color doing double duty (link + button) = tight, trustworthy palette
- Empty space between field and button isn't wasted — it's pacing
- "Let's get started!" tone = warm, not clinical — copy does UX work
- Inline × clear button: only show when field populated — reduces visual noise at rest state
- Consider: does the button stay fixed above keyboard or scroll with content?

## Screen
A status-selection bottom sheet modal in a health or fitness tracking app. Appears mid-flow — triggered from a user profile screen (visible dimmed behind). The sheet presents mutually exclusive status options for a user to self-report their current physical state. Layout is a vertically stacked list of radio options inside a floating card with a fixed CTA at the bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Bottom sheet takes roughly 70% of screen height, leaving the profile context visible above. Content uses generous internal padding (~20–24px sides). Option rows are evenly spaced with consistent gaps (~10px) between cards. The "Done" button sits in its own visual zone, separated from the list with clear breathing room — feels anchored rather than floating.

## Components
- **Bottom sheet card** — white, large top corner radius (~24px), no visible handle/drag indicator; modal feel rather than drawer
- **Status option rows** — pill-shaped cards with light gray background; contain a leading icon, title + subtitle text block, and trailing radio indicator
- **Selected state row** — warm peach/salmon background fill, icon and title shift to brand orange, radio dot fills solid orange
- **Unselected radio** — empty circle, light gray border
- **Leading icons** — monochrome, contextually matched (walking figure, sunrise, thermometer, bandage); sized consistently ~22px
- **Primary CTA button** — full-width, rounded pill, solid terracotta/burnt orange, white label

## Typography & Color
- Sheet title: large bold (~22px), near-black, centered
- Row title: medium weight (~16px), dark near-black when unselected, brand orange when selected
- Row subtitle: small (~13px), medium gray, descriptive one-liner
- **Palette:** Warm white background, terracotta/burnt orange (#C8573A-ish) as primary accent, peach tint for selected row fill, mid-gray for unselected row backgrounds and icons

Color is used functionally: orange = selected/active state, gray = neutral/inactive, peach background reinforces selection without relying solely on the radio indicator.

## Patterns
- **Contextual dimming with identity persistence** — the profile (avatar + name) remains visible but dimmed behind the sheet, reinforcing *who* the status is being set for without repeating it inside the modal
- **Full-row selection affordance** — the entire card changes color on selection, not just the radio button; reduces reliance on small touch targets
- **Subtitle as micro-copy reassurance** — each option has a short, empathetic description ("Needing rest to get well") that reduces ambiguity and adds warmth to a clinical choice
- **Icon as semantic reinforcement** — icons aren't decorative; they provide a second visual channel for quick scanning alongside text
- **Warm color palette for health context** — avoids clinical blues/greens; terracotta palette feels personal and low-anxiety for a sensitive self-reporting task
- **Mutually exclusive options as cards vs. standard radio list** — elevating radio choices to full card components increases perceived importance of the selection

## Notes
- Selected row = bg tint + label color change + filled radio = triple confirmation of state, very clear
- Peach fill is subtle enough not to feel alarming for "Active" — color choice matters for each status (would sick/injured use different tints?)
- No drag handle on sheet — intentional? Suggests non-dismissible until explicit action
- "Close" text button top-right of background screen still visible — escape hatch without cluttering the sheet
- Empathetic microcopy pattern worth stealing for any self-reporting or check-in flow
- CTA label "Done" not "Save" — feels lighter, less transactional

## Screen
Subscription plan selection screen from a consumer app (likely audio/media). Modal or full-screen overlay presented during onboarding or upgrade flow. Layout is single-column, top-to-bottom: headline → trust banner → billing toggle → plan cards → CTA → legal text.

## Layout & Spacing
Generous vertical rhythm throughout. Trust banner sits in its own contained block between headline and toggle, creating a clear pre-selection moment before the user engages with pricing. Plan cards stack vertically with consistent internal padding. Legal/fine-print is pushed well below the CTA, reducing friction at the decision point without hiding it. Left-aligned headline feels editorial rather than centered/formal.

## Components
- **Trust banner**: Rounded rectangle with light lavender fill, bell icon in a circular gray container, bold headline + supporting body text. Soft, non-alarming treatment for what is essentially a billing warning.
- **Billing toggle**: Pill-shaped segmented control. Selected state = black fill with white text; unselected = transparent with gray text. Discount callout ("-25%") rendered in orange/amber directly on the unselected tab — nudges toward annual without forcing it.
- **Plan cards**: Outlined cards with radio button on left, plan name in bold uppercase, account count badge top-right, pricing info below. Selected card gets a purple border + filled purple radio. Unselected card is neutral gray border. Active card has a decorative abstract purple shape bleeding into the bottom-right corner — adds personality without cluttering info.
- **Account count badge**: Small pill tag (dark on selected, light gray on unselected) — quick scannable differentiator.
- **CTA button**: Full-width, black, high-contrast, rounded. "Try for free" — commitment-reducing language.

## Typography & Color
Strong type hierarchy: oversized bold serif-style display for the headline ("Our plans"), bold condensed uppercase for plan names, medium weight for pricing, small regular for body/legal. Color palette is minimal — off-white background, near-black text, purple as the single accent (selection states, decorative element), amber/orange used exclusively for the discount percentage. Color is used functionally: purple = selected/active, orange = savings signal, black = primary action.

## Patterns
- **Objection handling before pricing**: The trial reminder banner appears *before* the plan cards, preemptively neutralizing the "I'll forget to cancel" anxiety. Sequencing this above the price is a deliberate trust-building move.
- **Discount nudge on unselected state**: Placing "-25%" inside the non-active toggle tab is a passive upsell — visible at all times without modal interruption or aggressive highlighting.
- **Decorative illustration as selection feedback**: The purple abstract shape inside the active card adds delight and reinforces selection state beyond just the border/radio change. Doubles as brand personality injection.
- **Commitment-reducing CTA copy**: "Try for free" instead of "Subscribe" or "Start plan" reduces perceived risk at the final tap.
- **Badge as plan differentiator**: Account count rendered as a small badge (not body text) allows instant plan comparison at a glance without reading full descriptions.

## Notes
- Trust copy *above* pricing = smart anxiety sequencing
- Discount % lives on the unselected tab — always visible, never pushy
- Decorative blob inside selected card = cheap delight, high ROI
- Orange used *only* for savings signal — single-purpose accent color
- Radio + border + illustration = three layers of selection feedback
- "Try for free" > "Subscribe" — always soften the commitment verb
- Legal below fold but still present — compliance without friction
- Bell icon in soft circle = non-threatening way to surface billing reminder

## Screen
Onboarding personalization screen from a personal finance app. User selects their primary goals/interests before entering the main app experience. Sits early in the onboarding flow — post-signup, pre-dashboard. Layout is: header copy at top, 2×3 grid of selectable goal cards in the middle, fixed CTA button at bottom.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is divided into three clear zones: header, selection grid, action. The 2×3 card grid uses consistent gutters (~12px) with generous internal padding. Cards are square-ish with rounded corners, giving a tile/app-icon feel. The overall screen background is a very light gray-blue, slightly offset from the white cards, creating subtle depth without shadows. Bottom CTA floats with enough breathing room above the home indicator.

## Components
- **Goal selection cards** — White rounded-rect cards with a centered app-icon-style illustration (rounded square color background + white glyph) above a short label. No visible checkbox or selection indicator shown in current state — selection likely triggers a highlighted border or fill state.
- **Illustration icons** — Each uses a distinct pastel color background (blue, coral/red, teal, light gray, pink, warm yellow). Icons are soft, rounded, friendly. Desaturated/grayed-out icons on "Pay off debt," "Share with partner," and "Sync between devices" suggest either unselected state or secondary priority options.
- **Continue CTA** — Full-width pill button, near-black fill, white label. High contrast, confident. Pill shape (fully rounded) differentiates it from the rectangular cards.
- **Header text block** — Large bold question headline + smaller regular-weight subtext explanation.

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: Large, heavy/black weight, dark near-black. Conversational question format ("How can we help?") — approachable tone.
- **Subtext**: Small, regular weight, medium gray. Explains the purpose of the selection.
- **Card labels**: Medium weight, dark, centered below icon. Two-line labels (e.g., "Track my spending") handled gracefully with center alignment.
- **Color palette**: Off-white/light lavender-gray background. Card icons use blue, coral, teal, gray, pink, amber — each unique per option. Grayed-out icons use desaturated versions, visually communicating lower engagement or unselected state. CTA is near-black — strong contrast anchor at bottom.

## Patterns
- **Intentional desaturation as state communication** — Three of the six cards have noticeably muted/gray icons vs. the vivid color of the top three. This could signal "not yet selected" vs. "selected" OR a visual hierarchy suggesting primary vs. secondary use cases. Either way, it's doing work without explicit labels.
- **App-icon metaphor for feature selection** — Using rounded-square icon tiles mimics the mental model of choosing apps on a home screen — immediately familiar, reduces cognitive load for what could feel like a form.
- **Conversational framing of a data collection step** — "How can we help?" reframes what is essentially a preference survey into a service offer. Subtext reinforces personalization benefit ("customise the app for your needs").
- **No explicit multi-select UI chrome** — No checkboxes, no radio buttons. The selection mechanic is implied through the card tap interaction, keeping the UI clean. Trust that color/border change on tap communicates selection.
- **Passive CTA until selections made** — "Continue" button is present but presumably activates only after selection — the always-visible button reduces anxiety about being "stuck."

## Notes
- Desaturated icons = elegant unselected state, no need for checkboxes
- App-icon grid = feature selection that feels like curation not a form
- Conversational H1 on data-collection screens → reframe as "helping you" not "interrogating you"
- Pastel icon backgrounds: each option gets a unique color = easier to remember / scan
- Near-black pill CTA as strong visual anchor at bottom of content-heavy screen
- Light bg offset from white cards = depth without shadows, very clean
- Consider: does graying out some options prime users toward certain choices? Intentional nudge?

## Screen
Streaming app search/browse screen. Sits at the top of the search flow before any query is entered — the "pre-search" state that doubles as a content discovery surface. Two-column grid of genre/category tiles fills the primary content area below a search bar. Large title header with avatar top-right.

## Layout & Spacing
Large display title anchors the top-left with generous top padding. Search bar sits directly beneath, full-width with consistent horizontal margins (~16px). "Browse" section header follows with tighter spacing, then a 2-column grid with uniform gutters (~8px) between tiles. Grid tiles use a consistent aspect ratio (roughly 16:9 landscape). Content bleeds toward the bottom edge, signaling scrollability.

## Components
- **Large title header**: Bold, oversized — iOS large title pattern, left-aligned
- **Avatar/profile button**: Circular, initials-based, top-right — subtle gray fill, no border
- **Search bar**: Rounded rectangle, light gray fill, left magnifier icon, right microphone icon — inactive/placeholder state
- **Browse section label**: Medium-weight label, left-aligned, acts as a section divider without a line
- **Category tiles**: Rounded corners (~12px), full-bleed imagery with text overlaid — some use solid color backgrounds with logos/text, others use photographic content with bottom-left label overlay. White text on imagery uses implicit shadow/contrast rather than explicit scrim in some tiles; others rely on dark background imagery naturally.

## Typography & Color
- Title: Heavy weight, ~34pt, black
- Section header ("Browse"): Semibold, ~17–20pt, black
- Tile labels: Medium/semibold white, ~14–16pt, bottom-left anchored
- Search placeholder: Gray, regular weight
- Overall palette: White background, black text, gray UI chrome. Tiles introduce full color via imagery. Active nav tab uses blue as the sole accent color — all other UI is achromatic.

## Patterns
- **Pre-search discovery grid**: Rather than an empty or minimal pre-search state, the screen becomes a full browsing surface — search intent is captured while passive browsing is enabled simultaneously
- **Mixed tile treatment**: Some tiles are brand/logo-forward (solid black bg + logo), others are mood/genre-forward (cinematic photography + text label) — creates visual rhythm and hierarchy within a uniform grid
- **Text-on-image without explicit scrim**: Genre tiles rely on naturally dark or high-contrast photography rather than adding a gradient overlay — keeps imagery clean while maintaining legibility
- **Microphone affordance in search bar**: Voice input surfaced immediately in the inactive state, not hidden behind interaction
- **Initials avatar as profile entry point**: Compact, no label needed — relies on position convention (top-right) for discoverability
- **Aspect ratio consistency creating implied rhythm**: All tiles share the same ratio, so varied visual content (logos vs. photos vs. illustrated) feels cohesive

## Notes
- Pre-search = browse mode — don't waste the empty state
- 2-col grid w/ 16:9 tiles = cinematic feel even in navigation
- Mix logo tiles + photo tiles for grid visual variety without breaking layout
- Achromatic chrome → color lives entirely in content
- Avatar initials top-right = profile without nav real estate
- Mic icon in dormant search bar = voice always one tap away
- Bottom-left text anchoring on tiles feels editorial, not UI-generic
- No scrims = trust your content photography to do the contrast work

## Screen
A processing/loading interstitial screen from a photo management or storage app. Sits between a user-initiated action (granting photo access or triggering a scan) and results display. Minimal single-purpose screen — purely communicative, no interaction required.

## Layout & Spacing
Vertically centered composition with the spinner and text grouped as a tight unit, positioned slightly above true center (optical centering). Generous empty space above and below creates calm, unhurried feeling. Text block sits ~16–20px below the spinner. Two-line text stack with tight line spacing between heading and subtext.

## Components
- **Spinner/loader**: Custom circular progress indicator, not a system default. Thin stroke weight (~3px). Gradient fill from solid green to fully transparent, creating a "fading tail" effect — elegant alternative to a solid ring
- **Heading text**: Bold, dark charcoal (~#1a1a1a), ~20–22px
- **Subtext**: Regular weight, medium gray (~#999), ~15–16px, centered
- **Background**: Pure or near-pure white (#ffffff or #fafafa)

## Typography & Color
Two-level hierarchy: bold heading communicates the active process, lighter gray subtext manages expectations without competing. No decorative type — purely functional. Color palette is intentionally minimal: white background, near-black text, single green accent. The green is soft/muted (desaturated, slightly warm) rather than vivid — reduces anxiety, feels gentle.

## Patterns
- **Gradient spinner tail**: The loader uses a transparency fade on the trailing edge rather than a solid arc — communicates motion direction and feels more refined than standard spinners. Worth replicating in custom loaders
- **Expectation-setting microcopy**: "This may take a few seconds" is a small but deliberate anxiety-reducer — preemptively addresses user concern about wait time without being apologetic
- **Color restraint as trust signal**: Using only one accent color (green) on a white field signals cleanliness and focus — common in health/utility apps where trust matters
- **Deliberate absence of progress percentage or bar**: No quantified progress shown — avoids the problem of inaccurate estimates and keeps the screen calm. Works when process duration is genuinely unknown
- **System chrome suppression**: No navigation bar, no back button — user is locked into the flow intentionally, preventing interruption mid-process

## Notes
- Fading-tail spinner > solid spinner for polish, easy to build with conic-gradient or stroke-dasharray + opacity mask
- "May take a few seconds" = set expectations early, don't apologize
- Optical centering slightly above midpoint feels more balanced than mathematical center on tall screens
- Muted green for loading = calm/safe, not urgent — color mood matters on wait screens
- Negative space is doing heavy lifting here — resist filling it
- No cancel button = intentional commitment gate

## Screen
A processing/loading interstitial screen from a financial or lending app, shown after a user submits an application. Sits between form submission and results — a "please wait" moment in an application flow. Single-column, vertically centered layout with minimal chrome. Primary interactive element is only the Cancel text link in the top-right corner.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is vertically centered slightly above true center, giving the illustration and text a natural resting point. Generous white space above and below the content cluster creates calm and reduces anxiety during a wait state. The loading indicator sits well below the text block, giving it breathing room as a secondary element. Top-right Cancel placement is intentionally low-key — accessible but not prominent.

## Components
- **3D Hourglass Illustration** — Rendered with soft shadows and a purple accent color on the rims. Feels tactile and premium, not a flat icon. Communicates "time passing" without a spinning loader.
- **Cancel Text Link** — Purple, top-right, no button container. Minimal visual weight intentional — present but not encouraged.
- **Animated Dot Loader** — Single small purple dot (appears to be part of a dot-pulse or dot-traveling animation). Extremely subtle; keeps the screen alive without being distracting.
- **Headline** — Bold, dark, no container or card.
- **Subtext** — Gray, centered, two lines max.

## Typography & Color
- **Hierarchy**: Bold ~22pt headline → Regular ~15pt gray subtext — clean two-level system, nothing else competing.
- **Color palette**: Near-white background (#F8F8F8 or similar), near-black headline, medium gray body copy, purple accent (~#7B5CF0 range) used exclusively for interactive elements (Cancel) and brand touches (hourglass rims, dot).
- Purple is used sparingly and functionally — it marks "things you can interact with" and "brand identity" simultaneously.

## Patterns
- **Anxiety reduction through restraint** — No progress bar, no percentage, no estimated time. Removing quantified uncertainty prevents users from fixating on progress. The copy "just a little more to go..." is conversational and reassuring without making a promise.
- **3D illustration as emotional labor** — The hourglass replaces a generic spinner with something that communicates patience and craft. It signals the wait is expected and normal, not an error state.
- **Escape hatch without promotion** — Cancel is available but styled to be nearly invisible. Users who need it can find it; users who are committed won't be tempted by it. No modal, no confirmation — just a quiet affordance.
- **Single animated element** — One dot animating keeps the screen from feeling frozen without creating visual noise that raises anxiety. The animation is peripheral, not focal.
- **Copy tone shift** — Using "..." ellipsis in the subtext mimics natural speech hesitation, making the wait feel human and in-progress rather than robotic.

## Notes
- 3D emoji-style illustration > flat icon for emotional loading states
- One-dot loader = "alive but calm" — steal this
- Cancel as naked text link, top-right, low contrast = soft escape hatch pattern
- White space IS the design here — resist filling it
- Conversational copy with ellipsis makes async feel synchronous
- Purple reserved only for brand + interactive = strong functional color discipline
- Vertically center slightly high, not true center — feels more intentional

## Screen
A loading/sync progress screen from a personal finance app. This appears mid-flow during a data refresh or initial account sync. The screen is a full-page interstitial that communicates background processing status. Layout is vertically centered with a sequential task list and a spinner below it.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is vertically centered in the lower-middle portion of the screen, leaving significant white space above — creates a calm, uncluttered feel during what could be an anxious wait. The task list items are evenly spaced with generous line height. The spinner sits below the list with clear separation, acting as a "still working" signal distinct from the completed items.

## Components
- **Step/task list items**: Each row pairs a circular status icon with a label. Completed items use a filled green circle with white checkmark. Active item uses a darker, more saturated green with bold text. Pending items use a light gray circle (empty) with gray text.
- **Spinner**: Minimal arc-style circular loader in light blue-gray, positioned below the list. Subtle and non-intrusive.
- **Fade/blur treatment**: Top and bottom list items are visually faded/blurred, implying a scrolling window or receding context — only the current and adjacent steps are in focus.

## Typography & Color
- Type hierarchy: Active step uses bold black (~16px), completed steps use regular weight medium gray, pending steps use light gray — weight and color together signal state.
- Color palette: White background, sage/medium green for completed states, darker green for active state, light gray for pending, near-black for active label text.
- Color is doing all the state communication — no additional labels or badges needed.

## Patterns
- **Progressive disclosure via opacity/blur**: Steps above and below the visible window fade out, creating a "film strip" or carousel-of-tasks metaphor. This implies continuity without showing everything at once — reduces cognitive load.
- **Typographic weight as state indicator**: The active step is the only bold item — weight change alone would communicate state even without color.
- **Dual loading signals**: The checkmark list tells *what's done*, the spinner tells *something is still happening* — separating completion feedback from in-progress feedback cleanly.
- **Calm loading UX**: Rather than a single spinner with no context, breaking work into named steps makes wait time feel productive and transparent. Users understand what the system is doing.
- **Size differentiation on active icon**: The active green circle appears slightly larger/more saturated than completed ones — subtle visual anchoring to the current moment.

## Notes
- Faded top/bottom rows = "you are here" in a sequence without a progress bar
- Bold weight = active state, no extra UI needed
- Spinner separated from list = "done items" vs "still going" — don't conflate these
- Named tasks during load = trust-building pattern for finance/sync flows
- All-white bg + generous space = loading doesn't feel like waiting
- Try: animate items upward as each completes, like a ticker

## Screen
Onboarding completion / personalization loading screen from a wellness or lifestyle app. Sits at the end of an onboarding questionnaire flow, bridging user input to their customized home experience. Full-bleed illustrated layout with minimal UI chrome — essentially a transitional holding screen.

## Layout & Spacing
Single vertical column, center-weighted. The spinner sits at true optical center of the screen. Progress bar and label text are anchored in the lower-middle third, creating a clear visual separation between "ambient atmosphere" (top) and "functional feedback" (bottom). The illustrated landscape bleeds into the very bottom edge with no padding, reinforcing immersion. Generous empty space above and below the spinner keeps focus calm rather than busy.

## Components
- **Spinner**: Small, subtle — uses a muted gray/white tone rather than a high-contrast color, keeping it unobtrusive against the dark gradient
- **Progress bar**: Pill-shaped, full width with generous horizontal padding. Track is semi-transparent white/gray; fill is solid white. No percentage label — intentionally vague to reduce impatience
- **Status label**: Single line of body text directly below the progress bar, left-aligned to the bar's start edge
- **Illustrated scene**: Layered flat illustration — rolling hills in teal/dark green, large sun in terracotta/burnt orange, all sitting at the bottom. Decorative, not interactive

## Typography & Color
Single text style — medium weight, white, ~16pt body. No headline present; the loading state IS the message. Color palette: deep indigo at top transitioning through purple-violet to deep maroon/burgundy at bottom. The gradient itself tells a story (dusk/dawn). Illustration palette — terracotta sun, muted teal hills — pulls warm and cool tones from the gradient, making it feel cohesive rather than pasted on.

## Patterns
- **Narrative loading**: "Finalizing your personalized journey" reframes waiting as something being done *for* the user, not a technical delay — emotionally recontextualizes friction
- **Atmospheric gradient as mood-setting**: The gradient isn't just background fill — it mirrors the app's thematic territory (mindfulness, nature, cycles) before any content is shown
- **Illustration anchored at bottom**: Landscape scene grounds the screen visually and creates a horizon metaphor — subtle but reinforces "journey" language in the copy
- **Intentionally vague progress**: No percentage, no ETA — reduces the user's ability to fixate on time remaining, keeping them in a passive, receptive state
- **Spinner + bar redundancy**: Two loading indicators used simultaneously — spinner signals active processing, bar signals overall completion arc. Together they feel more reassuring than either alone
- **Negative space as calm**: Massive empty gradient above the interaction zone — resists the urge to fill space, which itself communicates the app's tone

## Notes
- Loading screen as brand moment — don't waste it on a spinner alone
- Gradient storytelling: color can carry thematic meaning before a single word loads
- "Journey" framing on loading copy — steal this pattern for any personalization flow
- Bottom-anchored illustration = horizon line = forward motion metaphor, very reusable
- Two-indicator loading (spinner + bar) = perceived reliability boost
- Vague progress bar intentional — consider when NOT showing % is better UX
- Full bleed scene with no safe-area padding = immersive, works well for transitional screens

## Screen
AI generation loading state within a dream/sleep journaling or dream interpretation app. This screen appears mid-flow after a user has submitted content for AI processing. Minimal single-focus layout — centered loading indicator with label, navigation bar at top, nothing else competing for attention.

## Layout & Spacing
Full-bleed dark background with a single focal point centered slightly above vertical midpoint (optical center). The loading element and label are vertically stacked with generous breathing room on all sides. The sparse layout is intentional — empty space reinforces the "waiting" state and prevents the user from feeling they should be doing anything else.

## Components
- **Navigation bar**: Back chevron left-aligned, centered title "Dreams" in medium weight white — minimal, no right action
- **Circular progress indicator**: Custom-styled ring (not system default) with a gradient/shimmer arc suggesting motion; partially filled indicating progress in motion
- **Central icon container**: Circular filled button in medium purple with sparkle/stars emoji-style icon — acts as the visual identity anchor for the AI feature
- **Ambient glow**: Radial purple glow emanating behind the circular element — not a component per se but a key atmospheric layer
- **Status label**: Single line of muted/desaturated text below the loader — "Generating Dreams with AI..."
- **Home indicator**: Standard system bar at bottom

## Typography & Color
- Title: White, semibold, ~17pt — standard nav weight
- Status text: Muted lavender-gray, ~15pt regular — intentionally low contrast to feel secondary and non-urgent
- **Palette**: Deep navy-to-dark-purple gradient background, medium purple (#6B3FA0 range) for icon fill, warm silver/pearl for the progress ring arc, soft purple radial glow as the only light source
- Color is used atmospherically — the glow creates depth and makes the screen feel alive without animation being visible in static form

## Patterns
- **Ambient glow as loading feedback**: Instead of a standard spinner, the purple radial bloom behind the icon implies energy/processing happening — emotional loading state rather than mechanical
- **AI identity icon**: The sparkle stars icon inside the loader doubles as brand shorthand for "AI feature" — consistent iconography trains users to associate this symbol with AI actions throughout the app
- **Custom progress ring over system spinner**: Replacing the default activity indicator with a styled circular arc keeps the visual language cohesive and premium; the pearl/gradient treatment feels intentional
- **Atmospheric waiting**: The screen uses darkness and negative space as a design element — the emptiness mirrors the dreamlike, liminal quality of the subject matter; form follows content
- **Low-contrast status copy**: The muted label text reduces anxiety during wait states — it informs without alarming or demanding attention
- **Vertical optical centering**: The loader sits slightly above true center, which reads as more balanced on mobile screens during passive viewing

## Notes
- Glow-behind-icon = instant "magic/AI" signal without words
- Muted status text = calm waiting, not urgent feedback
- Custom ring > system spinner for premium feel
- Empty screen = intentional patience UX, not lazy design
- Sparkle icon as persistent AI affordance marker across app
- Dark + purple = dreamy/nocturnal theming baked into loading state
- Try: animated glow pulse synced to ring rotation for next level
- Emotional loading states > mechanical ones — match content domain

## Screen
Loading/transition screen within a language learning app. Sits between lesson initiation and lesson content delivery. Full-screen white canvas with a centered mascot character and minimal chrome. Back navigation and progress bar anchored at top.

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct vertical zones: top navigation strip (back arrow + progress bar), large empty white void (roughly 40% of screen height), then centered mascot + label cluster in the lower-middle third. The deliberate emptiness above the character creates a stage-like focus. Progress bar spans nearly full width with generous side padding. "LOADING..." label sits tight below the character with minimal gap.

## Components
- **Progress bar**: Pill-shaped track in light gray, filled segment in brand green — rounded caps, low height (~8px), very minimal
- **Back arrow**: Simple left chevron in muted gray, low visual weight
- **Mascot illustration**: Fully rendered character with expressive eyes, slight lean/dance pose, musical notes floating above — implies animation in live state
- **Loading label**: All-caps spaced text in light gray, no spinner or traditional loading indicator

## Typography & Color
Single text element: "LOADING..." in all-caps, wide letter-spacing, light gray — reads as secondary/passive information. No headline hierarchy. Color palette is extremely restrained: white background, brand green (progress fill, mascot body), warm orange (mascot feet/beak), light gray (track, text, arrow). Color is used sparingly so the green mascot becomes the sole focal point.

## Patterns
- **Mascot-as-loading-state**: Replaces generic spinner entirely with animated character — loading becomes a brand moment rather than friction
- **Implied animation over static indicator**: Musical notes suggest the character is dancing/moving, making the wait feel alive even in static screenshot
- **Progress bar during loading**: Showing lesson progress bar even on the loading screen maintains continuity — user knows where they are in the lesson arc before content appears
- **Emotional reframing of wait**: The character's posture (slight dance, music notes) reframes "waiting" as "something fun is about to happen" — anxiety reduction through personality
- **Whitespace as anticipation**: The large empty upper zone isn't wasted — it creates visual breathing room that makes the character feel like it's performing on a stage
- **All-caps spaced label without spinner**: Communicates loading state typographically without any mechanical UI element, keeping the screen feeling warm and non-technical

## Notes
- Mascot replaces spinner = brand equity in dead time
- Musical notes as motion hint → always animate the mascot here
- Progress bar persistence across loading = reduces disorientation
- White void above character = intentional stage framing, don't fill it
- All-caps + letter-spacing for loading text = softer than a spinner, still clear
- Minimal color palette makes green character pop with zero effort
- Loading screens are brand moments — use characters, not circles

## Screen
A loading/fetching state screen from a document management or productivity app. This sits between a navigation action and a content-populated list view — a transitional state screen. Layout is minimal: top nav bar with back chevron and overflow menu, centered loading indicator with messaging, skeleton content ghosted at the bottom, and a 5-item tab bar.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Content is vertically centered in the upper-middle zone of the screen, not true mathematical center — slightly above midpoint, which feels natural for thumb-reading posture. The skeleton placeholders at the bottom hint at the incoming content structure without committing to full skeleton treatment. Large amounts of negative space surround the loading message, creating calm rather than anxiety during a wait state.

---

## Components
- **Spinner**: Thin-stroke circular indeterminate loader, light gray, minimal weight — matches the muted palette rather than using a brand accent color
- **Loading copy block**: Two-line text group, bold headline + lighter subtext, center-aligned
- **Skeleton placeholders**: Faint rectangular ghost blocks visible at bottom — very low contrast, almost subliminal
- **Top nav**: Back chevron (left) + circular outlined overflow icon (right), no title — intentionally sparse
- **Tab bar**: 5 icons, first tab active in blue, rest in gray; center tab uses a "plus in square" add action

---

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Bold ~17px headline ("Downloading your documents") / Regular ~14px subdued subtext in medium gray
- **Color palette**: Near-white/light gray background (#F0F0F4 range), dark charcoal text, mid-gray secondary text, single blue accent on active tab icon
- **Functional color use**: Blue used exclusively to signal active state; gray spinner signals "non-critical, passive" wait rather than urgent processing

---

## Patterns
- **Expectation-setting microcopy**: "This might take a minute or so…" proactively manages user patience — honest, casual tone reduces frustration without over-promising
- **Skeleton below the fold during load**: Rather than a full skeleton screen, only partial ghost content appears at the bottom edge — suggests content is coming without fabricating false structure
