- **Spinner without progress bar**: Deliberate choice to use indeterminate loader — avoids the anxiety of a stalled progress bar when duration is unknown
- **No title in nav during load**: Withholding the screen title until content is ready prevents a jarring label-before-content moment
- **Accent color restraint**: Blue appears only on the active tab — zero use of brand color in the loading state keeps the eye calm

---

## Notes
- Honest wait copy > generic "Loading…" — set real expectations
- Partial skeleton at bottom = clever content preview without full skeleton commitment
- Spinner color matches UI chrome, not brand — intentional calm signal
- No nav title during load = clean progressive reveal pattern
- Lots of breathing room during wait = reduces perceived load time
- Single accent color discipline: only active tab gets blue, nothing else

## Screen
Food delivery app home screen in a loading/skeleton state. This is the initial landing screen users see immediately after opening the app or navigating home, before content has fully loaded. Layout flows top-to-bottom: location header → search bar → category pills row → restaurant/store cards with metadata.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in clear horizontal bands with consistent left-right margins (~16px). Category filters sit in a single horizontal row of circles. Card content uses generous vertical spacing between cards (~12-16px gap). Text skeleton lines beneath each card are left-aligned and vary in width, mimicking real title/subtitle hierarchy. A circular element (likely a rating or action button) floats right-aligned next to the text lines, creating a two-column micro-layout within each card's metadata row.

## Components
- **Location selector**: Two-line treatment — small label above, bold address with dropdown chevron below. Functional hierarchy in a compact header
- **Search bar**: Full-width pill shape, light gray fill, left-anchored search icon. No border, relies on fill contrast
- **Category circles**: Four equal-diameter circular skeleton placeholders in a horizontal row — implies icon + label chips in loaded state
- **Content cards**: Large rounded rectangle skeletons (~16px radius), full-width minus margins, tall aspect ratio suggesting hero image cards
- **Metadata rows**: Two stacked skeleton lines of different widths beneath each card, paired with a right-side circular skeleton (rating badge or favorite button)
- **Bottom nav**: 5-item tab bar with outline icons, active Home tab uses filled icon + bold label

## Typography & Color
Skeleton state uses a single flat gray (#E5E5E5 approx) for all placeholder elements — no shimmer animation visible in this static frame. Live UI elements (location text, nav labels) use black for active/primary and medium gray for inactive states. The location header uses a two-weight system: small regular label ("Deliver now") over larger semi-bold address text.

## Patterns
- **Skeleton screens over spinners**: The entire content area renders structural placeholders that mirror the exact layout of loaded content — circles where category icons will be, wide rectangles where hero images will be, variable-width text lines where titles/subtitles will be. This teaches the user the layout before content arrives
- **Asymmetric text skeleton widths**: The two metadata lines beneath cards are deliberately different lengths (longer second line), mimicking natural text variance. This prevents the skeleton from feeling mechanical
- **Right-aligned circular skeleton**: Placing a circle element flush-right in the metadata row preserves space for a rating badge or action button — the skeleton communicates interaction affordance even before load
- **Two-line location header**: Separating delivery context ("Deliver now") from the actual address creates a scannable pattern where the label sets context and the address is the actionable element with a clear dropdown affordance
- **5-tab navigation with mixed icon styles**: Active state uses filled icon variant, inactive uses outline — a standard but well-executed differentiation that works without color alone

## Notes
- Skeleton line width variation = critical detail, don't use uniform widths
- Circle skeletons for category row = smart: communicates scrollable chip row format before load
- Right-float circle in card meta = reserve space for rating/CTA even in skeleton
- Two-line address header pattern reusable for any context-setting location/filter header
- Full-bleed card skeleton with generous height = implies image-forward card design
- No shimmer here — flat gray skeleton is cleaner, less distracting for fast loads

## Screen
A skeleton loading state for a shopping or content discovery app's home feed. This is the initial load screen users see before content populates. Layout is vertically scrollable with distinct horizontal sections — a filter/category chip row, a horizontal card list, a grid section, and a two-column card section below.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized into clearly delineated horizontal bands, each with a section label placeholder above it. Consistent left-edge alignment across all sections. The bottom section uses a 2-column asymmetric or equal grid with tall cards, suggesting editorial or product imagery. Generous vertical spacing between sections creates breathing room and signals distinct content categories.

## Components
- **Search bar**: Full-width pill shape, light gray fill, search icon prefix — passive/inactive state
- **AI/sparkle action button**: Square with rounded corners, sparkle icon, red notification badge dot — stands out as a secondary action with urgency signal
- **Category chips**: Short rounded rectangles in a horizontal row — filter or tab navigation
- **Horizontal list items**: Square thumbnail + stacked text lines — classic media/product list skeleton
- **Grid cards**: Equal-width tall rectangles with heavy rounded corners — content or product tiles
- **Two-column cards**: Large tall rectangles, slightly different widths — editorial or featured content
- **Bottom nav**: 5 icons, home filled/active, others outline — standard tab bar

## Typography & Color
No live type visible — all skeleton. Color palette is minimal: pure white background, single gray tone (#E8E8E8 approx) for all skeleton elements. The only color accent is the red notification dot on the AI button, which draws immediate attention. The monochromatic skeleton maintains visual hierarchy purely through shape and size variation.

## Patterns
- **Notification badge on AI feature button** — using urgency (red dot) to drive engagement with a feature, not just a message count
- **Mixed layout sections in one feed** — horizontal scroll row, grid, and large 2-col cards coexist, teaching the user that content types vary before anything loads
- **Skeleton mirrors exact final layout** — section labels, card proportions, and grid columns are all represented, reducing layout shift perception on load
- **Sparkle icon for AI** — communicates "smart/magic" functionality without text, now a recognizable convention worth noting
- **Section header placeholders** — short label-width bars above each section group teach content taxonomy even in loading state

## Notes
- Red dot on feature button = clever engagement hook, not just notification
- Skeleton as layout teacher — user learns structure before content arrives
- Mix 3 layout patterns in one feed: horizontal list, grid, tall 2-col
- Rounded corners on skeleton tiles feel softer, less clinical than rect skeletons
- AI button isolated top-right = persistent utility, always accessible from home
- Section label skeletons vary in width — adds realism, avoids mechanical repetition

## Screen
Technical analysis panel for a stock trading/financial app. This is a modal or slide-up sheet overlay showing oscillator indicators for a specific stock. Sits within a chart detail flow — accessed from a chart view. Three-column data table within a sheet layout, with a persistent bottom toolbar and nav bar visible beneath.

## Layout & Spacing
Sheet card with rounded top corners floats over the underlying chart screen. Header zone holds company identity (logo + name + category label). Section header ("Oscillators") acts as a collapsible/navigable group label. Three-column table: left-heavy name column (~60% width), right-aligned numeric value column, right-aligned action column. Rows use generous line height with thin full-width dividers. Consistent left/right padding (~16–20px) throughout.

## Components
- **Sheet/modal card** — white surface, rounded top corners, drop shadow implied by layering over chart
- **Company header** — circular logo thumbnail (dark fill, white icon), stacked label + title typography, X dismiss button top-right
- **Section header with chevron** — bold label + `>` indicating tappable/expandable category navigation
- **Column headers** — small, muted gray, all-caps weight, right-aligned for Value and Action
- **Data rows** — name in regular weight dark text, value in medium-weight neutral, action in colored semantic text
- **Semantic action labels** — "Buy" rendered in blue, "Neutral" in muted gray — no pills or badges, pure text color coding
- **Bottom persistent bar** — ticker + timeframe + action icons (add, draw, more) sitting above main nav

## Typography & Color
Clear three-level hierarchy: company name (large, bold, black) → section label (medium bold) → column headers (small, gray, subdued) → data rows (regular, dark). Action words use blue as the sole accent color to signal signal strength. Neutral states use gray to visually recede. No red visible — likely reserved for Sell signals. Background is pure white; text contrast is high throughout.

## Patterns
- **Color-only signal encoding** — Buy/Neutral/Sell differentiated purely through text color with no additional iconography or badges; keeps the table scannable without visual noise
- **Chevron on section header** — implies the "Oscillators" view is one of multiple swappable technical categories (Moving Averages, etc.), making the label a nav control, not just a title
- **Contextual persistence** — underlying chart ticker bar (AAPL, 1D, drawing tools) remains visible below the sheet, maintaining spatial context without full dismissal
- **Parameter transparency in labels** — indicator names include their parameters inline ("RSI (14)", "Stochastic %K (14, 3, 3)") — serves advanced users without a separate settings layer
- **No visual weight on neutral** — the majority state (Neutral) is intentionally de-emphasized in gray, making the minority Buy/Sell states pop immediately during a scan
- **Sheet-over-chart pattern** — data panel doesn't navigate away from the chart; preserves orientation for a user mid-analysis

## Notes
- Text-color-only for signal states = elegant data density, no badge clutter
- Inline params in row labels = self-documenting table, no tooltips needed
- Chevron section header doubles as category switcher — one component, two jobs
- Bottom context bar pattern: keep current asset/timeframe visible even inside sub-panels
- Gray column headers nearly disappear — intentional, data is the focus
- Consider: what does "Sell" look like? Probably red — full traffic light system in text only
- Sheet doesn't full-screen — partial overlay keeps chart memory alive

## Screen
Cryptocurrency exchange markets list screen. This is a spot trading market overview — a browse/discovery screen sitting between app home and individual asset trading views. Three-column list layout with horizontal filter navigation stacked above. Users land here to scan market conditions before entering a trade.

---

## Layout & Spacing
Three distinct navigation tiers stack vertically: top-level tab row → asset category tabs → filter chips + currency selector. Content area is a dense data table with consistent row height. Columns are left-aligned (asset name), right-aligned (price), and right-aligned (change pill). Tight but readable row spacing — roughly equal padding top/bottom per row. A subtle divider separates the column headers from data rows. The currency selector (USDT ▼) is pushed far right of the filter chips, separated by a vertical rule — smart use of a divider to group unlike controls.

---

## Components

- **Top nav tabs** — plain text, weight differentiates active (bold) vs inactive (light gray). No pill/underline except second tier.
- **Second-tier tabs** — underline indicator on active item only, minimal style.
- **Filter chips row** — flat text chips, no border/background. Active state is bold black. Vertical rule separator before currency dropdown.
- **Currency dropdown** — inline text + chevron, no button chrome. Lightweight.
- **Column headers** — small gray text with bidirectional sort arrows (↕) on each column. Clean affordance.
- **Asset rows** — two-line left cell: ticker bold + pair in gray + leverage badge; volume in small gray below. Right price cell: primary price large, USD equivalent smaller gray below.
- **Leverage badge** — small amber/orange text label (e.g. "10x", "5x"), no background. Color alone signals the badge type.
- **Change pill** — rounded rectangle, solid red or green fill, white bold percentage text. High contrast, immediately scannable.

---

## Typography & Color

Hierarchy: Bold ticker symbol → regular pair text → small gray volume/USD equivalent. Price uses a slightly larger weight than surrounding text to anchor the eye. Color palette is minimal: near-black for primary text, medium gray for secondary, amber for leverage multipliers, semantic red (#D9534F-ish) and green for change indicators. Color is used almost exclusively functionally — no decorative color anywhere. White background keeps data legible.

---

## Patterns

- **Three-tier progressive filtering** — top tabs (section) → sub-tabs (asset type) → chips (category) creates a drill-down without navigating away. All visible simultaneously, reducing cognitive load.
- **Leverage badge as ambient data** — showing "10x" or "5x" in amber inline with the pair name communicates risk/product type without a separate column or icon. Unobtrusive but informative.
- **Dual price display** — native token price + USD equivalent stacked in the same cell. Solves the problem of non-USD pairs without adding a column.
- **Change pill as the only high-contrast element** — everything else is muted; the colored pill becomes the natural focal point for scanning, which matches user intent (spotting movers).
- **Sort arrows on all columns simultaneously** — rather than showing active sort state, all columns show the neutral ↕ icon, keeping the header visually consistent and low-noise.
- **FAB trade button** — center-docked, elevated circular button breaks the tab bar pattern to make the primary action (trade) always reachable without interrupting the browsing list.

---

## Notes
- Leverage badge = color-only label, no pill bg — saves horizontal space, still readable
- Dual price stack in one cell > adding USD column
- Change pill = only saturated color on screen → instant eye magnet
- Vertical rule as separator between filter chips and dropdown — underused pattern
- Three nav tiers stacked without feeling heavy because each tier uses different visual weight
- FAB in tab bar center = trade action elevated above browse actions
- All sort arrows neutral state = cleaner than highlighting active sort

## Screen
Sports app live game screen showing league standings. Sits within a game detail view, accessible mid-game. Three-tab structure (Summary / Play-By-Play / Box Score) with the standings table as the primary content under Summary. Layout is header → tab bar → scrollable data table → sticky footer navigation.

## Layout & Spacing
- Scoreboard header is compact and centered, using a three-column layout: home score / game clock / away score
- Tab bar sits directly below with equal-width segments
- Table uses consistent row height (~52pt) with generous vertical rhythm — numbers breathe well
- Rank column, logo, team name, then right-aligned stat columns (W / L / PCT / GB) — classic tabular left-to-right priority
- A dashed divider line separates playoff position from play-in/lottery zone — functional use of a subtle separator

## Components
- **Scoreboard header**: Dark background, team logos flanking large bold scores, center shows quarter + clock in smaller muted text
- **Segmented tab bar**: Active tab ("Summary") in white bold, inactive in medium-gray — no pill or underline indicator, purely weight/color differentiation
- **Data table rows**: Alternating implicit grouping via the dashed line; team logos are small circular icons (~24pt); rank numbers left-aligned in muted gray
- **Dashed divider**: Used specifically to mark the playoff cutoff line — not a full rule, intentionally subtle
- **Sticky footer**: "Western Conference" as a tappable underlined label — acts as a section switcher

## Typography & Color
- **Type hierarchy**: Large bold scores (top) → medium bold team names → regular tabular figures → small muted column headers
- **Color palette**: Near-black background (#1a1a2e range), white for active/primary data, medium gray for secondary labels and inactive tabs, muted warm gray for column headers
- **Color as function**: White = active/important data; gray = metadata/inactive; no color used for rows except the dashed separator which carries a subtle red tint (possibly team-color bleed from the gradient background)
- Background has a subtle dark gradient with faint team color washes bleeding in from top corners

## Patterns
- **Playoff cutoff line as data** — the dashed divider communicates standings context (playoff vs. non-playoff) without any label, relying on sports-literate users knowing the convention
- **Footer as conference switcher** — underlined text at the bottom acts as a persistent toggle between Eastern/Western conferences, avoiding a full tab or modal
- **Score header persists across tabs** — live game context stays visible regardless of which tab is active, grounding the user in the live moment
- **Tab differentiation by weight only** — no background pill or underline on active tab, just bold white vs. gray; clean but risks lower affordance clarity
- **Logo as row identifier** — small team logos do more visual work than the text name for recognition, especially for sports-fluent users; text is secondary confirmation
- **Tabular number alignment** — all stat columns are right-aligned or center-aligned consistently, making vertical scanning fast

## Notes
- Dashed divider = smart way to encode "playoff line" without a label — borrow for any ranked list with a threshold
- Footer-as-section-switcher is elegant — avoids nested tabs or modal sheets
- Bold weight only for active tab state is risky but very clean — works in dark mode
- Team color gradient bleed in header background is subtle brand nod without being loud
- Consider: how does this degrade if a team has a very light logo on dark bg?
- Rank number in muted gray is smart — it's context, not the primary data point
- PCT column is the real "ranking signal" — GB is supplementary; column order reflects this

## Screen
A merchant/seller info bottom sheet within a shopping app. This is a secondary overlay screen — likely accessed via a "seller info" or overflow menu tap on a product or store page. The underlying screen is dimmed/blurred. Layout is a modal card with a header identity block, truncated description, and a vertical action list.

## Layout & Spacing
Content is organized in two clear zones: an identity header (app icon + name + rating) and a scrollable action list. Generous vertical rhythm between list items (~40–44pt tap targets). The description block sits between header and actions as a transitional content layer. Consistent left-alignment with icon+label pairs. The close button is top-right, leaving the left side clean for content scanning.

## Components
- **Modal card** — white, large rounded corners (~20pt radius), floats over dimmed background with visible app content peeking above
- **App icon** — small rounded square, matches standard icon treatment, paired with name + star rating inline
- **Star rating** — filled gold star with numeric score and review count in muted smaller text, all inline
- **Truncated description** — 3-line clamp with a blue "More" inline text link to expand
- **Icon+label list rows** — outline-style icons left of labels, no dividers, relies on whitespace alone for separation
- **Destructive row (Report)** — red icon + red label, visually isolated at bottom, no separator but color alone signals danger

## Typography & Color
- App name: medium weight, ~16pt
- Rating: small, ~12pt, muted gray
- Description: regular weight, ~14pt, dark gray
- List labels: regular/medium, ~16pt, near-black
- "More" link: accent blue, same size as body copy
- "Report": red, same size as other list items — color does all the destructive signaling
- Palette: white card, near-black text, blue accent, red for destructive, gray for secondary info

## Patterns
- **Peek-through modal** — underlying screen content is visible above the sheet, reinforcing context without full dismissal; the large brand wordmark visible behind creates continuity
- **Color-only destructive differentiation** — "Report" uses no separator, no section break, just red to isolate it from neutral actions; trusts color literacy entirely
- **Divider-free list** — spacing alone separates rows, keeping the UI visually quiet; works because icon+label pairs are visually self-contained
- **Inline "More" truncation** — description is clipped mid-sentence with an inline blue link rather than a separate "read more" button, saving vertical space while staying accessible
- **Icon semantic variety** — each row uses a contextually distinct outline icon (chat bubble, external link arrow, upload/share, receipt, box, shield, info circle) adding scanability without color coding

## Notes
- Red-only destructive row = bold choice, no extra visual separation needed if placement is last
- Peek-through modal adds depth without full-screen takeover — great for "about this seller" type flows
- No dividers + generous padding = cleaner than most settings-style lists
- Inline truncation with colored "More" saves a full row of UI
- Rating + review count inline with name = compact trust signal in header
- Outline icons at consistent size create rhythm without overwhelming text

## Screen
Confirmation/cancellation dialog screen within a car rental app. Sits in the post-booking flow when a user initiates a cancellation action. The underlying screen shows a booking confirmation with vehicle image and reservation details; a bottom sheet modal overlays it to handle the destructive action.

## Layout & Spacing
Two-layer composition: the purple confirmation screen sits behind, partially visible, providing context for what's being cancelled. The white bottom sheet rises from the bottom with generous internal padding. Action buttons are separated by hairline dividers rather than spacing, creating a list-style action sheet feel. The icon sits centered with comfortable breathing room above the title.

## Components
- **Bottom sheet modal** — white card with rounded top corners, no shadow needed since it overlays a colored background. Partially reveals content beneath, reinforcing the layered context.
- **Compound icon** — key icon in a circle with a red badge overlay (×). Badge communicates "cancellation" without text. Clean use of iconographic metaphor.
- **Action list buttons** — three stacked text buttons separated by hairline dividers. No button chrome (no borders, no fills). Pure text actions in a list format.
- **Underlying confirmation card** — purple background with centered vehicle PNG (no shadow/container), calendar icon + structured text block for reservation metadata.
- **X dismiss button** — top-left, minimal, white on purple.

## Typography & Color
- **Purple background**: bold brand color used for the confirmed state — signals positivity/success
- **White sheet**: neutral container for the decision moment
- **Red/orange**: used exclusively for the destructive action label ("Cancel reservation") — strong functional signal
- **Black bold**: used for neutral/safe actions ("View reservation," "Keep reservation")
- Type hierarchy: confirmation title in bold white → reservation metadata in lighter weight → sheet title bold dark → body copy regular gray → action labels vary by weight and color based on consequence

## Patterns
- **Destructive action sandwiching**: The cancel action (red) is placed between two safer options — "View reservation" above and "Keep reservation" below. This is a deliberate friction pattern; the eye naturally exits toward the bottom action, nudging toward retention.
- **Contextual underlayer**: Keeping the booking confirmation visible behind the sheet reminds users what they're about to lose — a subtle psychological anchor against cancellation.
- **Edit nudge in body copy**: The confirmation message proactively suggests editing as an alternative to cancelling ("You can also edit and update your reservation for free") — reducing churn without a separate screen.
- **Badge-as-verb on icon**: The red × badge on the key icon efficiently communicates the action type without relying solely on text — useful for quick scanning.
- **No button chrome**: Action items styled as plain text with dividers rather than filled buttons — reduces visual weight and keeps the sheet feeling lightweight and non-alarming.

## Notes
- Sandwich destructive action between safe options → reduces accidental taps + nudges retention
- Show what's at stake in the background layer — context as persuasion
- Inline copy as micro-retention tool ("edit for free") — cheaper than a modal upsell
- Badge overlay on icon = efficient compound meaning
- Divider-separated text actions > button grid for 3-option sheets
- Purple = confirmed/positive state; red = only for irreversible/destructive
- Bottom sheet doesn't need shadow when background is saturated color

## Screen
Notification permission prompt modal in a peer-to-peer payments app. This is an in-app pre-permission screen that appears before triggering the native OS permission dialog — a common onboarding or first-session interstitial. Sits over the home screen with a dimmed background. Card-style modal centered on screen with illustration, headline, body copy, and two CTAs.

## Layout & Spacing
Single-column modal card with generous internal padding. Content flows top-to-bottom: illustration → headline → body → primary CTA → secondary CTA. Illustration takes roughly 35% of the card height, creating strong visual anchoring before the text. Comfortable breathing room between each section. The two CTAs have clear vertical separation — no ambiguity about hierarchy.

## Components
- **Modal card**: White, rounded corners (large radius ~20px), floats over a dimmed/blurred background. No hard shadow visible — elevation implied by contrast alone.
- **Close button (X)**: Small circular gray button top-right corner of the card. Subtle, doesn't compete with primary action.
- **Illustration**: Circular light-blue background shape containing a hand tapping a phone with sketch-style lightning bolt marks. Feels hand-drawn/editorial, adds warmth.
- **Primary CTA button**: Full-width pill shape (fully rounded), solid blue fill, white bold text. High contrast, dominant.
- **Secondary CTA**: Text-only link in blue, no button container. Clear visual de-emphasis without hiding the option.

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: ~20px, bold/black weight, dark near-black, centered, 2-line wrap
- **Body**: ~14–15px, regular weight, medium gray, centered, relaxed line height
- **CTA primary**: ~16px, bold, white on blue
- **CTA secondary**: ~15px, medium weight, blue — matches primary button color to signal it's still interactive, just lower priority

Color palette: White modal, light blue illustration circle, brand blue (#3D95CE-ish) for interactive elements, dark charcoal for headline, mid-gray for body text. Background app UI is desaturated/dimmed to ~40% opacity.

## Patterns
- **Pre-permission priming**: Custom in-app modal fires before native OS prompt, letting the app frame the value proposition on its own terms. If user taps "Allow," the real OS dialog follows. Reduces cold-ask rejection rates.
- **Asymmetric CTA pairing**: Filled button vs. bare text link creates strong hierarchy without hiding the decline path — respects user autonomy while still nudging toward the preferred action.
- **Escape hatch transparency**: "You can change this at any time in Settings" — proactively defuses anxiety about commitment. Placed as a second body paragraph, not buried in fine print.
- **Illustration as emotional softener**: The hand-drawn style illustration humanizes what is essentially a data/permission request. The circular crop acts as a contained "stage" for the illustration, keeping it tidy.
- **Dimmed-but-visible background**: The home screen remains partially visible behind the modal, grounding the user in context — they know exactly where they are and what they're interrupting.

## Notes
- Pre-permission modal = best practice before any OS permission ask; always frame the "why" first
- Pill CTA + text-only decline = clean hierarchy without dark pattern pressure
- Circular illustration container is a reusable pattern for any feature education modal
- "Change anytime in Settings" copy = anxiety reducer, worth including in any permission ask
- Sketch/hand-drawn illustration style softens transactional/financial UI significantly
- Close X in corner = always give escape; don't trap users in modals

## Screen
Permission request modal overlay on a food delivery app's home screen. This is a mid-session permission prompt triggered when a user taps the microphone/voice search icon in the search bar. The underlying home screen is dimmed but visible, providing context for why the permission is being requested.

## Layout & Spacing
Modal card is center-anchored with generous internal padding (~24px). Content follows a clear vertical stack: headline → supporting copy → dual CTA buttons. The two action buttons sit side-by-side in a 40/60 visual weight split. Modal has rounded corners (~16px) creating a floating card feel. Background dim is moderate — enough to focus attention without fully obscuring context.

## Components
- **Modal card**: White, heavily rounded, drop-shadow elevation. Clean white background separates it sharply from the dimmed orange-tinted background.
- **Close (X) button**: Small circular ghost button, top-right corner of modal. Subtle gray outline, provides escape without competing with primary CTAs.
- **Dismiss button ("Not now")**: Pill-shaped, light peach/salmon fill, orange text. No hard border — tinted background matches brand color at low opacity.
- **Confirm button ("Continue")**: Pill-shaped, solid brand orange, white text. Full visual weight primary action.
- **Headline text**: Bold, left-aligned, 3-line question format.
- **Body text**: Regular weight, muted gray, left-aligned below headline.

## Typography & Color
Two-level hierarchy: heavy bold headline (~18px) vs. lighter body copy (~14px, gray). Brand orange (#E8521A approx.) used consistently across CTAs and background accents. The dismiss button uses a tinted version of the same orange — maintaining palette cohesion while clearly communicating secondary priority. No third color introduced.

## Patterns
- **Contextual permission priming**: Modal fires immediately after user interaction with the microphone icon — not on app launch. This just-in-time approach dramatically increases permission grant rates by tying the ask to clear user intent.
- **Tinted secondary CTA**: Instead of a plain text link or outlined button for "Not now," the dismiss option uses a low-opacity brand-color fill. This keeps visual harmony while still de-emphasizing the action — subtler than a ghost button, less dismissive than plain text.
- **In-app pre-permission dialog**: This is a custom app-level dialog that precedes the OS system permission prompt. Allows the app to explain value before the irreversible native dialog appears — if user dismisses here, the OS prompt is preserved for a better moment.
- **Question-framed headline**: Phrasing the permission ask as a question ("Allow...?") rather than a statement softens the demand and frames it as a user choice.
- **Dual escape paths**: Both the X button and "Not now" dismiss — redundant dismissal reduces anxiety about being trapped.

## Notes
- Tinted-fill secondary button = elegant alternative to ghost/text-only dismiss
- Pre-permission modal pattern: always gate OS prompts behind in-app explanation
- Trigger permission ask on explicit user action, never on cold open
- Question headline framing reduces friction vs. imperative statements
- Keep background partially visible — context reinforces why permission matters
- Same hue, different saturation/opacity for primary vs. secondary CTA = strong cohesion trick

## Screen
A feature introduction modal overlaying a community/topics feed screen in a language learning app. This appears mid-flow — likely triggered when a user taps a locked feature or navigates to a section requiring premium/onboarding. The modal is a bottom-anchored card with a dimmed background showing the underlying content.

## Layout & Spacing
The modal uses a centered card with generous internal padding (~24px). Content flows vertically: headline → supporting copy → preview card → CTA. The nested preview card (scenario example) sits inset within the modal, creating a card-within-card hierarchy. Background content is intentionally legible enough to provide context but dimmed to focus attention. The modal doesn't cover the full screen — it floats with visible corners, reinforcing it as a temporary interruption.

## Components
- **Modal card**: White, large corner radius (~20px), elevated with soft shadow, not full-bleed
- **Nested preview card**: Slightly off-white/light gray background, smaller radius, mimics a real content item from the feed — functions as a live example
- **"NEW" badge**: Small blue label with heart icon, top-right of preview card, adds social proof/freshness signal
- **Primary CTA button**: Full-width, bold blue fill, large corner radius (pill-adjacent), white label — dominant visual anchor
- **Floating decorative hearts**: Small scattered blue/purple heart shapes above the preview card, adds playfulness without cluttering
- **Username + wand icon**: Micro-detail inside preview card reinforcing user-generated content feel

## Typography & Color
- **Headline**: Bold, ~20px, dark near-black — high contrast, immediate read
- **Body copy**: Regular weight, ~14px, medium gray, centered alignment — secondary hierarchy
- **Preview card text**: Left-aligned, mixed weights (bold title, light description), smaller scale — tertiary
- **Color palette**: Deep blue (#3D4EFF-ish) as primary action color, white modal, medium gray body text, soft purple accent hearts
- Blue used functionally: CTA button, badge label, decorative accents — creates color consistency across interactive and decorative elements

## Patterns
- **Card-within-card preview**: Rather than describing the feature abstractly, a real content item is embedded inside the modal — shows don't tell, reduces cognitive load about what the feature actually is
- **Blurred/dimmed but readable background**: The underlying feed remains partially visible, reinforcing that this is an overlay on real content the user was already exploring — creates continuity rather than a hard context switch
- **Scattered micro-illustrations**: The floating hearts are decorative but reinforce the emotional tone (confidence, fun, low-stakes practice) — not random, they're semantically linked to the copy
- **No dismiss/close button visible**: Removes the easy escape, nudging toward the CTA — common in feature introduction flows where the goal is conversion
- **User-generated content framing in preview**: Showing a username and scenario title inside the preview card implies community participation, making the feature feel alive and social rather than templated

## Notes
- Card-in-card = show the actual UX inside the pitch
- Scatter decorative elements that echo the emotional message, not just fill space
- Keeping background visible = "you're close, just one tap away" psychology
- No X button = intentional friction toward action
- "NEW" badge inside preview card adds urgency without screaming sale
- Centered body copy works here because it's short — would break at longer lengths
- Full-width pill CTA at bottom of modal = thumb-friendly, natural resting zone
